words.m: verb perfect passive participle genitive plural neuter

verses.flow: Forasmuch as many have undertaken to compile a narrative concerning the matters that have been fully accomplished among us,
verses.note: GREEK: "Epeidēper" (ἐπειδήπερ) is a hapax legomenon in the NT, an intensified form of "epeidē" meaning "inasmuch as indeed." It signals Luke's elevated literary register. "Peplērophorēmenōn" (πεπληροφορημένων, from plērophoreō, Strong's G4135) means "fully accomplished" or "fully established," indicating certainty and divine completion of the events narrated.

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: just as those who from the beginning were eyewitnesses and servants of the word delivered them to us,
verses.note: GREEK: "Autoptai" (αὐτόπται, Strong's G845) is a hapax legomenon in the NT, literally "self-seers" or "eyewitnesses." It is the origin of the English "autopsy." "Hypēretai tou logou" — "servants of the Word" — indicates a recognized class of authoritative tradents in the early community. THEOLOGICAL: Luke distinguishes himself from the eyewitnesses, establishing a second-generation perspective while affirming the apostolic chain of testimony.

words.m: verb perfect active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: it seemed good also to me, having followed all things closely from above, to write to you in orderly sequence, most excellent Theophilus,
verses.note: GREEK: "Anōthen" (ἄνωθεν, Strong's G509) can mean "from above" (divine perspective, cf. John 3:3) or "from the beginning." Luke likely intends both senses — tracing events from their origin with divinely guided accuracy. "Kratiste" (κράτιστε) is a title of social rank ("most excellent"), used also for Roman governors Felix (Acts 23:26) and Festus (Acts 26:25). HISTORICAL: This formal dedication follows Greco-Roman literary convention for historical works, placing Luke's Gospel in the genre of orderly historical narrative.

verses.flow: that you might know the certainty concerning the words in which you were instructed.
There came to be in the days of Herod, king of Judea, a certain priest by the name of Zechariah, of the division of Abijah, and his wife was from the daughters of Aaron, and her name was Elizabeth.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Herod the Great reigned 37–4 BC. The priestly division of Abijah was the eighth of twenty-four courses established by David (1 Chr 24:10). That both Zechariah and Elizabeth descend from priestly lines underscores the thoroughly Levitical heritage of John's family. THEOLOGICAL: Luke's narrative begins in the temple with the old covenant priesthood, signaling that the new era of salvation emerges organically from within Israel's covenantal structures.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And they were both righteous before God, walking in all the commandments and ordinances of the Lord, blameless.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And they had no child, because Elizabeth was barren, and both were advanced in their days.
And it came to pass, while he was serving as priest in the order of his division before God,

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: according to the custom of the priesthood, his lot fell to burn incense, having entered into the temple of the Lord.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: And the whole multitude of the people was praying outside at the hour of the incense offering.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And there appeared to him an angel of the Lord, standing at the right side of the altar of incense.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Angelos kyriou" (ἄγγελος κυρίου) — "angel of the Lord" — denotes a member of the heavenly council dispatched on divine commission. The right side of the altar is the position of honor and authority. This is the first angelic visitation recorded in Israel since the prophetic era, breaking roughly four centuries of prophetic silence. TYPOLOGY: The appearance at the incense altar echoes the heavenly temple liturgy (Rev 8:3-4), connecting earthly worship with the divine council's activity.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And Zechariah was troubled upon seeing him, and fear fell upon him.
But the angel said to him, "Do not fear, Zechariah, for your prayer has been heard, and your wife Elizabeth shall bear a son to you, and you shall call his name John.
And he shall be joy and gladness to you, and many shall rejoice at his birth.
For he shall be great before the Lord, and wine and strong drink he shall by no means drink, and he shall be filled with the Holy Spirit even from his mother's womb.
verses.note: GREEK: "Sikera" (σίκερα, Strong's G4608) is a Semitic loanword for fermented drink other than grape wine. TYPOLOGY: The prohibition of wine and strong drink echoes the Nazirite vow (Num 6:3) and specifically recalls the angelic announcement concerning Samson (Judg 13:4-7). John's filling with the Holy Spirit from the womb sets him apart as the eschatological prophet. THEOLOGICAL: The prenatal filling with the Spirit is unprecedented and signals the inbreaking of the messianic age.

verses.flow: And many of the sons of Israel shall he turn back to the Lord their God.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: And he shall go before him in the spirit and power of Elijah, to turn the hearts of fathers to children, and the disobedient to the wisdom of the righteous, to make ready for the Lord a people prepared."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Direct allusion to Malachi 4:5-6 (LXX 3:22-23), identifying John with the eschatological Elijah who precedes the Day of the Lord. "To make ready for the Lord a people prepared" echoes Isa 40:3. THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "before him" (enōpion autou) is ambiguous — it could refer to going before the Lord God (v.16) or before the coming Messiah, creating a deliberate theological overlap between YHWH's visitation and the Messiah's coming.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: And Zechariah said to the angel, "By what shall I know this? For I am an old man, and my wife is advanced in her days."

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine
verb perfect active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And the angel answered and said to him, "I am Gabriel, the one standing in the presence of God, and I was sent to speak to you and to bring you good tidings of these things.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: Gabriel (Γαβριήλ, "mighty one of God") is one of the named angels who stands in the presence of God — a member of the inner divine council (cf. Dan 8:16; 9:21). "Ho parestēkōs enōpion tou theou" — "the one standing before God" — denotes his permanent station in the heavenly throne room. In Second Temple Jewish literature (1 Enoch 9:1; 40:9), Gabriel is one of four archangels with specific cosmic responsibilities.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And behold, you shall be silent and unable to speak until the day these things come to pass, because you did not believe my words, which shall be fulfilled in their appointed time."

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And the people were waiting for Zechariah, and they were marveling at his delay in the temple.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having come out, he was unable to speak to them, and they perceived that he had seen a vision in the temple. And he was making signs to them, and remained mute.
And it came to pass, when the days of his priestly service were fulfilled, he departed to his house.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: And after these days, Elizabeth his wife conceived, and she hid herself five months, saying,
"Thus the Lord has done for me in the days in which he looked upon me, to take away my reproach among men."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Elizabeth's language echoes the experience of barren matriarchs in Israel's history: Sarah (Gen 21:6-7), Rachel (Gen 30:23), and Hannah (1 Sam 1:19-20). The removal of "reproach" (oneidos, ὄνειδος) among humans parallels Rachel's exact words in Gen 30:23 LXX. Each barren-woman narrative in Scripture signals a pivotal moment in redemptive history.

verses.flow: And in the sixth month, the angel Gabriel was sent from God to a city of Galilee, the name of which was Nazareth,

words.m: verb perfect passive participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: to a virgin betrothed to a man whose name was Joseph, of the house of David; and the name of the virgin was Mary.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb perfect passive participle singular feminine

verses.flow: And having come in to her, he said, "Rejoice, favored one! The Lord is with you."
But she was greatly troubled at the word, and was pondering what manner of greeting this might be.
And the angel said to her, "Do not fear, Mary, for you have found favor with God.
And behold, you shall conceive in your womb and shall bear a Son, and you shall call his name Jesus.
verses.note: GREEK: "Iēsoun" (Ἰησοῦν) — the Greek form of Hebrew Yehoshua/Yeshua, meaning "YHWH saves." TYPOLOGY: The annunciation pattern — angel appears, fear arises, divine message given, child named, destiny foretold — mirrors the pattern of Isaac (Gen 17-18), Samson (Judg 13), and Samuel (1 Sam 1). The naming of the child before conception signifies divine predetermination of his mission.

verses.flow: He shall be great, and shall be called Son of the Most High; and the Lord God shall give to him the throne of David his father.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Son of the Most High" (huios hypsistou) is a divine title with both Davidic royal (2 Sam 7:14; Ps 2:7) and divine-nature implications. "The Lord God shall give to him the throne of David his father" fulfills the Davidic covenant (2 Sam 7:12-16). TYPOLOGY: This verse weaves together the Nathan oracle, the royal psalms, and Daniel 7:14, presenting Jesus as the ultimate Davidic King whose enthronement is enacted by God himself.

verses.flow: And he shall reign over the house of Jacob unto the ages, and of his kingdom there shall be no end."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "He shall reign over the house of Jacob unto the ages, and of his kingdom there shall be no end" directly fulfills Dan 7:14 and 2 Sam 7:13,16. The eternal, unending nature of this kingdom distinguishes it from all earthly monarchies and confirms Jesus as the promised Messiah whose dominion is absolute and everlasting.

verses.flow: And Mary said to the angel, "How shall this be, since I do not know a man?"

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine
verb present passive participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: And the angel answered and said to her, "The Holy Spirit shall come upon you, and the power of the Most High shall overshadow you; therefore also the holy one being born shall be called Son of God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The Holy Spirit shall come upon you, and the power of the Most High shall overshadow you" — the verb "episkiazō" (ἐπισκιάζω, Strong's G1982) echoes the Shekinah glory overshadowing the tabernacle (Exod 40:35 LXX). The virginal conception is attributed to the direct creative action of the Spirit. "The holy one being born shall be called Son of God" — "dio kai" (therefore also) establishes a causal link between the Spirit's overshadowing and the designation "Son of God," though this title carries both messianic-royal and ontological weight. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The language of divine creative power descending parallels Gen 1:2 where the Spirit hovers over the waters, marking this as a new-creation event.

words.m: verb present passive participle dative singular feminine

verses.flow: And behold, Elizabeth your kinswoman has also herself conceived a son in her old age, and this is the sixth month for her who was called barren.
For no word from God shall be impossible."
verses.note: GREEK: "Ouk adynatēsei para tou theou pan rhēma" — literally "no word/thing from God shall be without power." "Rhēma" (ῥῆμα) means both "word" and "thing/matter," preserving the Hebrew dabar concept where God's spoken word is itself the effective power that accomplishes the thing spoken. TYPOLOGY: Direct echo of Gen 18:14 LXX, spoken concerning Sarah's miraculous conception of Isaac, linking Mary's situation to the foundational covenant promise.

verses.flow: And Mary said, "Behold, the handmaid of the Lord; let it be to me according to your word." And the angel departed from her.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Mary's self-designation as "doulē kyriou" (δούλη κυρίου, "handmaid of the Lord") expresses total submission to the divine will. Her "genoito" (γένοιτο, "let it be") is an optative of willing consent, contrasting with Zechariah's doubt. Mary's faith-response becomes the human instrument through which the Incarnation proceeds.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: And Mary arose in those days and went with haste into the hill country, to a city of Judah,
and entered into the house of Zechariah and greeted Elizabeth.
And it came to pass, when Elizabeth heard the greeting of Mary, the infant leaped in her womb; and Elizabeth was filled with the Holy Spirit.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular feminine
verb perfect passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And she cried out with a loud voice and said, "Blessed are you among women, and blessed is the fruit of your womb!
And whence is this to me, that the mother of my Lord should come to me?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Elizabeth's exclamation "the mother of my Lord" (hē mētēr tou kyriou mou) is theologically momentous. "Kyrios mou" echoes Ps 110:1 LXX — "The Lord said to my Lord" — a key messianic text. Elizabeth, filled with the Holy Spirit, recognizes the unborn Jesus as her Lord, a confession of his messianic and divine identity even before his birth.

verses.flow: For behold, when the sound of your greeting came to my ears, the infant leaped in my womb with gladness.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular feminine
verb perfect passive participle dative plural neuter

verses.flow: And blessed is she who believed that there shall be a fulfillment of the things spoken to her from the Lord."
And Mary said, "My soul magnifies the Lord,
verses.note: STRUCTURE: The Magnificat (vv.46-55) is a hymnic unit closely modeled on Hannah's Song (1 Sam 2:1-10). It follows a chiastic structure: personal praise (46-49), God's universal character (50-53), covenantal faithfulness to Israel (54-55). TYPOLOGY: Mary recapitulates and surpasses Hannah's role — as Hannah's barrenness gave way to Samuel who anointed kings, Mary's virginal conception brings forth the King himself.

verses.flow: and my spirit has rejoiced in God my Savior.
For he has looked upon the humble state of his handmaid. For behold, from now on all generations shall call me blessed.
For the Mighty One has done great things for me, and holy is his name.

words.m: verb present middle participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: And his mercy is unto generations and generations for those who fear him.
He has shown strength with his arm; he has scattered the proud in the thought of their hearts.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The aorist tenses in vv.51-55 are prophetic aorists — speaking of future divine actions as already accomplished, reflecting the Hebrew prophetic perspective. "He has scattered the proud in the thought of their hearts" — "dianoiā kardias autōn" indicates that pride originates in the inner reasoning of the heart, and God's judgment addresses the root disposition. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The scattering of the proud echoes the divine council's judgment against the builders of Babel (Gen 11:8) and the overthrow of rebellious spiritual powers.

verses.flow: He has brought down rulers from thrones, and has exalted the humble.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine
verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: The hungry he has filled with good things, and the rich he has sent away empty.
He has helped Israel his servant, in remembrance of mercy,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "He has helped Israel his servant" — "paidos autou" (παιδὸς αὐτοῦ) echoes the Servant of the Lord language in Isaiah (Isa 41:8-9; 42:1; 44:1). Israel as God's "pais" (servant/child) connects to the Servant Songs, which find their ultimate fulfillment in the Messiah whom Mary now carries.

verses.flow: just as he spoke to our fathers, to Abraham and to his seed, unto the age."
And Mary remained with her about three months, and returned to her house.
Now the time was fulfilled for Elizabeth to give birth, and she bore a son.
And her neighbors and her kinsmen heard that the Lord had magnified his mercy with her, and they were rejoicing together with her.
And it came to pass on the eighth day, they came to circumcise the child, and they were calling him by the name of his father, Zechariah.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: And his mother answered and said, "No, but he shall be called John."
And they said to her, "There is no one from your kindred who is called by this name."
And they were making signs to his father, as to what he would wish him to be called.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having asked for a writing tablet, he wrote, saying, "John is his name." And they all marveled.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And his mouth was opened immediately, and his tongue, and he was speaking, blessing God.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: And fear came upon all those dwelling around them, and in all the hill country of Judea all these words were being spoken of.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And all who heard them laid them up in their hearts, saying, "What then shall this child be?" For indeed the hand of the Lord was with him.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And Zechariah his father was filled with the Holy Spirit and prophesied, saying,
"Blessed be the Lord, the God of Israel, for he has visited and wrought redemption for his people,
verses.note: STRUCTURE: The Benedictus (vv.68-79) divides into two sections: God's redemption of Israel through the Davidic Messiah (68-75) and the prophetic destiny of John (76-79). THEOLOGICAL: "Epeskepsato" (ἐπεσκέψατο, "has visited") implies God's personal, gracious intervention — he has come to inspect, care for, and deliver his people. "Epoiēsen lytrōsin" — "wrought redemption" — uses sacrificial-economic language for God's liberating action.

verses.flow: and has raised up a horn of salvation for us in the house of David his servant,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "Horn of salvation" (keras sōtērias) is drawn from the royal-messianic vocabulary of the OT (Ps 18:2; 132:17; 1 Sam 2:10). The horn symbolizes strength and royal power. "In the house of David his servant" confirms this as Davidic-messianic fulfillment — God raises up a mighty deliverer from David's lineage, fulfilling the covenant of 2 Sam 7.

verses.flow: just as he spoke through the mouth of his holy prophets from of old,

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: salvation from our enemies, and from the hand of all who hate us;
to show mercy with our fathers, and to remember his holy covenant,
the oath which he swore to Abraham our father, to grant to us

words.m: verb aorist passive participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: that we, having been delivered from the hand of our enemies, might serve him without fear,
in holiness and righteousness before him all the days of our life.
And you, child, shall be called a prophet of the Most High; for you shall go before the face of the Lord to prepare his ways,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Prophet of the Most High" (prophētēs hypsistou) parallels Jesus' title "Son of the Most High" (v.32), carefully distinguishing John's role as prophetic forerunner from Jesus' identity as the divine Son. "You shall go before the face of the Lord" — "pro prosōpou kyriou" echoes Mal 3:1 and Isa 40:3, identifying John as the messenger preparing the way before YHWH himself, who comes in the person of the Messiah.

verses.flow: to give knowledge of salvation to his people in the forgiveness of their sins,
through the tender mercies of our God, by which the Dayspring from on high shall visit us,
verses.note: GREEK: "Anatolē ex hypsous" (ἀνατολὴ ἐξ ὕψους) — "Dayspring from on high" — is a richly layered messianic title. "Anatolē" (Strong's G395) means "rising" or "dawn" and translates Hebrew "tsemach" ("Branch") in the LXX of Jer 23:5, Zech 3:8, and 6:12, a recognized messianic designation. "Ex hypsous" — "from on high" — indicates heavenly origin. THEOLOGICAL: The Messiah is thus simultaneously the Davidic Branch growing from below and the divine Dayspring descending from above, uniting the earthly and heavenly dimensions of his person.

words.m: verb present middle participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: to shine upon those sitting in darkness and in the shadow of death, to guide our feet into the way of peace."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "To shine upon those sitting in darkness and in the shadow of death" quotes Isa 9:1-2 (LXX), the great messianic prophecy of light dawning in Galilee. "Shadow of death" (skia thanatou) echoes Ps 23:4 and Ps 107:10. "To guide our feet into the way of peace" — "eirēnē" (εἰρήνη) translates Hebrew "shalom," encompassing not merely absence of conflict but the total restoration of right relationship with God, humanity, and creation.

verses.flow: And the child grew and was strengthened in spirit, and he was in the wilderness until the day of his public showing to Israel.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: John's time "in the wilderness" connects him to the prophetic tradition of desert preparation and possibly to communities like those at Qumran near the Dead Sea. "Anadeixis" (ἀνάδειξις, Strong's G323) is a hapax legomenon in the NT, meaning "public showing" or "commissioning" — a term used in Greco-Roman contexts for the formal installation of officials. THEOLOGICAL: The wilderness motif recalls Israel's formation in the desert and the prophetic expectation that God's definitive salvation would begin again from the wilderness (Isa 40:3; Hos 2:14).

verses.flow: And it came to pass in those days that a decree went forth from Caesar Augustus to register all the inhabited earth.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Caesar Augustus (Octavian, reigned 27 BC–AD 14) issued imperial decrees (δόγμα, dógma, G1378) for census enrollments across the Roman Empire. Luke deliberately frames the birth of the true King of all the earth against the backdrop of the most powerful human ruler, showing that God's sovereign plan operates through and above imperial power. The term οἰκουμένη (oikouménē, G3625) refers to the entire inhabited world under Roman authority.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: (This registration was the first while Quirinius was governing Syria.)
And all were going to be registered, each to his own city.
And Joseph also went up from Galilee, out of the city of Nazareth, into Judea, to the city of David which is called Bethlehem, because of his being of the house and lineage of David,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Joseph's journey from Nazareth to Bethlehem fulfills Micah 5:2, which prophesied that from Bethlehem would come forth a ruler over Israel. The phrase "city of David" (πόλιν Δαυίδ) connects Jesus to the Davidic covenant (2 Sam 7:12-16). Luke emphasizes both "house" (οἶκος, oîkos, G3624) and "lineage" (πατριά, patriá, G3965) to establish royal genealogical legitimacy.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle dative singular feminine
verb present active participle dative singular feminine

verses.flow: to be registered with Mariam, the one having been betrothed to him, she being with child.
And it came to pass while they were there, the days were fulfilled for her to give birth.
And she bore her son, the firstborn, and she wrapped him in swaddling cloths and laid him in a manger, because there was no place for them in the guest chamber.
verses.note: GREEK: πρωτότοκος (prōtótokos, G4416) means "firstborn," carrying both legal rights of primogeniture and deep theological resonance with Israel as God's firstborn (Exod 4:22) and the messianic Psalm 89:27. The κατάλυμα (katályma, G2646) is not a commercial inn (πανδοχεῖον) but a guest room or lodging space, possibly in a relative's home. TYPOLOGY: The manger (φάτνη, phátnē, G5336) where animals feed receives the Bread of Life; the one who will be the Lamb of God is laid where livestock are nourished.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And shepherds were in the same region, living in the fields and keeping watch by night over their flock.
And an angel of the Lord stood before them, and the glory of the Lord shone around them, and they feared with a great fear.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "angel of the Lord" (ἄγγελος κυρίου) employs a standard Septuagint formula for divine messengers acting as emissaries of the heavenly court. The "glory of the Lord" (δόξα κυρίου) shining around them recalls the kabod-YHWH theophanies of the Old Testament (Exod 24:16-17; Ezek 1:28). The shepherds' response of great fear (ἐφοβήθησαν φόβον μέγαν) uses the cognate accusative construction typical of Semitic narrative style, emphasizing the overwhelming nature of the divine encounter.

verses.flow: And the angel said to them, "Fear not, for behold, I bring you good tidings of great joy, which shall be for all the people,
for there was born to you this day a Savior, who is Christ the Lord, in the city of David.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This is one of the most theologically dense verses in the New Testament. Three titles converge: σωτήρ (sōtḗr, G4990, "Savior"), χριστός (Christós, G5547, "Christ/Anointed One"), and κύριος (kýrios, G2962, "Lord"). In the LXX, "Savior" is applied to God himself (Isa 45:21); "Christ" designates the anointed Davidic king; and "Lord" renders both 'adon and the divine Name. The angel's announcement declares that this infant is simultaneously God's salvation, the promised Davidic Messiah, and sovereign Lord. TYPOLOGY: "City of David" echoes the angelic promise to David in 2 Samuel 7, now fulfilled in the flesh.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle accusative singular neuter
verb present middle participle accusative singular neuter

verses.flow: And this shall be the sign to you: you shall find a babe wrapped in swaddling cloths and lying in a manger."

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine
verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: And suddenly there came to be with the angel a multitude of the heavenly host, praising God and saying,
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "multitude of the heavenly host" (πλῆθος στρατιᾶς οὐρανίου) is the divine army of God's celestial council. The term στρατιά (stratiá, G4756) means "army" or "host," directly corresponding to the Hebrew tsaba in "YHWH of Hosts" (YHWH Tseba'ot). This is a momentary parting of the veil between the heavenly and earthly realms, as the divine council's worship becomes audible to human ears.

verses.flow: "Glory in the highest to God, and upon earth peace among men of good pleasure."
verses.note: GREEK: The genitive εὐδοκίας (eudokías, G2107) modifies ἀνθρώποις, yielding "men of [his] good pleasure" or "men upon whom his favor rests"—not a general wish for goodwill but a declaration of God's electing favor. THEOLOGICAL: The hymn has a tripartite structure: glory directed upward to God in the highest, peace directed downward to earth, and the identifying mark of its recipients—those upon whom divine favor rests. This mirrors the divine council's role of declaring the decrees of God.

words.m: verb perfect active participle accusative singular neuter

verses.flow: And it came to pass, when the angels departed from them into heaven, the shepherds spoke to one another, "Let us go now even unto Bethlehem and see this word that has come to pass, which the Lord has made known to us."

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present middle participle accusative singular neuter

verses.flow: And they came with haste and found both Mariam and Joseph, and the babe lying in the manger.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist passive participle genitive singular neuter

verses.flow: And having seen, they made known the word that had been spoken to them concerning this child.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist passive participle genitive plural neuter

verses.flow: And all who heard marveled concerning the things spoken to them by the shepherds.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: But Mariam kept all these words, pondering them in her heart.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And the shepherds returned, glorifying and praising God for all things which they had heard and seen, even as it had been spoken to them.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: And when eight days were fulfilled for the circumcising of him, his name was called Jesus, the name called by the angel before he was conceived in the womb.
And when the days of their purification were fulfilled according to the law of Moses, they brought him up to Jerusalem to present him to the Lord,
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The purification refers to the Levitical requirements of Lev 12:1-8, where a mother was ceremonially unclean for forty days after bearing a male child. Luke says "their purification" (αὐτῶν, autōn—plural), possibly including Joseph in the broader family purification or reflecting the presentation rite. THEOLOGICAL: The verb παραστῆσαι (parastēsai, "to present") indicates formal consecration before the Lord, connecting to the firstborn consecration law of Exodus 13:2, 12.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: even as it is written in the law of the Lord: "Every male opening the womb shall be called holy to the Lord,"
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The quotation is from Exodus 13:2, 12 (LXX). Every firstborn male belongs to God as a memorial of the Passover deliverance. Jesus as the firstborn is consecrated to the Lord, but uniquely he is the Lord to whom all firstborn belong. The one being presented is the very God to whom the presentation is made—a profound Christological irony Luke embeds in the narrative.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle accusative singular neuter

verses.flow: and to offer a sacrifice according to that which is spoken in the law of the Lord, "a pair of turtledoves or two young pigeons."

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And behold, there was a man in Jerusalem whose name was Simeon, and this man was righteous and devout, awaiting the consolation of Israel, and the Holy Spirit was upon him.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: And it had been revealed to him by the Holy Spirit that he would not see death before he had seen the Christ of the Lord.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "the Christ of the Lord" (τὸν χριστὸν κυρίου) echoes the LXX expression christos kyriou, "the Lord's Anointed" (1 Sam 2:10; Ps 2:2; Lam 4:20). The Holy Spirit's revelation (κεχρηματισμένον, from chrēmatízō, G5537—a divine oracle or warning) to Simeon that he would not see death before seeing the Messiah places him in the prophetic tradition of those who waited for God's eschatological king. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The Spirit functions here as the divine agent mediating the council's decree to a faithful human.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle accusative singular neuter

verses.flow: And he came in the Spirit into the temple, and when the parents brought in the child Jesus, for them to do according to the custom of the law concerning him,
he himself received him into his arms and blessed God and said,
"Now you release your servant, O Master, according to your word, in peace,
verses.note: GREEK: δέσποτα (despóta, G1203) is the vocative of despótēs, meaning "Master" or "Sovereign Lord"—a term denoting absolute ownership, used of God in contexts of sovereign authority (Acts 4:24; Rev 6:10). It differs from κύριος in emphasizing dominion over slaves or servants. The verb ἀπολύεις (apolýeis, "you release/dismiss") pictures a sentinel being relieved from duty, or a slave being set free. TYPOLOGY: Simeon's Nunc Dimittis echoes Isaiah 40:5 and 52:10, where God's salvation is revealed before all nations.

verses.flow: for my eyes have seen your salvation,
which you have prepared before the face of all peoples,
a light for revelation to the nations, and for glory of your people Israel."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: φῶς εἰς ἀποκάλυψιν ἐθνῶν (phōs eis apokálypsin ethnōn)—"a light for revelation to the nations"—draws directly from Isaiah 42:6 and 49:6, the Servant Songs, where the Servant of YHWH is appointed as a light to the Gentiles. The pairing with "glory of your people Israel" establishes the dual scope of the Messiah's mission: universal revelation to the nations and the consummation of Israel's hope. TYPOLOGY: Jesus fulfills the role of the Isaianic Servant whose light reaches beyond Israel to all peoples.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present passive participle dative plural neuter

verses.flow: And his father and mother were marveling at the things being spoken concerning him.

words.m: verb present passive participle accusative singular neuter

verses.flow: And Simeon blessed them and said to Mariam his mother, "Behold, this one is set for the fall and rising of many in Israel, and for a sign that is spoken against—
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Simeon's oracle to Mariam is both messianic and ominous. The phrase εἰς πτῶσιν καὶ ἀνάστασιν (eis ptōsin kai anástasin, "for fall and rising") indicates that Jesus will be the point of division—a stone of stumbling and a rock of rising (Isa 8:14; 28:16). The σημεῖον ἀντιλεγόμενον (sēmeîon antilegómenon, "sign spoken against") foreshadows the rejection of Jesus by the religious establishment and ultimately the cross.

verses.flow: and a sword shall pierce through your own soul also—so that the reasonings out of many hearts may be revealed."
verses.note: GREEK: ῥομφαία (rhomphaía, G4501) denotes a large, broad sword—not the common μάχαιρα—suggesting devastating penetration of grief. TYPOLOGY: The sword piercing Mariam's soul foreshadows her witness of the crucifixion (John 19:25-27). The purpose clause ὅπως ἂν ἀποκαλυφθῶσιν ("so that may be revealed") indicates that Jesus' ministry and death will serve as the ultimate apocalyptic disclosure of human hearts—what people truly believe will be exposed by how they respond to him.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative singular feminine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: And there was Anna, a prophetess, daughter of Phanuel, of the tribe of Asher. She was advanced in many days, having lived with a husband seven years from her virginity,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: and she was a widow until eighty-four years, who did not depart from the temple, serving with fastings and prayers night and day.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular feminine
verb present middle participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: And at that very hour, having come near, she gave thanks to God and spoke concerning him to all those awaiting the redemption of Jerusalem.
verses.note: GREEK: ἀνθωμολογεῖτο (anthōmologéomai, G437) is a NT hapax legomenon meaning "to give thanks" or "to confess praise in return." It appears in the LXX (e.g., Ps 79:13 LXX) but occurs only here in the New Testament. THEOLOGICAL: Anna speaks about the child to all those "awaiting the redemption (λύτρωσιν, lýtrōsin, G3085) of Jerusalem," a phrase rooted in prophetic expectation of God's eschatological deliverance of his people (Isa 52:9). Both Simeon and Anna function as prophetic witnesses confirming the child's messianic identity.

verses.flow: And when they had accomplished all things according to the law of the Lord, they returned to Galilee, to their own city, Nazareth.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: And the child grew and became strong, being filled with wisdom, and the grace of God was upon him.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Luke summarizes Jesus' childhood growth with language echoing the description of Samuel (1 Sam 2:26). The verb ἐκραταιοῦτο (ekrataioûto, "became strong") and the phrase "filled with wisdom" (πληρούμενον σοφίᾳ) indicate the genuine human development of the incarnate Son. The grace of God (χάρις θεοῦ) being "upon him" (ἐπ' αὐτό) points to the Father's continual favor resting on the child. TYPOLOGY: As Samuel grew in the temple precincts and found favor with God and men, so Jesus grows as the greater prophet-priest-king.

verses.flow: And his parents went every year to Jerusalem for the feast of the Passover.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: And when he became twelve years old, as they went up according to the custom of the feast,

words.m: verb aorist active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: and when they had completed the days, as they were returning, the boy Jesus remained behind in Jerusalem, and his parents did not know it.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: But supposing him to be in the company, they went a day's journey and were seeking him among the relatives and the acquaintances.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And not finding him, they returned to Jerusalem, seeking him.

words.m: verb present middle participle accusative singular masculine
verb present active participle accusative singular masculine
verb present active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: And it came to pass, after three days they found him in the temple, sitting in the midst of the teachers, both hearing them and questioning them.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And all those hearing him were amazed at his understanding and his answers.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And seeing him, they were astonished, and his mother said to him, "Child, why have you done thus to us? Behold, your father and I, being greatly distressed, were seeking you."
And he said to them, "Why is it that you were seeking me? Did you not know that I must be in the things of my Father?"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus' first recorded words in Luke are a declaration of divine sonship: "in the things of my Father" (ἐν τοῖς τοῦ πατρός μου). The phrase τοῦ πατρός μου ("my Father") is striking on the lips of a twelve-year-old, distinguishing his relationship to God from any other. The verb δεῖ (deî, G1163, "it is necessary") expresses divine compulsion and will appear throughout Luke's Gospel at critical moments of Jesus' mission (Luke 4:43; 9:22; 24:7, 26, 44). Even at age twelve, Jesus is aware of his unique vocation and identity as the Son of God.

verses.flow: And they did not understand the word which he spoke to them.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And he went down with them and came to Nazareth, and he was being subject to them. And his mother kept all these words in her heart.
And Jesus advanced in wisdom and in stature and in grace before God and men.
verses.note: GREEK: προέκοπτεν (proékopten, from prokóptō, G4298) means "to advance" or "to cut forward," like a pioneer cutting a path. TYPOLOGY: This summary statement parallels 1 Samuel 2:26 almost verbatim in the LXX, completing the Samuel typology Luke has woven throughout the infancy narrative. Jesus is the greater Samuel—prophet, intercessor, and the one who will anoint himself as both priest and king. The fourfold advance—wisdom, stature, and grace before both God and men—affirms the full, authentic humanity of the incarnate Word.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular masculine
verb present active participle genitive singular masculine
verb present active participle genitive singular masculine
verb present active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: Now in the fifteenth year of the reign of Tiberius Caesar, Pontius Pilate being governor of Judea, and Herod being tetrarch of Galilee, and Philip his brother being tetrarch of the region of Iturea and Trachonitis, and Lysanias being tetrarch of Abilene,
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Luke anchors the gospel narrative in precise Roman chronology — the fifteenth year of Tiberius Caesar places this around AD 28-29. The sixfold political designation (emperor, governor, three tetrarchs, and high priests) situates the divine word breaking into a specific moment of world history, emphasizing that God acts within real, datable events. The mention of multiple rulers also subtly portrays the fractured political landscape of Israel under foreign domination.

verses.flow: in the high priesthood of Annas and Caiaphas, the word of God came upon John the son of Zechariah in the wilderness.
verses.note: GREEK: The phrase ἐγένετο ῥῆμα θεοῦ (egeneto rhēma theou, "the word of God came") echoes the LXX prophetic formula (cf. Jer 1:1; Hos 1:1; Joel 1:1), deliberately placing John in the line of the Old Testament prophets. THEOLOGICAL: The dual high priesthood of Annas and Caiaphas reflects a corrupted priestly office — Annas had been deposed by Rome but retained power behind Caiaphas, pointing to the broken state of Israel's religious leadership into which God's word now irrupts.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And he went into all the surrounding region of the Jordan, proclaiming a baptism of repentance for the forgiveness of sins,
verses.note: GREEK: βάπτισμα μετανοίας (baptisma metanoias) — "baptism of repentance." Metanoia (Strong's G3341) means a fundamental change of mind and direction, not mere remorse. The genitive μετανοίας is a genitive of characterization: this baptism is defined by and expresses repentance. TYPOLOGY: The Jordan river setting evokes Israel's original crossing into the Promised Land (Josh 3), suggesting a new entry into covenant faithfulness.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: as it has been written in the book of the words of Isaiah the prophet: "A voice of one crying out in the wilderness: Prepare the way of the Lord, make straight his paths;
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The quotation from Isaiah 40:3-5 (LXX) originally addressed Israel's return from Babylonian exile, the preparation of a highway for God himself to travel. Luke applies this to John preparing the way for the Lord Jesus, identifying Jesus with the coming of YHWH himself. STRUCTURE: Luke extends the Isaiah quotation further than Matthew or Mark, continuing through verse 6 to include "all flesh shall see the salvation of God," reflecting Luke's universal emphasis.

verses.flow: every valley shall be filled, and every mountain and hill shall be brought low, and the crooked shall become straight, and the rough ways shall become smooth,
and all flesh shall see the salvation of God."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Luke alone among the Synoptics includes this final line from Isaiah 40:5 LXX — καὶ ὄψεται πᾶσα σὰρξ τὸ σωτήριον τοῦ θεοῦ ("and all flesh shall see the salvation of God"). This universalism is a key Lukan theme: the salvation brought by the Messiah is not for Israel alone but for all nations (cf. Luke 2:30-32; Acts 28:28). GREEK: σωτήριον (sōtērion, Strong's G4992) — "salvation" in a substantive, concrete sense, denoting God's saving act made visible.

words.m: verb present middle participle dative plural masculine
verb present active participle genitive singular feminine

verses.flow: He said therefore to the crowds coming out to be baptized by him, "Offspring of vipers! Who warned you to flee from the coming wrath?
verses.note: GREEK: Γεννήματα ἐχιδνῶν (gennēmata echidnōn) — "offspring of vipers." Génnēma (Strong's G1081) means "that which is begotten/produced," emphasizing lineage and nature. The viper metaphor implies a poisonous and dangerous nature inherited from their spiritual parentage. THEOLOGICAL: John's warning subverts the assumption that ethnic descent guarantees divine favor, a theme Jesus will continue throughout Luke's Gospel.

verses.flow: Produce therefore fruits worthy of repentance, and do not begin to say within yourselves, "We have Abraham as father." For I say to you that God is able from these stones to raise up children to Abraham.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: John demolishes the presumption that Abrahamic descent automatically secures covenant standing. God's power to raise children from stones (lithōn/lithōn playing on a possible Aramaic wordplay with "children"/benayya and "stones"/abnayya) demonstrates divine sovereignty over covenant membership. This anticipates the inclusion of Gentiles in the people of God (cf. Rom 4:16-17; Gal 3:7-9).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: And already also the axe is laid to the root of the trees; every tree therefore not producing good fruit is cut down and cast into fire.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The imagery of the axe at the root of the trees and the fruitless tree cast into fire is an eschatological judgment motif. This echoes Isaiah 10:33-34 where God hews down the forest of the proud. The present tense κεῖται ("is laid") indicates the imminence of judgment — the axe is already positioned, not merely approaching.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And the crowds were questioning him, saying, "What then shall we do?"

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle dative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And answering he said to them, "The one having two tunics, let him share with the one having none, and the one having food, let him do likewise."
And tax collectors also came to be baptized, and they said to him, "Teacher, what shall we do?"

words.m: verb perfect passive participle accusative singular neuter

verses.flow: And he said to them, "Collect nothing more beyond what has been appointed to you."

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And soldiers also were questioning him, saying, "And what shall we do, even we?" And he said to them, "Extort no one by violence, nor accuse falsely, and be content with your wages."

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular masculine
verb present middle participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: Now as the people were in expectation, and all were reasoning in their hearts concerning John, whether perhaps he might be the Christ,
verses.note: GREEK: διαλογιζομένων (dialogizomenōn, Strong's G1260) — "reasoning, deliberating" — denotes inner dialogue and weighing of possibilities. The people's messianic expectation reflects the intense eschatological hope of Second Temple Judaism. HISTORICAL: Various messianic expectations circulated in this period, including a royal Davidic messiah, a priestly messiah (as at Qumran), and a prophetic figure like Moses (Deut 18:15).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: John answered, saying to all, "I indeed baptize you with water; but he who is mightier than I is coming, of whom I am not worthy to loose the strap of his sandals. He shall baptize you in the Holy Spirit and fire,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: John draws a sharp contrast between his water baptism and the coming One's baptism ἐν πνεύματι ἁγίῳ καὶ πυρί ("in the Holy Spirit and fire"). The language of unworthiness to loose a sandal strap signifies that John considers himself lower than a slave in relation to the Coming One. TYPOLOGY: The dual baptism of Spirit and fire echoes the prophetic promises of purification (Mal 3:2-3; Isa 4:4) and the outpouring of the Spirit (Joel 2:28-29), fulfilled at Pentecost (Acts 2:3-4) where tongues "as of fire" appear with the Spirit.

verses.flow: whose winnowing fork is in his hand, to thoroughly cleanse his threshing floor and to gather the wheat into his storehouse, but the chaff he will burn up with unquenchable fire."
verses.note: GREEK: πτύον (ptyon, Strong's G4425) — "winnowing fork," used to toss threshed grain into the air so wind separates wheat from chaff. ἄσβεστος (asbestos, Strong's G762) — "unquenchable," describing the finality and inescapability of eschatological judgment. THEOLOGICAL: This verse presents Christ as the eschatological Judge who separates the righteous from the unrighteous — a function attributed to God alone in the Old Testament (cf. Ps 1:4-6; Mal 4:1).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Therefore with many other exhortations also he was proclaiming good tidings to the people.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But Herod the tetrarch, being reproved by him concerning Herodias, the wife of his brother, and concerning all the evil things which Herod had done,
added also this upon all: he shut up John in prison.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle genitive singular masculine
verb present middle participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: Now it came to pass, when all the people were baptized, and Jesus having been baptized and praying, the heaven was opened,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Luke uniquely notes that Jesus was praying when heaven opened — prayer accompanies every major turning point in Luke's Gospel (Luke 6:12; 9:18, 28-29; 22:41-44). The opening of heaven signifies the rupture of the barrier between the divine and human realms, recalling Isaiah 64:1: "Oh, that you would rend the heavens and come down." STRUCTURE: Luke places the baptism after John's imprisonment narrative (vv.19-20), de-emphasizing the baptizer and focusing on the divine attestation of Jesus.

verses.flow: and the Holy Spirit descended upon him in bodily form as a dove, and a voice came from heaven: "You are my Son, the Beloved; in you I am well pleased."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The voice from heaven combines Psalm 2:7 ("You are my Son") — a royal enthronement psalm — with Isaiah 42:1 ("in whom my soul delights") — the Servant of the Lord. This dual allusion identifies Jesus simultaneously as the Davidic King and the Suffering Servant. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The heavenly voice constitutes a divine decree of sonship, echoing the council decrees of Psalm 2 where God installs his King on Zion. The bodily descent of the Spirit (σωματικῷ εἴδει, sōmatikō eidei — "in bodily form") emphasizes the reality and visibility of this anointing.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And Jesus himself was about thirty years of age when he began, being the son, as was supposed, of Joseph, the son of Eli,
verses.note: GREEK: ὡς ἐνομίζετο (hōs enomizeto) — "as was supposed/reckoned by custom." The verb νομίζω (nomizō, Strong's G3543) implies conventional assumption, not legal or biological certainty. Luke thus carefully qualifies the Joseph-Jesus relationship. HISTORICAL: Beginning at about thirty years of age echoes David's age at the start of his reign (2 Sam 5:4) and the age at which Levites began temple service (Num 4:3), framing Jesus as both King and Priest.

verses.flow: the son of Maththat, the son of Levi, the son of Melchi, the son of Jannai, the son of Joseph,
the son of Mattathias, the son of Amos, the son of Nahum, the son of Hesli, the son of Naggai,
the son of Maath, the son of Mattathias, the son of Semein, the son of Josech, the son of Joda,
the son of Joanan, the son of Rhesa, the son of Zerubbabel, the son of Shealtiel, the son of Neri,
the son of Melchi, the son of Addi, the son of Cosam, the son of Elmadam, the son of Er,
the son of Joshua, the son of Eliezer, the son of Jorim, the son of Maththat, the son of Levi,
the son of Simeon, the son of Judah, the son of Joseph, the son of Jonam, the son of Eliakim,
the son of Melea, the son of Menna, the son of Mattatha, the son of Nathan, the son of David,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Luke traces Jesus' line through Nathan, son of David (2 Sam 5:14), rather than through Solomon as in Matthew's genealogy. This traces a different Davidic line, possibly through Mary's lineage, and avoids the curse on Jeconiah's royal line (Jer 22:30). Nathan's line nonetheless establishes Jesus firmly within the house of David, fulfilling the Davidic covenant promise (2 Sam 7:12-16).

verses.flow: the son of Jesse, the son of Obed, the son of Boaz, the son of Sala, the son of Nahshon,
the son of Amminadab, the son of Admin, the son of Arni, the son of Hezron, the son of Perez, the son of Judah,
the son of Jacob, the son of Isaac, the son of Abraham, the son of Terah, the son of Nahor,
the son of Serug, the son of Reu, the son of Peleg, the son of Eber, the son of Shelah,
the son of Cainan, the son of Arphaxad, the son of Shem, the son of Noah, the son of Lamech,
the son of Methuselah, the son of Enoch, the son of Jared, the son of Mahalaleel, the son of Cainan,
the son of Enosh, the son of Seth, the son of Adam, the son of God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Luke traces the genealogy all the way back to τοῦ Ἀδὰμ τοῦ θεοῦ — "of Adam, of God." This is the theological climax of the genealogy: Jesus is not merely son of Abraham (as in Matthew) but son of Adam, and ultimately Son of God. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The designation of Adam as "of God" (τοῦ θεοῦ) frames human origin in direct divine relationship and sonship. By tracing Jesus back to Adam and to God, Luke establishes Jesus as the representative of all humanity — the last Adam (cf. Rom 5:14; 1 Cor 15:45) — whose redemptive work extends to every nation, not Israel alone.

verses.flow: And Jesus, full of the Holy Spirit, returned from the Jordan and was being led by the Spirit in the wilderness,
verses.note: GREEK: "Plērēs" (πλήρης, G4134) meaning "full, complete" — denotes not partial but total saturation by the Holy Spirit. The passive voice of "ēgeto" (ἤγετο, imperfect of ágō) indicates Jesus was continually being led by the Spirit, not acting on his own initiative. TYPOLOGY: Jesus's forty days in the wilderness recapitulates Israel's forty years of wilderness testing (Deut 8:2), yet where Israel failed, the faithful Son succeeds.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist passive participle genitive plural feminine

verses.flow: for forty days, being tempted by the devil. And he ate nothing in those days, and when they were completed, he hungered.
verses.note: GREEK: "Peirazomenos" (πειραζόμενος, G3985) is a present passive participle — the temptation was ongoing throughout the forty days, not confined to the three episodes that follow. "Diabolos" (διάβολος, G1228) literally means "slanderer" or "one who casts across," i.e., the accuser who throws charges (cf. Rev 12:10). TYPOLOGY: The forty-day fast parallels Moses on Sinai (Exod 34:28) and Elijah's journey to Horeb (1 Kgs 19:8), positioning Jesus as the prophet greater than both.

verses.flow: And the devil said to him, "If you are the Son of God, speak to this stone that it might become bread."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The conditional "Ei" (εἰ) with the indicative assumes the condition as true for the sake of argument — "If you are the Son of God (and you are)..." The devil does not question Jesus's identity but challenges him to exercise divine prerogatives outside the Father's will. This is a test of obedient sonship, not of ontological identity.

verses.flow: And Jesus answered to him, "It is written that, 'Not on bread alone shall the man live.'"

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having led him up, he showed him all the kingdoms of the inhabited world in a moment of time.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The devil's claim to show "all the kingdoms of the inhabited world" (oikoumenē, G3625) in a "stigmē chronou" (στιγμῇ χρόνου, a point of time) implies a supernatural vision. The term "oikoumenē" refers specifically to the civilized, governed world — the political sphere over which the devil claims delegated authority (cf. John 12:31; Eph 6:12; Dan 10:13,20).

verses.flow: And the devil said to him, "To you I will give this authority, all of it, and the glory of them, for it has been delivered to me, and to whomever I wish I give it.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Paradedotai" (παραδέδοται, G3860, perfect passive) — "it has been delivered over to me" — implies the devil claims a prior bestowal of cosmic authority. This language echoes the Deuteronomy 32 worldview where God allotted the nations to the sons of God (LXX Deut 32:8-9), some of whom became corrupt. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The devil's claim is not entirely false — he exercises delegated, usurped authority over fallen world systems — but it is deceptive in implying absolute and rightful ownership. Jesus does not dispute the claim but refuses the terms.

verses.flow: You therefore, if you will worship before me, it shall all be yours."

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And answering, Jesus said to him, "It is written: 'The Lord your God you shall worship, and him alone you shall serve.'"
verses.note: GREEK: Jesus quotes Deuteronomy 6:13 (LXX), using "Kyrion" (Κύριον) for the divine name and "latreuō" (λατρεύω, G3000), which denotes cultic, sacred service — worship reserved for God alone. TYPOLOGY: Where Israel worshipped the golden calf and bowed to foreign gods in the wilderness, the true Son of God refuses all idolatry even when offered all earthly power.

verses.flow: And he led him to Jerusalem and set him upon the pinnacle of the temple and said to him, "If you are the Son of God, cast yourself down from here,
for it is written that, 'He shall command his angels concerning you, to guard you,'
and that, 'Upon their hands they shall bear you up, lest you strike your foot against a stone.'"

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And answering, Jesus said to him, "It has been said, 'You shall not put to the test the Lord your God.'"
verses.note: GREEK: "Ekpeiraseis" (ἐκπειράσεις, G1598) is an intensified form of "peirazō" — to test thoroughly, to put to the ultimate test. Jesus quotes Deuteronomy 6:16 (LXX), referencing the incident at Massah (Exod 17:1-7). THEOLOGICAL: Jesus refuses to manipulate God into proving his faithfulness, demonstrating the trust that Israel failed to show.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having completed every temptation, the devil departed from him until an opportune time.
verses.note: GREEK: "Achri kairou" (ἄχρι καιροῦ) — "until an opportune/appointed time" — "kairos" (G2540) denotes not mere chronological time but a strategic, decisive season. THEOLOGICAL: Luke uniquely emphasizes this temporary withdrawal; the devil returns at the Passion (Luke 22:3, 53), suggesting the cross is the supreme confrontation between Jesus and the powers of darkness.

verses.flow: And Jesus returned in the power of the Spirit into Galilee, and a report went out through all the surrounding region concerning him.

verses.flow: And he came to Nazareth, where he had been brought up, and he entered, according to his custom, on the day of the Sabbath into the synagogue, and he stood up to read.

verses.flow: And there was delivered to him the scroll of the prophet Isaiah, and having unrolled the scroll, he found the place where it was written:

words.m: verb perfect passive participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: "The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me to proclaim good tidings to the poor; he has sent me to proclaim to the captives release, and to the blind recovery of sight, to send forth the oppressed in deliverance,
verses.note: GREEK: "Echrisen" (ἔχρισεν, G5548, from chriō) — "he anointed me" — is the verbal root from which "Christos" (Christ/Anointed One) derives. Jesus is identifying himself as the Anointed One of Isaiah 61:1-2. TYPOLOGY: The anointing language evokes the threefold anointing of prophet, priest, and king in the OT. Jesus's reading conflates Isaiah 61:1-2a with Isaiah 58:6 ("to send forth the oppressed in deliverance"), showing a deliberate hermeneutical act. THEOLOGICAL: The "captives" (aichmalōtois) and "oppressed" (tethrausmenous) carry both physical and spiritual connotations — liberation from sin, Satan, and systemic injustice.

verses.flow: to proclaim the acceptable year of the Lord."
verses.note: GREEK: "Eniautos kyriou dektos" (ἐνιαυτὸν κυρίου δεκτόν) — "the acceptable year of the Lord" — echoes the Jubilee legislation of Leviticus 25, when debts were cancelled, slaves freed, and land restored. TYPOLOGY: Jesus stops reading mid-sentence in Isaiah 61:2, omitting "and the day of vengeance of our God." This deliberate omission announces the era of grace and Jubilee fulfillment while deferring the day of judgment to his return (cf. Acts 17:31; Rev 19).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having rolled up the scroll, he gave it back to the attendant and sat down. And the eyes of all in the synagogue were gazing intently at him.
And he began to say to them, "Today this Scripture has been fulfilled in your hearing."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Sēmeron" (Σήμερον, G4594) — "Today" — carries immense eschatological weight. The perfect tense "peplērōtai" (πεπλήρωται, G4137) indicates that the Scripture stands fulfilled in the present with abiding results. Jesus does not say "I will fulfill" but declares the fulfillment accomplished in his very speaking. This is the programmatic declaration of Jesus's ministry in Luke.

words.m: verb present middle participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: And all were bearing witness to him and were marveling at the words of grace proceeding from his mouth, and they were saying, "Is not this the son of Joseph?"

words.m: verb aorist middle participle accusative plural neuter

verses.flow: And he said to them, "Surely you will speak to me this parable: 'Physician, heal yourself!' Whatever things we heard having come to pass in Capernaum, do also here in your homeland."
And he said, "Amen, I say to you that no prophet is accepted in his own homeland."
But in truth I say to you, there were many widows in the days of Elijah in Israel, when the heaven was shut up for three years and six months, when a great famine came upon all the land,
and to none of them was Elijah sent, except to Zarephath of Sidon, to a woman, a widow.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Zarephath (Sarepta) was a Phoenician town in the territory of Sidon — pagan Gentile territory. TYPOLOGY: Jesus's appeal to Elijah being sent to a Gentile widow (1 Kgs 17:8-24) and Elisha cleansing Naaman the Syrian (2 Kgs 5:1-14) foreshadows the inclusion of the Gentiles in God's salvation. This teaching provokes the rage of verse 28 precisely because it implies God's grace transcends ethnic Israel.

verses.flow: And many lepers were in Israel in the time of Elisha the prophet, and none of them was cleansed, except Naaman the Syrian.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And all in the synagogue were filled with wrath, hearing these things.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And having risen up, they cast him out of the city and led him to the brow of the hill upon which their city had been built, so as to throw him down headlong.
verses.note: GREEK: "Katakrēmnisai" (κατακρημνίσαι, G2630) is a hapax legomenon in the NT — "to throw down a precipice/cliff." HISTORICAL: Nazareth sits on a ridge overlooking the Jezreel Valley. The attempt to cast Jesus from the cliff edge anticipates the violent rejection that culminates at Calvary. TYPOLOGY: The rejected prophet motif (v. 24) reaches its climax; like Joseph rejected by his brothers, Jesus is expelled from his own people only to become their deliverer.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But he, having passed through the midst of them, went on his way.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Dielthōn dia mesou autōn" (διελθὼν διὰ μέσου αὐτῶν) — "having passed through the midst of them" — implies a supernatural or sovereign departure. His hour had not yet come (cf. John 7:30; 8:59). Jesus's authority over his own destiny is evident even in the face of murderous intent.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And he went down to Capernaum, a city of Galilee, and he was teaching them on the Sabbaths.
And they were astonished at his teaching, for his word was with authority.

verses.flow: And in the synagogue there was a man having a spirit of an unclean demon, and he cried out with a great voice,
"Ha! What is there between us and you, Jesus of Nazareth? Have you come to destroy us? I know you, who you are — the Holy One of God!"
verses.note: GREEK: "Ea" (Ἔα) is an interjection of alarm or dismay — "Ha!" or "Ah!" The demon uses the plural "us" (hēmas), possibly speaking for the entire demonic realm. "Ho hagios tou theou" (ὁ ἅγιος τοῦ θεοῦ) — "the Holy One of God" — is a messianic title expressing Jesus's unique consecration and divine identity. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The demon recognizes Jesus's authority within the spiritual hierarchy. The question "Have you come to destroy us?" acknowledges Jesus's eschatological mission to overthrow the rebellious spiritual powers (cf. 1 John 3:8; Col 2:15).

verses.flow: And demons also were coming out from many, crying out and saying, "You are the Son of God!" And rebuking them, he was not permitting them to speak, because they knew him to be the Christ.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The demons declare both "You are the Son of God" and (by Luke's editorial comment) that they "knew him to be the Christ." Luke equates the titles — Son of God and Christ are two aspects of the same identity. Jesus silences them not because their confession is false but because the messianic secret must unfold on God's terms, not through demonic proclamation. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The unclean spirits possess accurate theological knowledge of Jesus's identity as the Anointed King, yet their knowledge does not produce saving faith — it produces terror (cf. James 2:19).

verses.flow: And when day came, he went out and journeyed to a desolate place. And the crowds were seeking him, and they came up to him and were restraining him from going away from them.
But he said to them, "It is necessary for me to proclaim the good tidings of the kingdom of God to the other cities also, for upon this purpose I was sent forth."
verses.note: GREEK: "Euangelisasthai" (εὐαγγελίσασθαι, G2097) — "to proclaim good tidings" — is a key Lukan term linking Jesus's mission to Isaiah's herald of salvation (Isa 52:7; 61:1). "Apestalēn" (ἀπεστάλην, G649, aorist passive) — "I was sent forth" — emphasizes divine commissioning; Jesus is the sent one par excellence, the Apostle of the Father (cf. Heb 3:1). THEOLOGICAL: The "kingdom of God" (basileian tou theou) is the central content of Jesus's proclamation — God's sovereign reign breaking into the present age through Jesus's person and ministry.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And he was proclaiming in the synagogues of Judea.

verses.flow: And it came to pass, as the crowd was pressing upon him and hearing the word of God, that he himself was standing beside the lake of Gennesaret,
verses.note: GREEK: The phrase ἐπικεῖσθαι (epíkeisthai, "to press upon") conveys the crowd physically crowding around Jesus. "The word of God" (τὸν λόγον τοῦ θεοῦ) is Luke's characteristic way of describing Jesus's teaching, identifying it as divine revelation. Lake Gennesaret is the Sea of Galilee (cf. Matt 14:34; Mark 6:53).

verses.flow: And when he ceased speaking, he said to Simon, "Put out into the deep, and let down your nets for a catch."

verses.flow: And Simon answering said, "Master, through the whole night having labored, we took nothing; but upon your word I will let down the nets."
verses.note: GREEK: Ἐπιστάτα (epistatá, "Master") is a term unique to Luke among the Gospels (Strong's G1988), used only by disciples addressing Jesus. It denotes one who stands over or commands—a superintendent or overseer. Simon's obedience despite failure ("upon your word," ἐπὶ τῷ ῥήματί σου) demonstrates nascent faith responding to divine authority.

verses.flow: And having done this, they enclosed a great multitude of fish, and their nets were breaking.

verses.flow: And Simon Peter, having seen it, fell down at the knees of Jesus, saying, "Depart from me, for I am a sinful man, Lord."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Peter's response echoes Isaiah's reaction before the holy God (Isa 6:5). The recognition of sinfulness in the presence of divine power reveals that this miraculous catch functions as a theophany—a manifestation of God's presence through Jesus. Peter addresses him as κύριε (kýrie, "Lord"), which in this context transcends mere respectful address.

verses.flow: For astonishment had seized him and all those with him, at the catch of fish which they had taken;

verses.flow: and likewise also James and John, sons of Zebedee, who were partners with Simon. And Jesus said to Simon, "Fear not; from now on you shall be catching men."
verses.note: GREEK: ζωγρῶν (zōgrōn, "catching alive," Strong's G2221) is a striking term meaning literally "to take alive" or "to capture for life." In classical usage it described taking prisoners of war alive. Jesus redefines the fisherman's vocation: from catching fish unto death to catching humans unto life. TYPOLOGY: The miraculous catch prefigures the apostolic mission and the great ingathering of the nations (cf. John 21:1-14; Ezek 47:9-10).

verses.flow: And having brought the boats to the land, leaving all things, they followed him.

verses.flow: And it came to pass, while he was in one of the cities, behold, a man full of leprosy; and having seen Jesus, falling upon his face, he begged him, saying, "Lord, if you are willing, you are able to cleanse me."

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having stretched out his hand, he touched him, saying, "I am willing; be cleansed." And immediately the leprosy departed from him.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus touching a leper violates Levitical purity boundaries (Lev 13:45-46), yet rather than Jesus becoming unclean, the leper is made clean. This reveals Jesus's authority over the ritual system itself—holiness flows from him and overpowers impurity. The immediacy (εὐθέως, euthéōs) emphasizes divine power.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And he charged him to tell no one, "But having gone, show yourself to the priest, and offer concerning your cleansing as Moses commanded, for a testimony to them."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The command to show himself to the priest and offer according to what Moses commanded (Lev 14:1-32) serves as "a testimony to them" (εἰς μαρτύριον αὐτοῖς). Jesus upholds the Mosaic law while simultaneously demonstrating that he possesses the authority that the priestly system was designed to mediate. The cleansed leper becomes living evidence that the messianic age has dawned (cf. Isa 35:5-6; Matt 11:5).

verses.flow: But the word concerning him was spreading all the more, and great crowds were gathering to hear and to be healed from their infirmities.

verses.flow: But he himself was withdrawing in the desolate places and praying.

verses.flow: And it came to pass on one of the days, that he was teaching, and there were Pharisees and teachers of the law sitting by, who had come from every village of Galilee and Judea and Jerusalem. And the power of the Lord was present for him to heal.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The power of the Lord was present for him to heal" (δύναμις κυρίου ἦν εἰς τὸ ἰᾶσθαι αὐτόν)—κυρίου here likely refers to YHWH, indicating that divine power was operative through Jesus. Luke uniquely emphasizes this divine empowerment. HISTORICAL: The presence of Pharisees and teachers of the law from every village of Galilee, Judea, and Jerusalem indicates that Jesus's ministry had attracted official scrutiny from the religious establishment.

verses.flow: And behold, men carrying upon a bed a man who was paralyzed, and they were seeking to bring him in and to place him before him.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And not finding by what way they might bring him in, on account of the crowd, having gone up upon the housetop, they let him down through the tiles, with the small bed, into the midst before Jesus.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having seen their faith, he said, "Man, your sins are forgiven you."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The passive "are forgiven" (ἀφέωνται, perfect passive indicative) is a divine passive—God is the implied agent. Yet Jesus speaks this forgiveness directly, claiming to enact what only God can do. This sets up the confrontation in v.21 and establishes Jesus's divine prerogative.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And the scribes and the Pharisees began to reason, saying, "Who is this who speaks blasphemies? Who is able to forgive sins except God alone?"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The scribes and Pharisees reason correctly that God alone can forgive sins (cf. Isa 43:25; Ps 103:3). Their logic is sound; their error is failing to recognize who stands before them. Jesus does not correct their theology about forgiveness being God's prerogative—instead he demonstrates that he possesses that very prerogative. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The claim to forgive sins places Jesus in the role of the divine Judge, a function belonging to the God of Israel alone in Second Temple Jewish theology.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But Jesus, having known their reasonings, answering said to them, "Why do you reason in your hearts?
"Which is easier, to say, 'Your sins are forgiven you,' or to say, 'Rise and walk'?

words.m: verb perfect passive participle dative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: "But that you may know that the Son of Man has authority upon the earth to forgive sins"—he said to the paralyzed man—"I say to you, rise, and having taken up your small bed, go to your house."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The Son of Man" (ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου) is Jesus's preferred self-designation, drawn from Daniel 7:13-14 where the "one like a son of man" receives authority, glory, and an everlasting kingdom from the Ancient of Days. Jesus claims that this heavenly authority to forgive sins operates "upon the earth" (ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς)—the eschatological kingdom is breaking into the present age through his person. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The Son of Man in Daniel 7 receives dominion from the throne of God; Jesus enacts that dominion here by forgiving sins and commanding healing.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And immediately, having risen before them, having taken up that upon which he was lying, he went away to his house, glorifying God.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And astonishment seized them all, and they were glorifying God, and they were filled with fear, saying, "We have seen extraordinary things today."
verses.note: GREEK: ἔκστασις (ékstasis, Strong's G1611) denotes more than mere surprise—it is a displacement of the mind, an overwhelmed state before the numinous. παράδοξα (parádoxa, "extraordinary things," hapax legomenon in NT) literally means "beyond opinion" or "contrary to expectation"—things that exceed all human categories of understanding.

verses.flow: And after these things he went out and beheld a tax collector by the name of Levi, sitting at the tax booth, and he said to him, "Follow me."

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having left all things, rising up, he was following him.

verses.flow: And Levi made a great feast for him in his house, and there was a great crowd of tax collectors and others who were reclining with them.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And the Pharisees and their scribes were grumbling against his disciples, saying, "Why do you eat and drink with the tax collectors and sinners?"
