verses.flow: And Jesus answering said to them, "Those who are well have no need of a physician, but those who are ill.
"I have not come to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus's mission statement echoes the prophetic tradition of God's pursuit of the lost (Ezek 34:16; Hos 6:6). "Repentance" (μετάνοια, metánoia) signifies not mere regret but a fundamental reorientation of mind and life toward God. Jesus defines his messianic vocation not as confirming the righteous but as reclaiming sinners.

verses.flow: And they said to him, "The disciples of John fast often and make prayers, and likewise also those of the Pharisees, but yours eat and drink."
And Jesus said to them, "Are you able to make the sons of the bridechamber fast while the bridegroom is with them?
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "Sons of the bridechamber" (τοὺς υἱοὺς τοῦ νυμφῶνος) designates wedding guests. Jesus identifies himself as the bridegroom, a title reserved for YHWH in the OT prophets (Isa 62:5; Hos 2:16-20; Jer 2:2). This is an implicit divine claim: God's covenant marriage to his people is being consummated in Jesus's presence. The coming "taking away" (ἀπαρθῇ, v.35) foreshadows his death.

verses.flow: "But days will come, and when the bridegroom is taken away from them, then they will fast in those days."

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And he also spoke a parable to them: "No one, having torn a piece from a new garment, puts it upon an old garment; but if so, both the new will tear, and the piece from the new will not match the old.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The twin parables of the patch and wineskins declare that Jesus's ministry is not a reform of the old covenant system but the arrival of something radically new. The "new garment" and "new wine" represent the kingdom of God breaking in through Jesus. Attempting to contain the new reality within old structures will destroy both. STRUCTURE: Luke uniquely includes the detail that the new garment is torn, emphasizing double loss—the old is not helped and the new is ruined.

verses.flow: "And no one puts new wine into old wineskins; but if so, the new wine will burst the wineskins, and it will be poured out, and the wineskins will be destroyed.
"But new wine must be put into new wineskins.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: "And no one having drunk old wine desires new, for he says, 'The old is good.'"
verses.note: GREEK: χρηστός (chrēstós, "good, pleasant, kind"; some manuscripts read χρηστότερος, "better") describes the old wine. This verse, unique to Luke, acknowledges the human tendency to prefer the familiar over the new. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus does not condemn those who prefer "the old" but recognizes the difficulty of receiving the new thing God is doing. This serves as a warning about the resistance his message will face from those comfortable with existing religious structures.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And it came to pass on a Sabbath that he was going through the grainfields, and his disciples were plucking and eating the heads of grain, rubbing them with their hands.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The grain-plucking incident echoes 1 Samuel 21:1-6, where David and his men ate the consecrated bread. Jesus draws a direct parallel between David's authority to override ceremonial law in necessity and his own authority as the greater David, the Anointed King. GREEK: σπορίμων (sporimōn, "sown fields/grainfields") appears also in Matt 12:1 and Mark 2:23 in the same Sabbath controversy.

verses.flow: And some of the Pharisees said, "Why do you do what is not lawful on the Sabbaths?"

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And answering them, Jesus said, "Have you not even read this, what David did when he hungered, he himself and those who were with him—

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: how he entered into the house of God and took the bread of the Presence and ate, and gave to those with him, which it is not lawful to eat except the priests alone?"
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The "bread of the Presence" (ἄρτους τῆς προθέσεως, artous tēs protheseōs) refers to the twelve loaves set before God's face in the tabernacle (Lev 24:5-9). Jesus' argument from the lesser to the greater implies that his authority exceeds that of David, and that mercy and human need transcend ceremonial regulation (cf. 1 Sam 21:1-6; Hos 6:6).

verses.flow: And he said to them, "The Son of Man is Lord of the Sabbath."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The claim "Lord of the Sabbath" (Κύριός ἐστιν τοῦ σαββάτου ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου) is a breathtaking assertion of divine prerogative, since in the OT God himself instituted the Sabbath (Gen 2:2-3; Exod 20:8-11). By claiming lordship over it, the Son of Man asserts authority equal to the Creator. GREEK: Κύριος (kyrios, Strong's G2962) here carries the full weight of sovereign authority, not merely "master" in a social sense.

verses.flow: And it came to pass on another Sabbath that he entered into the synagogue and was teaching. And there was a man there, and his right hand was withered.
And the scribes and the Pharisees were watching him closely, whether he would heal on the Sabbath, so that they might find cause to accuse him.

words.m: verb present active participle dative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But he himself knew their reasonings, and he said to the man having the withered hand, "Rise up and stand in the midst." And having risen, he stood.
And Jesus said to them, "I ask you whether it is lawful on the Sabbath to do good or to do evil, to save a soul or to destroy it?"
verses.note: GREEK: ἀγαθοποιῆσαι (agathopoiēsai, "to do good") and κακοποιῆσαι (kakopoiēsai, "to do evil") are compound infinitives contrasting active goodness with active harm. Jesus reframes the Sabbath question: inaction in the face of suffering is itself evil. The parallel term ψυχὴν σῶσαι (psychēn sōsai, "to save a soul/life") elevates the stakes beyond mere halakhic debate (cf. Mark 3:4).

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having looked around at them all, he said to him, "Stretch out your hand." And he did so, and his hand was restored.
But they were filled with madness and were discussing with one another what they might do to Jesus.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And it came to pass in those days that he went out to the mountain to pray, and he was continuing all night in prayer to God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Luke uniquely records that Jesus spent the entire night in prayer (διανυκτερεύων ἐν τῇ προσευχῇ τοῦ θεοῦ) before choosing the Twelve. GREEK: διανυκτερεύων (dianyktereuōn, Strong's G1273) is a NT hapax legomenon, meaning "to spend the whole night." The phrase τῇ προσευχῇ τοῦ θεοῦ could mean "the prayer of God" (prayer directed to God) or possibly "the place of prayer to God." This night of prayer precedes the most consequential selection of leaders in redemptive history.

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And when it became day, he called his disciples to himself, and having chosen from them twelve, whom he also named apostles:
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The choosing of twelve apostles (ἀποστόλους, apostolous, "sent ones") deliberately mirrors the twelve tribes of Israel, signaling that Jesus is reconstituting Israel around himself as its King and center. GREEK: ἐκλεξάμενος (eklexamenos, "having chosen out") is a middle voice participle, emphasizing Jesus' personal, deliberate selection (cf. John 15:16).

verses.flow: Simon, whom he also named Peter, and Andrew his brother, and James and John, and Philip and Bartholomew,

verses.flow: and Matthew and Thomas, and James the son of Alphaeus, and Simon the one called the Zealot,
and Judas the son of James, and Judas Iscariot, who became a betrayer.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having come down with them, he stood on a level place, and a great crowd of his disciples, and a great multitude of the people from all Judea and Jerusalem and the seacoast of Tyre and Sidon,
verses.note: STRUCTURE: Luke's "Sermon on the Plain" (vv. 17-49) parallels Matthew's Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5-7) but is set on a "level place" (τόπου πεδινοῦ). The geographic detail may carry theological weight: Jesus descends to meet the people where they are, in contrast to Moses ascending Sinai. HISTORICAL: The mention of Tyre and Sidon indicates Gentile regions, showing Jesus' fame extending beyond Jewish territories.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: who came to hear him and to be healed from their diseases. And those being troubled by unclean spirits were being cured.
And all the crowd was seeking to touch him, for power was going out from him and was healing all.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And he, having lifted up his eyes upon his disciples, was saying: "Blessed are the poor, for yours is the kingdom of God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Luke's Beatitudes are in the second person ("Blessed are you") and directed specifically at the disciples, whereas Matthew's are in the third person. Luke's version is starkly material—"the poor" (οἱ πτωχοί) without Matthew's qualifier "in spirit"—emphasizing God's concrete eschatological reversal. TYPOLOGY: This echoes the Magnificat of Mary (Luke 1:52-53) and the theme of divine reversal found throughout the OT prophets (Isa 61:1-2; 1 Sam 2:7-8).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Blessed are those who hunger now, for you shall be satisfied. Blessed are those who weep now, for you shall laugh.
Blessed are you when men hate you, and when they separate you, and reproach you, and cast out your name as evil, on account of the Son of Man.
verses.note: GREEK: ἀφορίσωσιν (aphorisōsin, "they separate/exclude you") likely refers to excommunication from the synagogue, a practice attested in Second Temple Judaism. HISTORICAL: Being "separated" and having one's name "cast out as evil" (ἐκβάλωσιν τὸ ὄνομα ὑμῶν ὡς πονηρόν) reflects the experience of Jewish Christians expelled from synagogues, a situation becoming acute by the time Luke writes.

verses.flow: Rejoice in that day and leap for joy, for behold, your reward is great in heaven; for according to the same things their fathers were doing to the prophets.
But woe to you who are rich, for you are receiving your consolation in full.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Woe to you who have been filled now, for you shall hunger. Woe to you who laugh now, for you shall mourn and weep.
Woe when all men speak well of you, for according to the same things their fathers were doing to the false prophets.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The parallel between the false prophets (ψευδοπροφήταις) and those whom "all men speak well of" inverts the prophetic pattern of the OT. True prophets like Jeremiah, Elijah, and Micaiah were persecuted and reviled (1 Kgs 22:8; Jer 38:6), while false prophets enjoyed popular acclaim (Jer 5:31; Mic 2:11). Jesus establishes a pattern: authentic kingdom proclamation invites opposition.

verses.flow: And if you love those who love you, what credit is that to you? For even sinners love those who love them.

verses.flow: And if you do good to those who do good to you, what credit is that to you? For even sinners do the same.
And if you lend to those from whom you hope to receive, what credit is that to you? Even sinners lend to sinners, so that they might receive back the equal amount.

verses.flow: But love your enemies, and do good, and lend, expecting nothing in return; and your reward shall be great, and you shall be sons of the Most High, for he himself is kind toward the ungrateful and evil.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Sons of the Most High" (υἱοὶ Ὑψίστου) is a significant divine council term. DIVINE_COUNCIL: In the OT, "sons of the Most High" (בְּנֵי עֶלְיוֹן, cf. Ps 82:6) refers to divine beings in God's heavenly assembly. Jesus applies this title to his followers who emulate the Father's character—kindness to the ungrateful and evil—thereby elevating believers to a status associated with the heavenly council. GREEK: Ὑψίστου (Hypsistou, Strong's G5310) is the Greek rendering of Hebrew Elyon, a name for God emphasizing his supreme sovereignty over all other powers.

verses.flow: Be merciful, just as your Father is merciful.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Be merciful as your Father is merciful" (οἰκτίρμονες καθὼς ὁ πατὴρ ὑμῶν οἰκτίρμων ἐστίν) parallels the imitatio Dei principle. GREEK: οἰκτίρμων (oiktirmōn, Strong's G3629) denotes deep compassion from the bowels, echoing the Hebrew רַחוּם (rachum), used of God in Exod 34:6 and Ps 103:8. Where Matthew 5:48 says "perfect" (τέλειος), Luke uses "merciful," grounding divine imitation in compassionate action rather than abstract moral completeness.

verses.flow: And do not judge, and you shall by no means be judged. And do not condemn, and you shall by no means be condemned. Release, and you shall be released.

verses.flow: Give, and it shall be given to you—good measure, pressed down, shaken together, overflowing, they shall give into your bosom. For with what measure you measure, it shall be measured back to you.
verses.note: GREEK: The fourfold description—καλὸν πεπιεσμένον σεσαλευμένον ὑπερεκχυννόμενον (good, pressed down, shaken together, overflowing)—employs marketplace grain-measuring imagery. HISTORICAL: In ancient grain markets, a generous merchant would press down the grain, shake the container to settle it, and let it overflow. The "bosom" (κόλπον) refers to the fold of the garment used as a pouch for carrying goods. Jesus portrays God's generosity as extravagantly exceeding any human measure.

verses.flow: And he spoke also a parable to them: "Can a blind man lead a blind man? Shall not both fall into a pit?

verses.flow: A disciple is not above the teacher; but everyone who has been fully trained shall be as his teacher.
verses.note: GREEK: κατηρτισμένος (katērtismenos, "fully trained/perfected," Strong's G2675) is a perfect passive participle meaning "completely equipped" or "restored to proper condition." The term is used in medical Greek for setting broken bones. The goal of discipleship is not to surpass the teacher but to be conformed to him—a principle with profound Christological implications (cf. Rom 8:29; Eph 4:12-13).

verses.flow: And why do you look at the speck that is in the eye of your brother, but the beam that is in your own eye you do not perceive?

First cast out the beam from your own eye, and then you shall see clearly to cast out the speck that is in the eye of your brother.

verses.flow: For there is no good tree producing rotten fruit, nor again a rotten tree producing good fruit.
For each tree is known by its own fruit. For from thorns they do not gather figs, nor from a bramble bush do they harvest grapes.
The good man out of the good treasure of his heart brings forth the good, and the evil man out of the evil brings forth the evil. For out of the abundance of the heart his mouth speaks.
And why do you call me "Lord, Lord," and do not do what I say?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The doubled vocative "Lord, Lord" (Κύριε κύριε) intensifies the claim of allegiance while exposing its emptiness without obedience. Jesus asserts that authentic recognition of his lordship requires corresponding action, not mere verbal confession (cf. Matt 7:21). The very fact that he demands obedience as the criterion of relationship implicitly claims divine authority.

verses.flow: He is like a man building a house, who dug and went deep and laid a foundation upon the rock. And when a flood came, the river burst against that house and was not able to shake it, because it had been well built.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The image of building on rock recalls the OT theme of God as the Rock (צוּר) of Israel (Deut 32:4; 2 Sam 22:2; Ps 18:2). GREEK: ἐβάθυνεν (ebathynen, "he went deep") is unique to Luke's version and emphasizes the deliberate, costly effort of obedient faith. The flood (πλημμύρης, plēmmyrēs—a NT hapax legomenon) represents eschatological testing. STRUCTURE: The parable forms the climactic conclusion to the sermon, presenting two destinies based on response to Jesus' words.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But the one who heard and did not do is like a man who built a house upon the earth without a foundation, against which the river burst, and immediately it collapsed, and the ruin of that house was great.
verses.note: GREEK: ῥῆγμα (rhēgma, Strong's G4485) is a NT hapax legomenon meaning "ruin" or "wreckage," derived from ῥήγνυμι ("to break/burst"). The word emphasizes total catastrophic collapse. THEOLOGICAL: The contrast between the two builders is not between hearing and not hearing, but between hearing-and-doing versus hearing-and-not-doing. Both men heard the words of Jesus; the difference is obedience. The "great ruin" warns of eschatological judgment for those who acknowledge Jesus verbally but refuse his lordship in practice.

verses.flow: After He had completed all His words in the hearing of the people, He entered into Capernaum.

verses.flow: And a certain centurion's slave, who was dear to him, being ill, was about to die.
verses.note: GREEK: The term ἔντιμος (entimos, "dear/honored") indicates this was no ordinary slave but one held in high esteem. The word ἑκατοντάρχης (hekatontarchēs) denotes a Roman officer commanding a century (approximately 80-100 soldiers). HISTORICAL: Roman centurions stationed in Galilee were typically part of Herod Antipas's forces modeled on Roman organization, though some may have served Rome directly.

verses.flow: And they, having come to Jesus, were beseeching Him earnestly, saying, "He is worthy to whom You should grant this,
"for he loves our nation, and he himself built the synagogue for us."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: A Gentile centurion who built a synagogue for the Jewish community represents the phenomenon of "God-fearers" — Gentiles attracted to Judaism without full conversion. Archaeological evidence from Capernaum confirms the presence of a notable synagogue. THEOLOGICAL: This foreshadows the inclusion of Gentiles in the kingdom, a major Lukan theme (Acts 10).

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: And Jesus was going with them. And when He was already not far from the house, the centurion sent friends, saying to Him, "Lord, do not trouble Yourself, for I am not worthy that You should enter under my roof.
Therefore neither did I count myself worthy to come to You. But speak a word, and let my servant be healed.
verses.note: GREEK: The term παῖς (pais) here can mean "servant" or "child," and is distinct from δοῦλος (doulos, "slave") used in v.2. Luke uses both terms, reflecting the intimate relationship. The centurion's faith that Jesus can heal by a spoken word (λόγῳ, logō — a single word/command) parallels the creative word of God in Genesis and the Psalms (Ps 33:6, 107:20). THEOLOGICAL: This anticipates the authority of Christ's word over all creation.

verses.flow: For I also am a man set under authority, having soldiers under myself, and I say to this one, 'Go,' and he goes, and to another, 'Come,' and he comes, and to my slave, 'Do this,' and he does it."

verses.flow: And having heard these things, Jesus marveled at him, and turning to the crowd following Him, He said, "I say to you, not even in Israel have I found such great faith."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: That Jesus "marveled" (ἐθαύμασεν, ethaumasen) is remarkable — the divine Son expressing genuine amazement at human faith. This is one of only two occasions where Jesus is said to marvel (cf. Mark 6:6, at unbelief). The centurion's faith surpasses that found in all Israel, establishing a pattern Luke develops throughout Luke-Acts: Gentile faith shaming Israelite unfaith.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And it came to pass on the next day, He went to a city called Nain, and His disciples were going with Him, and a great crowd.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And as He drew near to the gate of the city, behold, one who had died was being carried out, an only-begotten son of his mother, and she was a widow, and a considerable crowd of the city was with her.
verses.note: GREEK: μονογενής (monogenēs) — "only-begotten" or "one-of-a-kind" — the same word used of Jesus in John 3:16. TYPOLOGY: The widow's only son echoes the widow of Zarephath whose son Elijah raised (1 Kgs 17:17-24). Luke deliberately parallels Jesus with Elijah, yet shows Jesus as greater — Elijah prayed; Jesus commands by His own authority.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having seen her, the Lord had compassion upon her and said to her, "Do not weep."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Luke calls Jesus ὁ κύριος (ho kyrios, "the Lord") in the narrative voice — not merely "teacher" or "rabbi." This is Luke's confession of Jesus's divine lordship. The verb ἐσπλαγχνίσθη (esplanchnisthē, "had compassion") derives from σπλάγχνα (splanchna, "inner organs/bowels"), signifying the deepest visceral compassion.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having come forward, He touched the bier, and the bearers stood still. And He said, "Young man, to you I say, arise."
verses.note: GREEK: σορός (soros) — an open bier or coffin for carrying the dead, not a sealed tomb. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus touches the bier, which would render one ceremonially unclean under Torah (Num 19:11-16), yet rather than defilement passing to Jesus, life passes from Him to the dead. His holiness is contagious rather than contaminated. His direct command "Arise" (ἐγέρθητι, egerthēti) carries the same authority as the creative word of God.

verses.flow: And the dead man sat up and began to speak, and He gave him to his mother.

verses.flow: And fear seized all, and they were glorifying God, saying, "A great prophet has been raised up among us," and, "God has visited His people."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The crowd's declaration "God has visited His people" (ἐπεσκέψατο ὁ θεὸς τὸν λαὸν αὐτοῦ) echoes Zechariah's prophecy in Luke 1:68 and the language of Exodus (Exod 4:31). The verb ἐπισκέπτομαι (episképtomai) carries divine council overtones — God personally intervening in the affairs of His people. THEOLOGICAL: They rightly perceive divine visitation but may not yet grasp that this prophet IS God visiting in person.

verses.flow: And this word concerning Him went out in all Judea and in all the surrounding region.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The Coming One" (ὁ ἐρχόμενος, ho erchomenos) is a messianic title drawn from Ps 118:26, Mal 3:1, and Hab 2:3 (LXX). John's question from prison is not necessarily doubt but may be a pedagogical sending — ensuring his disciples encounter Jesus directly. TYPOLOGY: The question itself reflects the eschatological expectation of Second Temple Judaism for a definitive redemptive figure.

In that hour He healed many from diseases and afflictions and evil spirits, and to many who were blind He graciously granted sight.

verses.flow: And answering, He said to them, "Having gone, report to John what you have seen and heard: the blind receive sight, the lame walk, lepers are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, the poor are evangelized.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Jesus's answer directly echoes Isaiah 35:5-6 and 61:1 — the hallmarks of the messianic age. Each item corresponds to an Isaianic prophecy: blind seeing (Isa 29:18), lame walking (Isa 35:6), lepers cleansed (implied in Isa 53:4), deaf hearing (Isa 35:5), dead raised (Isa 26:19), poor evangelized (Isa 61:1). Jesus answers not with a title but with evidence — His works fulfill the prophetic script.

verses.flow: And blessed is whoever is not offended on account of Me."
verses.note: GREEK: σκανδαλίζω (skandalizō) — "to cause to stumble, to be offended" — from σκάνδαλον (skandalon), a trap-stick or stumbling block. THEOLOGICAL: This gentle warning implies that the Messiah's manner of coming — humble, suffering, not immediately overthrowing Rome — could be a stumbling block. Blessedness belongs to those who do not find Jesus's messianic profile offensive (cf. 1 Pet 2:8, Isa 8:14).

verses.flow: And when the messengers of John had departed, He began to speak to the crowds concerning John: "What did you go out into the wilderness to behold? A reed being shaken by the wind?

words.m: verb perfect passive participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: But what did you go out to see? A man clothed in soft garments? Behold, those in splendid clothing and living in luxury are in the palaces of kings.
But what did you go out to see? A prophet? Yes, I say to you, and far more than a prophet.
This is he concerning whom it has been written: 'Behold, I send My messenger before Your face, who shall prepare Your way before You.'
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This is a composite quotation from Mal 3:1 and Exod 23:20 (LXX). In Malachi, YHWH says "I send My messenger before MY face" — yet here Jesus applies it as "before YOUR face," identifying Himself as the Lord whom the messenger precedes. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "messenger" (ángelos) language connects John to the role of the divine herald who prepares the way for God's own arrival, as in the angelic messenger tradition.

verses.flow: I say to you, among those born of women there is no one greater than John; yet the least in the kingdom of God is greater than he.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus's declaration about John is both the highest human commendation and a startling relativization — the least in the kingdom of God surpasses even this greatest of prophets. This indicates a radical eschatological shift: the kingdom inaugurated by Jesus creates a new order of existence that transcends even the greatest figures of the old covenant era. GREEK: μείζων (meizōn) is comparative of μέγας — "greater," used twice with deliberate contrast.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The βουλή (boulē) or "counsel/purpose" of God is a term with divine council resonance — the deliberated will and decree of God (cf. Acts 2:23, 4:28, Eph 1:11). The Pharisees and lawyers "rejected" (ἠθέτησαν, ēthetēsan) this counsel "against themselves" (εἰς ἑαυτούς) — their rejection harms only themselves, not God's plan. DIVINE_COUNCIL: God's boulē is His sovereign decree, and human rejection cannot nullify it but only exclude the rejector.

You did not anoint My head with oil, but she anointed My feet with ointment.
For this reason I say to you, her sins, which are many, have been forgiven, for she loved much. But to whom little is forgiven, he loves little."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The logic of this verse is often misread. Jesus does not say her love earned forgiveness; rather, her great love is the evidence and result of great forgiveness already received. The parable in vv.41-42 makes the causal direction clear: forgiveness produces love, not the reverse. The one forgiven much, loves much. GREEK: ἀφέωνται (apheōntai) is perfect passive — "have been and remain forgiven" — the state is already accomplished.

verses.flow: And He said to her, "Your sins have been forgiven."

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And those reclining at table with Him began to say among themselves, "Who is this who even forgives sins?"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The question "Who is this who even forgives sins?" echoes the controversy in Luke 5:21 where scribes and Pharisees asked the same. In Jewish theology, only God forgives sins (Isa 43:25, Ps 51:4). Jesus's act of forgiving sins is an implicit claim to divine authority and identity — precisely the conclusion Luke intends his readers to reach. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Forgiveness of sins is a prerogative of the divine throne; Jesus exercises it from a dining couch.

verses.flow: And He said to the woman, "Your faith has saved you; go in peace."
verses.note: GREEK: σέσωκέν (sesōken) — perfect active of σῴζω (sōzō), meaning "has saved" with lasting result. The word encompasses physical healing, spiritual deliverance, and eschatological salvation. THEOLOGICAL: "Go into peace" (εἰς εἰρήνην, eis eirēnēn) — not merely "in peace" (ἐν εἰρήνῃ) but "into peace" — she enters a new state of shalom, of wholeness and right relationship with God. Faith is the instrument, not the cause; Christ is the source of salvation.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And it came to pass in the time following, that he was journeying through city by city and village by village, proclaiming and bringing the good news of the kingdom of God, and the twelve were with him,
verses.note: GREEK: The phrase "ἐν τῷ καθεξῆς" (en tō kathexēs) is a distinctly Lukan expression meaning "in the time following" or "in succession," emphasizing the orderly progression of Jesus's ministry. "Εὐαγγελιζόμενος" (euangelizomenos, Strong's G2097) means "bringing good news" — a present middle participle indicating ongoing proclamation. HISTORICAL: This itinerant preaching circuit through both cities (poleis) and villages (kōmas) reflects the comprehensive nature of Jesus's Galilean ministry.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative plural feminine

verses.flow: and certain women who had been healed from evil spirits and infirmities: Mary, the one called Magdalene, from whom seven demons had gone out,
verses.note: GREEK: "Τεθεραπευμέναι" (tetherapeumenai) is a perfect passive participle, indicating a completed healing with lasting effect. HISTORICAL: Mary Magdalene's seven demons (ἑπτὰ δαιμόνια) indicate a severe case of spiritual oppression; the number seven in Jewish thought signifies completeness. She is distinguished from the sinful woman of Luke 7:36-50, despite later conflation in Western tradition.

verses.flow: and Joanna, wife of Chuza, steward of Herod, and Susanna, and many others, who were ministering to them out of their possessions.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Joanna's connection to Chuza (Χουζᾶ), Herod Antipas's steward (ἐπίτροπος, epitropos — a high-ranking household administrator), reveals that the gospel had penetrated even Herod's court. "Διηκόνουν" (diēkonoun) indicates ongoing financial and material support — these women were patrons of the movement, a remarkable feature in first-century Judaism.

verses.flow: And when a great crowd was gathering together, and those from city after city were coming to him, he spoke by means of a parable:

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

And his disciples were questioning him, saying, "What might this parable be?"

verses.flow: And he said, "To you it has been given to know the mysteries of the kingdom of God, but to the rest it is given in parables, so that seeing they may not see, and hearing they may not understand.
verses.note: GREEK: "Μυστήρια" (mystēria, Strong's G3466) does not mean hidden esoteric knowledge but rather divine truths previously concealed and now revealed to those with ears to hear. TYPOLOGY: The quotation draws on Isaiah 6:9-10 (LXX), where the prophet's commission includes the hardening of Israel — a pattern now fulfilled in Jesus's parabolic teaching, where the same word brings illumination to disciples and obscurity to the resistant. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The mysteries of the kingdom relate to God's sovereign plan now being unveiled through the Messiah.

verses.flow: Now this is the parable: The seed is the word of God.

verses.flow: And those along the path are those who have heard; then the devil comes and takes away the word from their heart, so that they might not, having believed, be saved.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Luke alone among the Synoptics names "the devil" (ὁ διάβολος, ho diabolos, Strong's G1228) rather than "Satan" in the sower interpretation, emphasizing the role of the cosmic adversary in preventing saving faith. The purpose clause "ἵνα μὴ πιστεύσαντες σωθῶσιν" (that they might not, having believed, be saved) makes explicit the soteriological stakes: the devil's aim is to prevent the faith-to-salvation sequence.

verses.flow: And those upon the rock are those who, when they hear, receive the word with joy; and these have no root, who believe for a time and in a season of testing fall away.

verses.flow: And that in the good earth—these are those who, having heard the word, hold it fast in a noble and good heart, and bear fruit with endurance.
verses.note: GREEK: "Καρδίᾳ καλῇ καὶ ἀγαθῇ" (kardia kalē kai agathē) — "noble and good heart" — uses language resonant with Hellenistic moral philosophy (the καλὸς κἀγαθός ideal), yet Luke applies it to receptivity to God's word. "Κατέχουσιν" (katechousin, Strong's G2722) means to "hold fast" or "retain," implying active grip against opposing forces. "Ὑπομονῇ" (hypomonē) denotes patient, steadfast endurance under trial — the distinguishing mark of true faith.

verses.flow: Now no one, having lit a lamp, covers it with a vessel or places it under a bed, but places it upon a lampstand, so that those entering in may see the light.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The pairing of "κρυπτόν" (hidden) and "ἀπόκρυφον" (secret/concealed, Strong's G614) emphasizes that all things hidden in the present age will be brought to manifest disclosure. This applies both to the present hiddenness of the kingdom and to the future eschatological revelation when all will be laid bare.

verses.flow: Take heed therefore how you hear; for whoever has, to him it shall be given; and whoever does not have, even what he thinks he has shall be taken from him."

verses.flow: But he, answering, said to them, "My mother and my brothers are these: those who hear the word of God and do it."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus redefines kinship around obedience to the word of God rather than biological descent. This is not a rejection of Mary but a radical declaration that spiritual family, constituted by hearing and doing God's word, takes precedence. TYPOLOGY: This echoes the Deuteronomic call to covenant faithfulness (Deut 6:4-6) and anticipates the new covenant community formed around Christ.

verses.flow: And it came to pass on one of the days, that he himself got into a boat, and his disciples with him, and he said to them, "Let us cross over to the other side of the lake." And they set sail.

verses.flow: And having come to him, they woke him, saying, "Master, Master, we are perishing!" And he, having arisen, rebuked the wind and the raging of the water; and they ceased, and there came a calm.
verses.note: GREEK: "Ἐπιστάτα" (epistata, Strong's G1988) is a uniquely Lukan title for Jesus, used only by his disciples, meaning "Master" or "Commander" — carrying overtones of authority. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus rebukes (ἐπετίμησεν, epetimēsen) the wind and the raging water with the same verb used for rebuking demons (4:35), suggesting sovereign authority over both spiritual powers and the natural order. TYPOLOGY: This echoes Psalm 107:29 and Psalm 89:9, where God alone stills the seas — the disciples' question "Who then is this?" points toward divine identity.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And he said to them, "Where is your faith?" And having been afraid, they marveled, saying to one another, "Who then is this, that he commands even the winds and the water, and they obey him?"
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The question "Who then is this?" (Τίς ἄρα οὗτός ἐστιν) is theologically loaded. In the Old Testament, sovereignty over sea and storm is exclusively the prerogative of YHWH (Ps 65:7; 89:9; 107:29; Job 38:8-11). The disciples are confronting the implication that Jesus exercises divine authority over creation itself.

verses.flow: And they sailed down to the region of the Gerasenes, which is opposite Galilee.

verses.flow: And having seen Jesus, he cried out and fell down before him, and with a loud voice said, "What is there between me and you, Jesus, Son of God Most High? I beg you, do not torment me!"
verses.note: GREEK: "Τί ἐμοὶ καὶ σοί" (ti emoi kai soi) is a Semitic idiom meaning "What have I to do with you?" — expressing awareness of a fundamental incompatibility between the demonic realm and the Son of God. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The demon's identification of Jesus as "Son of God Most High" (υἱὲ τοῦ θεοῦ τοῦ ὑψίστου) uses "Most High" (Hypsistos, Strong's G5310), a title emphasizing God's supreme sovereignty over all spiritual beings. The demons recognize the divine council hierarchy that humans fail to perceive.

verses.flow: For he had been commanding the unclean spirit to come out from the man. For many times it had seized him, and he was bound with chains and shackles, being kept under guard, yet breaking the bonds he was driven by the demon into the desolate places.
And Jesus asked him, "What is your name?" And he said, "Legion," for many demons had entered into him.
And they were beseeching him that he would not command them to depart into the abyss.
verses.note: GREEK: "Ἄβυσσον" (abysson, Strong's G12) refers to the abyss or bottomless deep — the place of confinement for rebellious spiritual entities. DIVINE_COUNCIL: This detail is unique to Luke and reveals the demons' awareness of their appointed eschatological judgment. The abyss appears in Jewish apocalyptic literature (1 Enoch 10:4-6; Jubilees 5:6) as the prison of fallen spiritual beings, and in Revelation 9:1-11 and 20:1-3 as the place of binding. The demons beg not to be sent there before the appointed time.

verses.flow: And the whole multitude of the surrounding region of the Gerasenes asked him to depart from them, for they were seized with great fear. And he himself, having entered into a boat, returned.

verses.flow: Now the man from whom the demons had gone out was begging him to be with him. But he sent him away, saying,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: "Return to your house, and declare what great things God has done for you." And he went away, proclaiming throughout the whole city what great things Jesus had done for him.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus commands the healed man to declare what "God" (ὁ θεός) has done, but the man proclaims what "Jesus" (ὁ Ἰησοῦς) has done — Luke subtly identifies the work of Jesus with the work of God. This is one of the few cases where Jesus commissions proclamation rather than commanding silence, notably in Gentile/semi-pagan territory (the Decapolis region).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And when Jesus returned, the crowd welcomed him, for they were all expecting him.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And behold, there came a man whose name was Jairus, and this man was a ruler of the synagogue. And falling at the feet of Jesus, he was beseeching him to come into his house,
for he had an only-begotten daughter, about twelve years of age, and she was dying. And as he went, the crowds were pressing against him.
verses.note: GREEK: "Μονογενής" (monogenēs, Strong's G3439) means "only-begotten" or "one of a kind" — the same term applied to Jesus in John 3:16. Luke uses it to heighten the pathos: she is the sole daughter, irreplaceable. STRUCTURE: Luke masterfully interweaves the Jairus narrative with the hemorrhaging woman, creating a literary sandwich (intercalation) where the themes of twelve years, faith, and being "saved/healed" (sōzō) link both stories.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: And a woman, being in a flow of blood for twelve years, who had spent all her livelihood on physicians, was not able to be healed by anyone.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: Having come up behind him, she touched the fringe of his garment, and immediately the flow of her blood stopped.
verses.note: GREEK: "Κρασπέδου" (kraspedou, Strong's G2899) refers to the tassel or fringe of the garment, the ritual tzitzit commanded in Numbers 15:38-39 and Deuteronomy 22:12. TYPOLOGY: The woman's touch of the garment fringe recalls Malachi 4:2 (LXX 3:20), where healing is found "in the wings" of the Sun of Righteousness — "wings" (kanaph) being the same Hebrew term for the corner of a garment bearing the fringes.

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And Jesus said, "Who is the one who touched me?" And when all were denying it, Peter said, "Master, the crowds are pressing upon you and crushing against you."

words.m: verb perfect active participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: But Jesus said, "Someone touched me, for I perceived power having gone out from me."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Δύναμιν ἐξεληλυθυῖαν ἀπ' ἐμοῦ" (dynamin exelēlythuian ap' emou) — "power having gone out from me." This is not magical force drained involuntarily; Jesus perceives it because he is the conscious source and mediator of divine power. His question is pedagogical, drawing the woman from secrecy to public confession of faith.

verses.flow: And the woman, seeing that she had not escaped notice, came trembling, and having fallen down before him, she declared before all the people the reason she had touched him, and how she was healed immediately.
And he said to her, "Daughter, your faith has saved you. Go in peace."
verses.note: GREEK: "Σέσωκέν" (sesōken) is the perfect tense of sōzō (Strong's G4982), meaning both "has saved" and "has healed" — the double meaning is theologically intentional. Physical healing is the sign of comprehensive salvation. "Εἰς εἰρήνην" (eis eirēnēn) echoes the Hebrew shalom — she is dismissed not merely without disease but into wholeness and covenant peace.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: While he was still speaking, someone comes from the ruler of the synagogue, saying, "Your daughter has died; trouble the Teacher no longer."

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But Jesus, having heard this, answered him, "Do not fear; only believe, and she shall be saved."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Μόνον πίστευσον" (monon pisteuson) — "only believe" — is an aorist imperative calling for decisive, immediate faith in the face of death's apparent finality. The promise "σωθήσεται" (sōthēsetai, "she shall be saved/healed") again holds the double meaning of sōzō, encompassing both physical restoration and eschatological salvation. Jesus calls Jairus to the same faith that saved the hemorrhaging woman.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having come into the house, he did not permit anyone to enter with him except Peter and John and James, and the father of the child and her mother.
Now all were weeping and mourning for her. But he said, "Do not weep, for she has not died, but is sleeping."

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And they were laughing him to scorn, knowing that she had died.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But he, having taken hold of her hand, called out, saying, "Child, arise!"
verses.note: GREEK: "Ἡ παῖς, ἔγειρε" (hē pais, egeire) — "Child, arise!" — uses the present imperative of egeirō (Strong's G1453), the same root used throughout the NT for resurrection. TYPOLOGY: Jesus takes her by the hand (kratēsas tēs cheiros), an act that would render one ritually unclean by contact with a corpse under Levitical law (Num 19:11), yet instead of defilement flowing to Jesus, life flows from him — a reversal pattern seen also with the leper (Luke 5:13) and the hemorrhaging woman.

verses.flow: And her spirit returned, and she arose immediately. And he directed that something be given to her to eat.
verses.note: GREEK: "Ἐπέστρεψεν τὸ πνεῦμα αὐτῆς" (epestrepsen to pneuma autēs) — "her spirit returned." This reflects the Jewish anthropology where death involves the departure of the spirit (ruach/pneuma) from the body (cf. Eccl 12:7; Gen 35:18). The command to give her food demonstrates the physical, bodily reality of the restoration — this is no ghostly apparition but genuine resurrection to embodied life.

words.m: verb perfect active participle accusative singular neuter

verses.flow: And her parents were amazed. But he charged them to tell no one what had happened.

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having called together the twelve, he gave to them power and authority over all the demons and to heal diseases,
verses.note: GREEK: "Synkalesamenos" (συγκαλεσάμενος) is a middle participle meaning "having called together" — indicating Jesus personally assembled the Twelve. "Dynamis" (δύναμις, Strong's G1411) denotes inherent power or ability, while "exousia" (ἐξουσία, G1849) denotes delegated authority. Jesus grants both the raw spiritual power and the right to wield it. THEOLOGICAL: This dual commission of power and authority reflects Christ's own divine prerogatives being delegated to his appointed representatives.

verses.flow: and he sent them forth to proclaim the kingdom of God and to heal the sick.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The twin mission — proclaiming the kingdom of God and healing — mirrors Jesus' own ministry pattern (Luke 4:43; 6:18). The kingdom (basileía, G932) is not merely announced but demonstrated through the signs accompanying it. TYPOLOGY: The sending of the Twelve echoes God sending Moses and the seventy elders (Num 11:16-17), now reconstituted around the Messiah.

verses.flow: And he said to them, "Take nothing for the journey, neither staff, nor bag, nor bread, nor silver, nor have two tunics each.
And into whatever house you enter, there remain, and from there go forth.

verses.flow: And as many as do not receive you, going forth from that city, shake off the dust from your feet as a testimony against them."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Shaking dust from the feet was a Jewish symbolic act performed when leaving Gentile territory, signifying separation from defilement. Jesus instructs the Twelve to treat rejecting Jewish towns as though they were pagan — a severe prophetic sign. The phrase "eis martyrion" (εἰς μαρτύριον) indicates this act serves as a legal witness against them at judgment.

verses.flow: And having taken the five loaves and the two fish, having looked up to heaven, he blessed them and broke them and was giving them to the disciples to set before the crowd.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Jesus' actions — taking, looking up to heaven, blessing, breaking, and giving — form a eucharistic pattern that reappears at the Last Supper (Luke 22:19). The feeding in the wilderness also typologically echoes God's provision of manna through Moses (Exod 16). THEOLOGICAL: Christ acts here as both the new Moses providing bread and as the true source of the bread, foreshadowing the Lord's Supper.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: And they ate and were all satisfied, and there was taken up that which remained over to them of broken pieces: twelve baskets.
verses.note: GREEK: "Klasmaton kophinoi dodeka" (κλασμάτων κόφινοι δώδεκα) — twelve baskets of broken pieces. The "kophinos" (G2894) was a distinctly Jewish wicker basket. THEOLOGICAL: The twelve baskets correspond to the twelve tribes of Israel, signifying that the Messiah's provision is abundant and sufficient for all of God's people. The superabundance points to eschatological fullness.

words.m: verb present middle participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: And it came to pass, while he was praying alone, the disciples were with him, and he questioned them, saying, "Who do the crowds say that I am?"

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And he said to them, "But you, who do you say that I am?" And Peter, answering, said, "The Christ of God."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Peter's confession "Ton Christon tou Theou" (Τὸν Χριστὸν τοῦ Θεοῦ) — "The Christ of God" — is the Lukan form of the great confession (cf. Matt 16:16; Mark 8:29). "Christos" (G5547) translates Hebrew "Mashiach" — the Anointed One, the promised Davidic King. The genitive "tou Theou" marks Jesus as God's own Anointed, the one set apart and commissioned by God himself for the eschatological rule.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: saying, "It is necessary for the Son of Man to suffer many things and to be rejected by the elders and chief priests and scribes, and to be killed, and on the third day to be raised."
verses.note: GREEK: "Dei" (δεῖ, G1163) expresses divine necessity — "it is necessary" according to the determined plan of God. This is not mere fate but the fulfillment of prophetic Scripture. TYPOLOGY: The suffering, rejection, and rising of the Son of Man fulfills the pattern of the Suffering Servant (Isa 53) and the righteous sufferer of the Psalms (Ps 22; 118:22). The "third day" echoes Hosea 6:2.

verses.flow: And he was saying to all, "If anyone wills to come after me, let him deny himself and take up his cross daily and follow me.
verses.note: GREEK: "Arnatō heauton" (ἀρνησάσθω ἑαυτόν) — "let him deny himself" — is an aorist imperative indicating a decisive, complete act. "Kath' hēmeran" (καθ' ἡμέραν) — "daily" — is unique to Luke's account, emphasizing the continuous, repeated nature of cross-bearing discipleship. THEOLOGICAL: The call to take up one's cross was shocking — the cross was Rome's instrument of shameful execution. Jesus redefines messianic discipleship not as triumphalist power but as self-renouncing suffering.

verses.flow: For whoever wills to save his soul shall lose it; but whoever loses his soul for my sake, this one shall save it.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For what is a man profited, having gained the whole world, but having lost or forfeited himself?
For whoever is ashamed of me and of my words, of this one the Son of Man shall be ashamed, when he comes in his glory and the glory of the Father and of the holy angels.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: Jesus speaks of coming "in his glory and the glory of the Father and of the holy angels" (ἐν τῇ δόξῃ αὐτοῦ καὶ τοῦ πατρὸς καὶ τῶν ἁγίων ἀγγέλων). This is a divine council scene: the Son of Man arrives as cosmic judge amid the heavenly host. The imagery draws on Daniel 7:13-14 where the Son of Man approaches the Ancient of Days and receives dominion, accompanied by the angelic myriads. Jesus claims participation in the Father's own glory — a claim to divine identity.

words.m: verb perfect active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: But I say to you truly, there are some of those standing here who shall by no means taste of death until they see the kingdom of God."

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Now it came to pass, about eight days after these words, that having taken along Peter and John and James, he went up onto the mountain to pray.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And it came to pass, while he was praying, the appearance of his face became different, and his clothing became white, flashing like lightning.
And behold, two men were conversing with him, who were Moses and Elijah,

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: who, having appeared in glory, were speaking of his exodus, which he was about to fulfill in Jerusalem.
verses.note: GREEK: "Exodon" (ἔξοδον, G1841) — "exodus" or "departure" — is a deeply loaded term. Luke deliberately uses this word rather than "thanaton" (death). TYPOLOGY: Moses and Elijah discuss Jesus' "exodus" which he is about to "fulfill" (plēroun) in Jerusalem. This connects Jesus' death, resurrection, and ascension to the original Exodus — the great redemptive act of God delivering his people. Jesus accomplishes the new and greater Exodus, liberating humanity from the bondage of sin and death. Moses represents the Law, Elijah the Prophets — both witness to Christ.

verses.flow: Now Peter and those with him were weighed down with sleep; but having become fully awake, they saw his glory and the two men standing with him.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And it came to pass, as they were departing from him, Peter said to Jesus, "Master, it is good for us to be here, and let us make three tabernacles: one for you, and one for Moses, and one for Elijah" — not knowing what he was saying.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: And as he was saying these things, a cloud came and was overshadowing them; and they were afraid as they entered into the cloud.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular feminine
verb perfect passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And a voice came out of the cloud, saying, "This is my Son, the Chosen One; hear him!"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The divine voice from the cloud echoes the baptism (Luke 3:22) but adds "ho eklelegmenos" (ὁ ἐκλελεγμένος) — "the Chosen One" — a title drawing on Isaiah 42:1 (LXX). This identifies Jesus as the Servant of the Lord. The command "autou akouete" (αὐτοῦ ἀκούετε) — "hear him!" — echoes Deuteronomy 18:15, the promise of the Prophet like Moses. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The cloud (nephélē) represents the divine presence, the same glory-cloud (Shekinah) that filled the tabernacle and temple (Exod 40:34-35; 1 Kgs 8:10-11). God himself speaks from within it, identifying Jesus as his unique Son.

verses.flow: And when the voice had come, Jesus was found alone. And they kept silent and reported to no one in those days anything of what they had seen.

words.m: verb aorist active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: Now it came to pass on the next day, when they had come down from the mountain, a great crowd met him.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And behold, a man from the crowd cried out, saying, "Teacher, I beg you, look upon my son, for he is my only child.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: And behold, a spirit seizes him, and suddenly he cries out, and it convulses him with foaming, and with difficulty it departs from him, bruising him.
And I begged your disciples that they might cast it out, and they were not able."

verses.flow: And Jesus, answering, said, "O faithless and perverted generation, how long shall I be with you and bear with you? Bring your son here."

verses.flow: And while he was still approaching, the demon threw him down and convulsed him violently. But Jesus rebuked the unclean spirit and healed the boy and gave him back to his father.

verses.flow: And all were astonished at the majesty of God. But while all were marveling at all things which he was doing, he said to his disciples,
"Put these words into your ears: for the Son of Man is about to be delivered into the hands of men."
verses.note: GREEK: "Thesthe hymeis eis ta ōta hymōn" (Θέσθε ὑμεῖς εἰς τὰ ὦτα ὑμῶν) — literally "Put you into your ears" — is an emphatic Semitic idiom meaning "let these words sink deeply in." The pronoun "hymeis" is emphatic, stressing the disciples specifically. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus issues this solemn charge amid the crowd's amazement at his power, sharply contrasting divine majesty with the coming betrayal — "paradidosthai" (παραδίδοσθαι, G3860), to be handed over, a term with both judicial and covenantal treachery connotations.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: But they did not understand this saying, and it was hidden from them, so that they might not perceive it; and they were afraid to ask him about this saying.
Now a dispute entered among them, as to which of them might be the greatest.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But Jesus, knowing the reasoning of their heart, having taken hold of a little child, set him beside himself,

words.m: verb aorist active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: and said to them, "Whoever receives this little child in my name receives me; and whoever receives me receives the one who sent me. For he who is least among you all, this one is great."

verses.flow: And John, answering, said, "Master, we saw someone casting out demons in your name, and we were forbidding him, because he does not follow with us."
But Jesus said to him, "Do not forbid him; for he who is not against you is for you."
Now it came to pass, when the days of his being taken up were being fulfilled, that he set his face steadfastly to go to Jerusalem.
verses.note: GREEK: "Analēmpseōs" (ἀναλήμψεως, G354) — a hapax legomenon in the NT — refers to Jesus' "being taken up," denoting his ascension. STRUCTURE: This verse marks the pivotal turning point of Luke's Gospel — the beginning of the great "Travel Narrative" or "Journey to Jerusalem" (9:51–19:44). Jesus "set his face" (to prosōpon estērisen) recalls Isaiah 50:7 (LXX), the Servant's resolve in suffering. THEOLOGICAL: The entire complex of suffering, death, resurrection, and ascension is encompassed in this single word "analēmpsis," showing Luke views these as one unified redemptive event.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And he sent messengers before his face. And having gone, they entered into a village of the Samaritans, so as to make ready for him.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: And they did not receive him, because his face was set toward going to Jerusalem.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And the disciples James and John, having seen this, said, "Lord, do you will that we command fire to come down from heaven and consume them?"
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: James and John allude to Elijah calling fire from heaven upon the soldiers of Ahaziah (2 Kgs 1:10-12). The disciples misunderstand the nature of Jesus' messianic mission — his journey to Jerusalem is not one of fiery judgment upon rejecting peoples but of self-sacrifice. HISTORICAL: Samaritan-Jewish hostility was longstanding (cf. Ezra 4; John 4:9). The Samaritans' refusal was rooted in the religious rivalry over the proper place of worship — Jerusalem versus Mount Gerizim.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But having turned, he rebuked them.
And they went on to another village.

words.m: verb present middle participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: And as they were going on the road, someone said to him, "I will follow you wherever you go."
And Jesus said to him, "The foxes have dens, and the birds of the heaven have nests, but the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head" reveals the radical homelessness of the incarnate King. TYPOLOGY: The foxes have dens and the birds have nests — even the creatures of the earth have dwelling places, yet the one through whom all things were made (John 1:3) has no earthly home. This inverts all expectations of messianic grandeur and royal comfort.

words.m: verb aorist active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: And he said to another, "Follow me." But he said, "Lord, permit me first, having gone away, to bury my father."

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But he said to him, "Let the dead bury their own dead; but you, having gone forth, proclaim the kingdom of God."
And yet another also said, "I will follow you, Lord; but first permit me to bid farewell to those in my house."

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But Jesus said, "No one having put his hand upon the plough and looking back to the things behind is fit for the kingdom of God."
verses.note: GREEK: "Arotron" (ἄροτρον, G723) — "plough" — appears only here in the NT. The image of ploughing requires looking forward to cut straight furrows; looking back produces crooked, useless work. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus demands undivided commitment to the kingdom of God. The saying echoes Elijah's call of Elisha (1 Kgs 19:19-21), where Elisha was permitted to say farewell — yet Jesus demands even greater urgency. The kingdom of God surpasses all prior prophetic calls in its radical demand for wholehearted allegiance.

verses.flow: And after these things, the Lord appointed also seventy-two others, and sent them two by two before his face into every city and place where he himself was about to come.
verses.note: GREEK: ἀνέδειξεν (anedeixen, from anadeiknymi) means "appointed" or "publicly designated" — a formal commissioning. The number seventy-two (variant: seventy) likely echoes the seventy elders of Israel (Num 11:16-25) and the seventy nations of Genesis 10 in the LXX tradition. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The seventy nations tradition from the Table of Nations (Gen 10; Deut 32:8-9 LXX) assigns seventy(-two) nations to the sons of God; the sending of seventy(-two) missionaries may symbolize the gospel's reach to all nations under the authority of the true Lord.

verses.flow: And he was saying to them, "The harvest indeed is plentiful, but the workers are few; beseech therefore the Lord of the harvest that he might send out workers into his harvest."
verses.note: GREEK: θερισμός (therismos, "harvest") carries eschatological weight, pointing to the final ingathering of God's people. ἐκβάλῃ (ekbalē, from ekballō) is strikingly forceful — literally "cast out" or "thrust out" — suggesting urgency and divine initiative in sending workers. TYPOLOGY: The harvest imagery echoes Joel 3:13 and anticipates the eschatological gathering under the Messiah's reign.

verses.flow: Go! Behold, I send you as lambs in the midst of wolves.
verses.note: GREEK: ἄρνας (arnas, "lambs") is used only here in the NT in this sending context (Matthew 10:16 uses πρόβατα, "sheep"). The image is deliberately more vulnerable than sheep — these are lambs among wolves, emphasizing total dependence on the Sender. THEOLOGICAL: The Messiah sends his emissaries into hostile territory not with worldly power but under divine protection.

verses.flow: Carry no purse, no bag, no sandals, and greet no one along the way.
And into whatever house you enter, first say, "Peace to this house."
And if a son of peace be there, your peace shall rest upon him; but if not, it shall return upon you.
verses.note: GREEK: υἱὸς εἰρήνης (huios eirēnēs, "son of peace") is a Semitic idiom meaning one characterized by or destined for peace — one receptive to the shalom of God's kingdom. ἐπαναπαήσεται (epanapaēsetai, "shall rest upon") echoes the Spirit resting upon persons in the OT (Num 11:25-26; Isa 11:2).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And in that same house remain, eating and drinking the things from them, for the worker is worthy of his wages. Do not move from house to house.

words.m: verb present passive participle accusative plural neuter

verses.flow: And into whatever city you enter and they receive you, eat the things set before you,
and heal the sick in it, and say to them, "The kingdom of God has drawn near to you."

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: But into whatever city you enter and they do not receive you, going out into its streets say,

words.m: verb aorist passive participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: "Even the dust from your city that clings to our feet we wipe off against you. Nevertheless, know this: that the kingdom of God has drawn near."
I say to you that for Sodom it shall be more tolerable in that day than for that city.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The pronouncement that Sodom's judgment will be more tolerable than that of cities rejecting the seventy-two implies a graduated judgment based on revelation received. Those who reject the direct heralds of the Messiah bear greater culpability than Sodom, which never witnessed such works. TYPOLOGY: This escalation pattern echoes Ezekiel 16:48-50 where Sodom is used as a comparative standard of judgment.

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative plural feminine
verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Woe to you, Chorazin! Woe to you, Bethsaida! For if the mighty works that were done in you had been done in Tyre and Sidon, they would have repented long ago, sitting in sackcloth and ashes.
But for Tyre and Sidon it shall be more tolerable in the judgment than for you.
And you, Capernaum, shall you be exalted unto heaven? Unto Hades you shall be brought down!
verses.note: GREEK: The language echoes Isaiah 14:13-15 (LXX), the taunt against the king of Babylon who sought to ascend to heaven but was brought down to Sheol/Hades. TYPOLOGY: Capernaum, exalted by the presence and ministry of the Messiah, faces the severest judgment for its rejection — mirroring the cosmic pride-and-fall pattern of Isaiah 14. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The Isaiah 14 background invokes the divine council setting where the arrogant one who would place his throne among the stars of God is cast down.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: The one hearing you hears me, and the one rejecting you rejects me; and the one rejecting me rejects the one who sent me.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse establishes an unbroken chain of delegated authority: to reject the seventy-two is to reject Christ, and to reject Christ is to reject the Father who sent him. This reflects the šāliaḥ ("sent one") principle in Jewish law — an agent bears the full authority of the one who sends him. The Messiah here claims unique agency from the Father, extending it through his appointed emissaries.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And the seventy-two returned with joy, saying, "Lord, even the demons are subject to us in your name!"

words.m: verb aorist active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: And he said to them, "I was beholding Satan having fallen as lightning from heaven.
verses.note: GREEK: ἐθεώρουν (etheōroun, imperfect tense of theōreō) — "I was beholding" — suggests a visionary or continuous observation, not a single past event. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Satan's fall "as lightning from heaven" resonates with the divine council motif of rebellious heavenly beings being cast down (Isa 14:12; Ezek 28:16-17; Rev 12:7-9). The exorcisms performed by the seventy-two are earthly manifestations of a heavenly defeat already in progress through Christ's authority.

verses.flow: Behold, I have given to you the authority to tread upon serpents and scorpions, and over all the power of the enemy, and nothing shall by any means harm you.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The authority to tread upon serpents and scorpions echoes Genesis 3:15 (the protoevangelium — the seed of the woman crushing the serpent's head) and Psalm 91:13. The "power of the enemy" (τὴν δύναμιν τοῦ ἐχθροῦ) identifies Satan and his hosts as the ultimate adversary. THEOLOGICAL: Christ grants to his emissaries participation in his own messianic victory over the hostile spiritual powers.

verses.flow: Nevertheless, do not rejoice in this, that the spirits are subject to you, but rejoice that your names have been written in the heavens."
verses.note: GREEK: ἐγγέγραπται (engegraptai, perfect passive of engraphō) — "have been written and remain written" — the perfect tense emphasizes the permanent, completed nature of the heavenly inscription. THEOLOGICAL: Rejoicing should be rooted not in spiritual power exercised but in one's secure standing before God — names inscribed in the heavenly register (cf. Exod 32:32; Dan 12:1; Phil 4:3; Rev 20:12).

verses.flow: In that same hour he rejoiced in the Holy Spirit and said, "I praise you, Father, Lord of heaven and earth, that you hid these things from the wise and understanding and revealed them to infants. Yes, Father, for thus it was well-pleasing before you.
verses.note: GREEK: ἠγαλλιάσατο (ēgalliasato, from agalliaō) — "exulted" or "rejoiced greatly" — a rare description of Jesus's emotion, used in the LXX for ecstatic praise (Ps 16:9). THEOLOGICAL: Jesus's prayer reveals the Father's sovereign election: divine mysteries are hidden from the humanly wise (σοφῶν καὶ συνετῶν) and revealed to νηπίοις ("infants," the unlearned and dependent). This is a Johannine-sounding passage within the Synoptic tradition, affirming the Father-Son relationship of mutual exclusive knowledge.

verses.flow: All things have been delivered to me by my Father, and no one knows who the Son is except the Father, and who the Father is except the Son, and anyone to whom the Son wills to reveal him."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse presents the most exalted Christological claim in the Synoptic Gospels. The mutual exclusive knowledge between Father and Son (cf. John 10:15; Matt 11:27) places the Son in a unique ontological relationship with the Father — no creature fully knows God except through the Son's sovereign revelation. The Son possesses the divine prerogative of revelation (ἀποκαλύψαι). DIVINE_COUNCIL: Unlike any angelic being in the divine council, the Son alone possesses exhaustive knowledge of the Father, establishing his supreme status above all heavenly beings.

verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And turning to the disciples privately he said, "Blessed are the eyes that see what you see.
For I say to you that many prophets and kings desired to see what you see and did not see it, and to hear what you hear and did not hear it."

verses.flow: And behold, a certain lawyer stood up, testing him, saying, "Teacher, what having done shall I inherit eternal life?"
And he said to him, "What has been written in the Law? How do you read?"

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And he answering said, "You shall love the Lord your God out of all your heart and with all your soul and with all your strength and with all your mind, and your neighbor as yourself."
verses.note: GREEK: The lawyer quotes Deuteronomy 6:5 (the Shema) combined with Leviticus 19:18. The fourfold division — καρδίας (heart), ψυχῇ (soul), ἰσχύϊ (strength), διανοίᾳ (mind) — expands the Hebrew original's threefold formula, representing the totality of human devotion. THEOLOGICAL: The entire Law is summarized in vertical love toward God and horizontal love toward neighbor — the two tablets of the covenant (cf. Rom 13:8-10).

verses.flow: And he said to him, "You have answered rightly; do this and you shall live."

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But he, desiring to justify himself, said to Jesus, "And who is my neighbor?"

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And Jesus, taking up the word, said, "A certain man was going down from Jerusalem to Jericho, and fell among robbers, who both stripped him and inflicted wounds, and departed, leaving him half-dead.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The road from Jerusalem to Jericho descended approximately 3,400 feet over 17 miles through the Judean wilderness — notoriously dangerous terrain called the "Ascent of Blood" (Adummim) due to frequent robberies. GREEK: ἡμιθανῆ (hēmithanē) — "half-dead" — is a NT hapax legomenon, emphasizing the extreme severity of the assault.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

words.g: pass by on the other side

verses.flow: And by coincidence a certain priest was going down on that road, and seeing him, passed by on the other side.

verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And likewise also a Levite, coming upon the place and seeing, passed by on the other side.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But a certain Samaritan, journeying, came upon him, and seeing him, was moved with compassion.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Samaritans were despised by Jews as ethnically mixed and religiously heretical (cf. 2 Kings 17:24-41; John 4:9). Jesus's choice of a Samaritan as the hero would have been shocking and deeply offensive to his Jewish audience. GREEK: ἐσπλαγχνίσθη (esplanchnisthē, from splanchnizomai) — "was moved in his inward parts" — denotes the deepest visceral compassion, used in the Gospels almost exclusively of Jesus or characters representing divine mercy.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And approaching, he bound up his wounds, pouring on oil and wine; and setting him upon his own beast, he brought him to an inn and took care of him.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And on the next day, taking out two denarii, he gave them to the innkeeper and said, "Take care of him, and whatever more you spend, I, upon my returning, will repay to you."

words.m: verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: "Which of these three do you think became a neighbor to the one having fallen among the robbers?"

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And he said, "The one who showed mercy to him." And Jesus said to him, "Go, and you do likewise."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus inverts the lawyer's question. The lawyer asked "Who is my neighbor?" seeking to limit the obligation. Jesus asks "Who became a neighbor?" — transforming the question from one of boundary-drawing to one of active mercy. The command "Go and do likewise" makes neighbor-love an unbounded, self-giving action rather than a category to be defined. TYPOLOGY: Some patristic interpreters saw the Good Samaritan as a figure of Christ himself — the rejected one who alone shows mercy to fallen humanity, binds wounds, and promises to return.

verses.flow: And as they were journeying, he entered into a certain village; and a certain woman named Martha received him.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular feminine
verb aorist passive participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: And she had a sister called Mary, who also, having sat down at the feet of the Lord, was hearing his word.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: But Martha was distracted with much serving, and standing near, she said, "Lord, does it not concern you that my sister has left me alone to serve? Tell her therefore that she should help me."

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But the Lord answering said to her, "Martha, Martha, you are anxious and troubled about many things,
but few things are needful, or one. For Mary has chosen the good portion, which shall not be taken away from her."
verses.note: GREEK: The textual tradition varies between "few things are needful, or one" (ὀλίγων δέ ἐστιν χρεία ἢ ἑνός) and simpler readings. The "one thing needful" (ἑνός) points to the singular priority of attending to Christ's word. μερίδα (merida, "portion") carries covenantal overtones — one's allotted inheritance (cf. Ps 16:5; 73:26). THEOLOGICAL: Mary's posture at the Lord's feet — the posture of a disciple — is declared the "good portion" that cannot be taken away, establishing the primacy of receiving Christ's word over even legitimate service.

words.m: verb present middle participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: And it came to pass, while he was in a certain place praying, as he ceased, one of his disciples said to him, "Lord, teach us to pray, just as John also taught his disciples."
verses.note: GREEK: The construction Καὶ ἐγένετο ἐν τῷ εἶναι αὐτόν (kaì egéneto en tō̧ eînai autón) is a Septuagintal Hebraism reflecting the Hebrew wayĕhî construction, common in Luke's narrative style. The verb προσεύχομαι (proseúchomai, Strong's G4336) indicates earnest, devoted prayer. HISTORICAL: Luke uniquely records that a disciple's request prompted the Lord's Prayer, whereas Matthew places it within the Sermon on the Mount (Matt 6:9-13). John the Baptist evidently had a distinctive prayer tradition for his disciples.

verses.flow: And he said to them, "When you pray, say: Father, let your name be hallowed; let your kingdom come;
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Luke's version of the Lord's Prayer is shorter than Matthew's (Matt 6:9-13), omitting "who art in heaven," "thy will be done," and the doxology. The aorist imperative ἁγιασθήτω (hagiasthḗtō) is a divine passive — God himself is asked to cause his name to be hallowed. The petition for the kingdom's coming reflects the eschatological hope central to Jesus's proclamation. TYPOLOGY: The address "Father" (Πάτερ) without further qualification reflects the intimate divine-filial relationship Jesus models, distinct from typical Jewish liturgical address.

verses.flow: our daily bread give to us each day;
verses.note: GREEK: The word ἐπιούσιον (epioúsion, Strong's G1967) is one of the most debated terms in the NT, a near-hapax legomenon found only here and in Matt 6:11. Its etymology is uncertain — possibly from ἐπί + οὐσία ("for subsistence/existence"), from ἐπί + ἰέναι ("for the coming day"), or from ἐπί + εἶναι ("sufficient for being"). Origen noted it was not found in ordinary Greek usage. The present imperative δίδου (dídou) suggests continuous, daily giving.
