verses.flow: and forgive us our sins, for we ourselves also forgive everyone who is indebted to us; and lead us not into temptation."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Luke uses ἁμαρτίας (hamartías, "sins") where Matthew has ὀφειλήματα ("debts," Matt 6:12), though Luke retains the debt metaphor with ὀφείλοντι (opheílonti, "everyone indebted to us"). The petition against πειρασμός (peirasmós, Strong's G3986) may denote either "temptation" or "testing/trial," with overtones of the eschatological trial. TYPOLOGY: The forgiveness petition echoes the Day of Atonement pattern — divine forgiveness enabling communal reconciliation (Lev 16).

verses.flow: I say to you, even if he will not give to him, having risen, because of his being his friend, yet because of his shameless persistence he will rise and give to him as many things as he needs.
verses.note: GREEK: The key term ἀναίδειαν (anaídeian, Strong's G335) is a hapax legomenon in the NT, meaning "shamelessness" or "shameless persistence." Some scholars render it "avoidance of shame" (i.e., the sleeper gives in to avoid the shame of refusing hospitality). HISTORICAL: In the honor-shame culture of first-century Palestine, refusing hospitality — especially to a traveler — brought communal disgrace. The parable argues from lesser to greater: if even a reluctant human responds to persistent asking, how much more God.

verses.flow: And I say to you: Ask, and it shall be given to you; seek, and you shall find; knock, and it shall be opened to you.

verses.flow: If you then, being evil, know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more shall the Father who is from heaven give the Holy Spirit to those who ask him!
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This is a critically significant Lukan variation from Matthew's parallel (Matt 7:11), where Matthew has "good things" (ἀγαθά) but Luke specifies πνεῦμα ἅγιον (pneûma hágion, "Holy Spirit"). This reflects Luke's distinctive pneumatology — the Holy Spirit as the supreme gift of the Father. The argument moves from lesser to greater (qal wahomer): if evil humans give good gifts, how much more the heavenly Father gives the Spirit. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The Father "who is from heaven" (ὁ ἐξ οὐρανοῦ) locates God in his heavenly council seat as the supreme giver.

verses.flow: And he was casting out a demon, and it was mute. And it came to pass, when the demon had gone out, the mute man spoke, and the crowds marveled.
But some of them said, "By Beelzebul, the ruler of the demons, he casts out the demons."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Βεελζεβούλ (Beelzeboúl, Strong's G954) derives from the Philistine deity Baal-Zebub ("Lord of the Flies," 2 Kgs 1:2), reinterpreted in Second Temple Judaism as a title for Satan or the chief of demons. The accusation implies Jesus operates under demonic authority rather than divine. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The charge reflects an understanding of a hierarchical realm of hostile spiritual powers — a "ruler" (ἄρχοντι) presiding over lesser demons — which Jesus does not deny as a cosmological structure but refutes as applied to himself.

verses.flow: And others, testing him, were seeking from him a sign from heaven.

verses.flow: But he, knowing their thoughts, said to them, "Every kingdom divided against itself is brought to desolation, and house falls upon house.
And if Satan also is divided against himself, how shall his kingdom stand? For you say that I cast out the demons by Beelzebul.
But if I by Beelzebul cast out the demons, by whom do your sons cast them out? Therefore they shall be your judges.
But if by the finger of God I cast out the demons, then the kingdom of God has come upon you.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "finger of God" (δακτύλῳ θεοῦ) is a direct allusion to Exod 8:19 (LXX 8:15), where Pharaoh's magicians recognize God's power in Moses's signs. Matthew's parallel (Matt 12:28) has "Spirit of God," suggesting Luke preserves the more archaic Exodus typology. The verb ἔφθασεν (éphthasen, "has come upon") is aorist, indicating the kingdom's arrival is not merely future but has already broken in through Jesus's exorcisms. TYPOLOGY: Jesus's exorcisms recapitulate the Exodus liberation — as God's finger defeated Egypt's powers, so now it defeats demonic powers. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "finger of God" implies direct divine-council authority exercised through Jesus, bypassing any intermediary spiritual hierarchy.

verses.flow: But when one stronger than he, having come upon him, overcomes him, he takes away his full armor upon which he had trusted, and distributes his spoils.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "stronger one" (ἰσχυρότερος) who overcomes the "strong man" (ἰσχυρός) is Christ himself overcoming Satan. The term πανοπλίαν (panoplían, Strong's G3833, "full armor") is used elsewhere only in Eph 6:11,13 for the believer's spiritual armor. Jesus strips Satan of his defensive and offensive capacity. DIVINE_COUNCIL: This passage presents a cosmic conflict theology — two rival powers contending, with Christ as the divinely authorized warrior who plunders the enemy's domain (cf. Isa 49:24-25, Isa 53:12).

verses.flow: But he said, "Rather, blessed are those who hear the word of God and keep it."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus redirects honor from biological motherhood to obedient hearing and keeping (φυλάσσοντες, phylássontes, Strong's G5442) of God's word. This is not a rejection of Mary but a reorientation of blessedness toward covenant faithfulness. The term μενοῦν (menoûn) is an emphatic particle meaning "rather" or "on the contrary indeed," making this a strong correction. TYPOLOGY: This echoes the Deuteronomic pattern where blessing follows obedience to the divine word (Deut 28:1-2).

verses.flow: And as the crowds were gathering thickly together, he began to say, "This generation is an evil generation; it seeks a sign, and no sign shall be given to it except the sign of Jonah.
For just as Jonah became a sign to the Ninevites, so also shall the Son of Man be to this generation.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Jonah's three days in the great fish prefigure Christ's death and resurrection (Matt 12:40 makes this explicit). But Luke's emphasis is on Jonah as a sign-figure to the Ninevites — as Jonah himself, having emerged from apparent death, was a sign calling Nineveh to repentance, so the Son of Man in his person and proclamation is a sign to this generation. The "Son of Man" title here carries both Danielic authority (Dan 7:13-14) and the suffering-servant overtone.

verses.flow: The queen of the south shall be raised up in the judgment with the men of this generation and shall condemn them, for she came from the ends of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon; and behold, something greater than Solomon is here.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "queen of the south" is the Queen of Sheba (1 Kgs 10:1-13; 2 Chr 9:1-12), who traveled from the Arabian Peninsula to hear Solomon's wisdom. THEOLOGICAL: The neuter πλεῖον (pleîon, "something greater") rather than masculine suggests Jesus points not merely to himself as greater than Solomon but to the entire reality of the kingdom breaking in — the wisdom, power, and presence of God manifested in his ministry surpasses all prior revelations.

verses.flow: The men of Nineveh shall stand up in the judgment with this generation and shall condemn it, for they repented at the preaching of Jonah; and behold, something greater than Jonah is here.

verses.flow: No one, having lit a lamp, places it in a hidden place, nor under the measuring basket, but upon the lampstand, so that those entering may see the light.
The lamp of the body is your eye. When your eye is single, your whole body also is full of light; but when it is evil, your body also is dark.
verses.note: GREEK: The term ἁπλοῦς (haploûs, Strong's G573) means "single, simple, sound, healthy" — denoting undivided focus or generosity (the opposite of a "double" or "evil" eye). In Jewish idiom, the "good eye" signifies generosity and the "evil eye" (πονηρός, ponērós) signifies stinginess or moral corruption. THEOLOGICAL: The eye as lamp of the body serves as a metaphor for spiritual perception — one's capacity to receive divine light depends on the moral condition of one's inner orientation.

verses.flow: Watch, therefore, lest the light that is in you be darkness.

verses.flow: And as he was speaking, a Pharisee asked him to dine with him; and having entered, he reclined at table.

verses.flow: But the Pharisee, having seen it, marveled that he had not first washed before the meal.
And the Lord said to him, "Now you Pharisees cleanse the outside of the cup and the dish, but your inside is full of plunder and wickedness.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Foolish ones! Did not the one who made the outside make the inside also?

verses.flow: But give as alms the things that are within, and behold, all things are clean to you.
verses.note: GREEK: The phrase τὰ ἐνόντα (tà enónta, "the things within") is ambiguous — it could mean "give the contents [of the cup and dish] as alms" or "give from your inner disposition." THEOLOGICAL: Jesus redirects from external ritual purity to inward transformation expressed in concrete mercy (ἐλεημοσύνη, eleēmosýnē, "alms/mercy"). True cleanliness comes not from ceremonial washing but from generous hearts that reflect God's own character.

verses.flow: But woe to you Pharisees! For you tithe the mint and the rue and every herb, and pass by the justice and the love of God. But these things it was necessary to do, and those not to leave aside.
Woe to you Pharisees! For you love the chief seat in the synagogues and the greetings in the marketplaces.

verses.flow: Woe to you! For you are as the unmarked graves, and the men walking over them do not know it.

verses.flow: And one of the lawyers, answering, says to him, "Teacher, in saying these things you insult us also."
And he said, "Woe also to you lawyers! For you load men with burdens hard to bear, and you yourselves do not touch the burdens with one of your fingers.
Woe to you! For you build the tombs of the prophets, and your fathers killed them.
Therefore you are witnesses and you consent to the deeds of your fathers; for they indeed killed them, and you build their tombs.
Because of this also the Wisdom of God said, "I will send to them prophets and apostles, and some of them they will kill and persecute,"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The Wisdom of God said" (ἡ σοφία τοῦ θεοῦ εἶπεν) is a striking formula with no exact OT parallel. It may refer to a lost Jewish wisdom text, to God speaking in his wisdom, or — most significantly — to Jesus identifying himself as the personified Wisdom of God (cf. Prov 8; 1 Cor 1:24,30). DIVINE_COUNCIL: The sending of prophets and apostles reflects divine council commissioning language — God dispatches his authorized messengers into the human realm, and their rejection constitutes rebellion against the heavenly court itself.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: so that the blood of all the prophets, which has been poured out from the foundation of the world, may be required of this generation,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase ἀπὸ καταβολῆς κόσμου (apò katabolȩ̄s kósmou, "from the foundation of the world") encompasses the entire scope of salvation history. The blood of the prophets functions as a covenant-lawsuit witness against the generation that completes the pattern of rejection. TYPOLOGY: Abel (Gen 4:8-10) represents the first righteous martyr and Zechariah (2 Chr 24:20-22) the last in the Hebrew canonical order, thus spanning the entire OT witness. The "requiring" of blood echoes Gen 9:5 where God demands an accounting for shed blood.

words.m: verb aorist middle participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: from the blood of Abel to the blood of Zechariah, who perished between the altar and the house. Yes, I say to you, it shall be required of this generation.

words.m: verb present middle participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: Woe to you lawyers! For you have taken away the key of knowledge; you yourselves did not enter, and those who were entering you hindered.
verses.note: GREEK: The term κλεῖδα (kleîda, Strong's G2807) means "key" — the instrument of access. The "key of knowledge" (τὴν κλεῖδα τῆς γνώσεως) signifies authoritative access to understanding God's revelation. THEOLOGICAL: The lawyers, entrusted with interpreting Torah, have not only failed to enter the kingdom themselves but have actively blocked others from entering through their distorted teachings. This parallels Jesus's words to Peter about the "keys of the kingdom" (Matt 16:19) — true spiritual authority opens rather than closes access to God.

verses.flow: In which time, the ten-thousands of the crowd having gathered together, so as to trample one another, he began to say to his disciples first, "Guard yourselves from the leaven, which is the hypocrisy of the Pharisees.
verses.note: GREEK: ἐπισυναχθεισῶν (episynachtheisōn) — a genitive absolute from ἐπισυνάγω (Strong's G1996), meaning "to gather together upon," implying an overwhelming convergence. The μυριάδων (myriadōn) literally means "ten-thousands," indicating a crowd of enormous, perhaps innumerable, size. THEOLOGICAL: The leaven (ζύμη, zymē) metaphor connects to the Passover command to purge leaven (Exod 12:15), here reinterpreted as hypocrisy (ὑπόκρισις, hypokrisis, originally a theatrical term for playing a role on stage).

verses.flow: And I say to you, my friends, do not fear those who kill the body, and after these things have nothing more to do.

verses.flow: But I shall show you whom you should fear: fear the One who, after killing, has authority to cast into Gehenna. Yes, I say to you, fear this One.
verses.note: GREEK: γέενναν (geennan, Strong's G1067) derives from Hebrew גֵּי הִנֹּם (Ge-Hinnom), the Valley of Hinnom south of Jerusalem, associated with child sacrifice to Molech (2 Kgs 23:10; Jer 7:31) and later Jewish eschatological imagery of final judgment. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus distinguishes between those who can merely kill the body and the One (God) who possesses ἐξουσίαν (exousian, "authority") over eternal destiny. This is a definitive statement of God's sovereign authority over both temporal and eternal judgment.

verses.flow: Are not five sparrows sold for two assaria? And not one of them is forgotten before God.
But even the hairs of your head have all been numbered. Do not fear; you are of more value than many sparrows.
And I say to you, everyone who confesses in me before men, the Son of Man also shall confess in him before the angels of God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "confess in me" (ὁμολογήσῃ ἐν ἐμοί) uses ἐν + dative rather than a simple accusative, suggesting not merely acknowledging Jesus but identifying with him, being "in" union with him before men. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The confession occurs "before the angels of God" (ἔμπροσθεν τῶν ἀγγέλων τοῦ θεοῦ), indicating a heavenly courtroom scene where the Son of Man serves as advocate or witness before the divine assembly (cf. Dan 7:13-14; Job 1:6; Ps 82:1).

verses.flow: But the one who denied me before men shall be denied before the angels of God.

verses.flow: And everyone who shall speak a word against the Son of Man, it shall be forgiven him; but to the one who blasphemes against the Holy Spirit, it shall not be forgiven.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse distinguishes blasphemy against the Son of Man (which is forgivable) from blasphemy against the Holy Spirit (τὸ ἅγιον πνεῦμα), which is not. The unforgivable nature points to a persistent, willful rejection of the Spirit's testimony concerning Christ, not a single utterance. This parallels Matt 12:31-32 and Mark 3:28-29. GREEK: βλασφημήσαντι (blasphēmēsanti) is an aorist participle, but the theological context indicates a settled disposition rather than a momentary act.

verses.flow: And when they bring you before the synagogues and the rulers and the authorities, do not be anxious how or what you shall answer in your defense, or what you shall say,

verses.flow: And I shall say to my soul, 'Soul, you have many goods laid up for many years; take your rest, eat, drink, be merry.''

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: God's direct speech to the rich man — ἄφρων (aphrōn, "fool," Strong's G878) — echoes the LXX of Psalm 13:1 (MT 14:1): "The fool said in his heart, there is no God." The demand of the soul (ψυχήν σου ἀπαιτοῦσιν) uses the third person plural, possibly an impersonal construction or suggesting angelic agents of death. verses.flow: And if God so clothes the grass in the field, being here today and tomorrow being cast into the oven, how much more shall he clothe you, O you of little faith!
And you, do not seek what you shall eat and what you shall drink, and do not be of anxious mind.
For all these things the nations of the world seek after; but your Father knows that you have need of these things.
But seek his kingdom, and these things shall be added to you.
Do not fear, little flock, for your Father was well pleased to give you the kingdom.
verses.note: GREEK: μικρὸν ποίμνιον (mikron poimnion) — "little flock" — is a diminutive, tenderly depicting the disciples as a small, vulnerable group. THEOLOGICAL: εὐδόκησεν (eudokēsen, "was well pleased," aorist tense) indicates that the Father's decision to give the kingdom was a resolved, completed act of sovereign good pleasure. The kingdom is a gift (δοῦναι, dounai), not an achievement. TYPOLOGY: The shepherd-flock imagery echoes Ezek 34:11-16, where God himself becomes the shepherd of his scattered flock, a role now mediated through Jesus (cf. John 10:11-16).

verses.flow: Blessed are those servants whom the lord, having come, shall find watching. Truly I say to you, that he shall gird himself and recline them at table, and having come alongside, he shall serve them.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The stunning reversal in which the lord girds himself (περιζώσεται) and serves (διακονήσει) his servants at table is without parallel in ancient master-slave relationships. This foreshadows Jesus washing the disciples' feet (John 13:4-5) and his declaration that he came "not to be served but to serve" (Mark 10:45). GREEK: ἀνακλινεῖ (anaklinei, "cause to recline") depicts the formal banquet posture, evoking the eschatological messianic banquet (Isa 25:6-8).

verses.flow: And if he comes in the second watch, or if in the third watch, and finds them so, blessed are those servants.

verses.flow: Truly I say to you, that he shall appoint him over all his possessions.
But if that servant should say in his heart, 'My lord delays in coming,' and should begin to beat the menservants and the maidservants, and to eat and drink and become drunk,
the lord of that servant shall come on a day when he does not expect, and at an hour he does not know, and shall cut him in two, and shall appoint his portion with the unfaithful.
verses.note: GREEK: διχοτομήσει (dichotomēsei, Strong's G1371) literally means "shall cut in two" — a hapax legomenon in the Gospels (also Matt 24:51). THEOLOGICAL: The pairing of cutting asunder with assigning a portion "with the unfaithful" (μετὰ τῶν ἀπίστων) indicates both judgment and permanent exclusion. The unexpectedness of the lord's return underscores the eschatological urgency of faithful stewardship.

verses.flow: And that servant who knew the will of his lord, and did not prepare or act according to his will, shall be beaten with many stripes.

verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But the one who did not know, yet did things worthy of stripes, shall be beaten with few. And to everyone to whom much was given, much shall be required of him; and to whom they entrusted much, of him they shall ask all the more.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse establishes a principle of proportional judgment: greater knowledge entails greater accountability. The distinction between the servant who knew and the one who did not know reveals graduated divine justice — God judges according to the light given. STRUCTURE: The concluding aphorism ("to whom much was given, much shall be required") forms a chiastic summary of the entire stewardship section (vv. 42-48), linking privilege to responsibility.

verses.flow: I came to cast fire upon the earth, and how I wish it were already kindled!
verses.note: GREEK: πῦρ (pyr, "fire") here is ambiguous — it may refer to the fire of judgment (Mal 3:2-3), the refining fire of the Spirit (Acts 2:3), or the fire of division that Christ's coming provokes. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus expresses an intense desire (τί θέλω, "how I wish") for this fire to be kindled, revealing his awareness of the eschatological crisis his mission inaugurates. This connects to John the Baptist's prophecy of one who baptizes "with the Holy Spirit and fire" (Luke 3:16).

verses.flow: But I have a baptism to be baptized with, and how I am constrained until it is accomplished!
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The βάπτισμα (baptisma, "baptism") Jesus must undergo is his suffering and death (cf. Mark 10:38-39). The verb συνέχομαι (synechomai, "I am constrained/pressed") reveals the intense inner pressure Jesus experienced as he moved toward the cross. TYPOLOGY: This baptism of death fulfills the pattern of passing through waters as judgment and deliverance — the Red Sea crossing (Exod 14), the Jordan crossing (Josh 3), and now the ultimate passage through death itself.

verses.flow: Do you suppose that I came to give peace on the earth? No, I say to you, but rather division.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For from now on there shall be five in one house divided, three against two and two against three.

They shall be divided: father against son and son against father, mother against daughter and daughter against mother, mother-in-law against her daughter-in-law and daughter-in-law against mother-in-law."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The list of divided family members echoes Micah 7:6, a prophetic text describing the corruption of Israel before God's vindication. Jesus' appropriation of this passage signals that his coming inaugurates the eschatological crisis Micah foresaw. THEOLOGICAL: Allegiance to Christ supersedes even the most fundamental human bonds, redefining kinship around faith rather than blood.

verses.flow: And he was saying also to the crowds, "When you see a cloud rising from the west, immediately you say, 'A shower is coming,' and so it happens.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: And when you see the south wind blowing, you say, 'There will be scorching heat,' and it happens.
Hypocrites! You know how to discern the face of the earth and of the sky, but how is it you do not know how to discern this present time?
verses.note: GREEK: ὑποκριταί (hypokritai) — the same root as ὑπόκρισις in v.1, forming an inclusio. The crowd can "discern" (δοκιμάζειν, dokimazein, Strong's G1381, meaning to test, prove, or examine) weather patterns but cannot discern the present καιρός (kairos) — the decisive, appointed time of God's action in Christ. THEOLOGICAL: The inability to read the signs of the times is a moral and spiritual failure, not merely an intellectual one.

verses.flow: And why do you not even of yourselves judge what is right?

For as you go with your adversary before a ruler, on the way give diligence to be released from him, lest he drag you before the judge, and the judge deliver you to the officer, and the officer cast you into prison.
I say to you, you shall by no means come out from there until you have paid back the very last lepton."
verses.note: GREEK: λεπτόν (lepton, Strong's G3016) was the smallest copper coin in circulation, worth roughly 1/128 of a denarius — the absolute minimum unit of currency. THEOLOGICAL: The parable of the adversary functions as a warning about eschatological judgment: settle accounts now, for divine justice is thorough and exacting. The imagery of imprisonment until the last coin is repaid underscores the completeness and inescapability of God's righteous judgment.

verses.flow: And there were present some at that very season, reporting to him concerning the Galileans whose blood Pilate had mingled with their sacrifices.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: This incident of Pilate mingling Galilean blood with their sacrifices is not recorded in Josephus or other extra-biblical sources, though it is consistent with Pilate's known brutality. The act of defiling worshippers' blood with their own sacrifices constituted an extreme sacrilege, violating the sanctity of the temple altar. This likely occurred during a festival pilgrimage when Galileans came to Jerusalem to offer sacrifice.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And answering, he said to them, "Do you suppose that these Galileans were sinners beyond all the Galileans, because they suffered such things?
No, I say to you, but unless you repent, you will all likewise perish.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus overturns the common Second Temple assumption that extraordinary suffering indicates extraordinary sin. The call to repentance (μετανοέω, metanoeō, Strong's G3340—a complete change of mind and orientation) is universal, not reserved for conspicuous sinners. The verb ἀπολεῖσθε (apoleisthe, "you will perish") carries both temporal and eschatological weight.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: Or those eighteen upon whom the tower in Siloam fell and killed them—do you suppose that they were debtors beyond all the men dwelling in Jerusalem?
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The tower in Siloam (Σιλωάμ) was located in the southeastern section of Jerusalem near the Pool of Siloam. This collapse is otherwise unattested in ancient sources. The term ὀφειλέται (opheiletai, "debtors," Strong's G3781) frames sin as a debt owed to God, consistent with Jewish theological vocabulary (cf. Matt 6:12).

verses.flow: No, I say to you, but unless you repent, you will all in like manner perish."

verses.flow: And he spoke this parable: "A certain man had a fig tree planted in his vineyard, and he came seeking fruit on it and found none.

verses.flow: And he said to the vinedresser, "Behold, three years I come seeking fruit on this fig tree and find none. Cut it down; why indeed does it render the ground useless?"
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The fig tree in the vineyard echoes Israel as God's planting (Isa 5:1-7; Jer 8:13; Hos 9:10). The three years likely correspond to the duration of Jesus' public ministry. The vinedresser's intercession prefigures Christ's own intercessory role on behalf of unfruitful Israel. The term ἀμπελουργόν (ampelourgon, "vinedresser," Strong's G289) is a compound of ἄμπελος (vine) and ἔργον (work)—a NT hapax legomenon.

verses.flow: And behold, a woman having a spirit of infirmity eighteen years, and she was bent together and not able to straighten herself up completely.
verses.note: GREEK: The phrase πνεῦμα ἀσθενείας (pneuma astheneias, "spirit of infirmity") indicates a demonic cause behind the physical condition, confirmed in v.16 where Satan is named as the agent of bondage. The verb συγκύπτουσα (synkyptousa, Strong's G4794, "bent together") is a NT hapax legomenon describing severe spinal curvature. verses.flow: But the Lord answered him and said, "Hypocrites! Does not each one of you on the Sabbath loose his ox or his donkey from the manger and lead it away to give it drink?

verses.flow: And this woman, being a daughter of Abraham, whom Satan bound—behold—eighteen years, was it not necessary that she be loosed from this bond on the day of the Sabbath?"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus designates the woman as a "daughter of Abraham" (θυγατέρα Ἀβραάμ), asserting her covenant membership and dignity against the synagogue ruler's dehumanizing disregard. The verb ἔδησεν (edēsen, "bound," Strong's G1210) directly parallels λυθῆναι (lythēnai, "be loosed"), framing the healing as liberation from Satanic bondage. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Satan's binding authority over a covenant member for eighteen years demonstrates the reality of hostile spiritual powers operating against God's people, which Christ has authority to overrule.

verses.flow: And as he said these things, all those opposing him were put to shame, and all the crowd was rejoicing over all the glorious things being done by him.
Therefore he said, "To what is the kingdom of God like, and to what shall I liken it?

verses.flow: It is like a grain of mustard, which a man took and cast into his garden, and it grew and became a tree, and the birds of the heaven lodged in its branches."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The image of birds lodging in branches echoes Daniel 4:12 (Nebuchadnezzar's tree-kingdom) and Ezekiel 17:23; 31:6, where a great tree sheltering birds represents a kingdom encompassing the nations. The κόκκῳ σινάπεως (kokkō sinapeōs, "grain of mustard") is the smallest cultivated seed in Palestine, making the contrast between origin and outcome dramatic. The kingdom of God begins in hiddenness but achieves universal scope.

verses.flow: And again he said, "To what shall I liken the kingdom of God?

verses.flow: And someone said to him, "Lord, are those being saved few?" And he said to them,
"Strive to enter through the narrow door, for many, I say to you, will seek to enter and will not be able.
verses.note: GREEK: Ἀγωνίζεσθε (agōnizesthe, Strong's G75) derives from ἀγών (agōn, "contest, struggle"), carrying the force of athletic or military exertion—not passive entry but intense striving. The στενῆς θύρας (stenēs thyras, "narrow door") differs from Matthew's "narrow gate" (πύλη, Matt 7:13-14); θύρα (Strong's G2374) specifically denotes a door to a house, fitting the householder parable that follows. THEOLOGICAL: The many who seek but cannot enter indicates that mere desire or association with Jesus is insufficient without genuine repentance.

verses.flow: From the time the master of the house rises up and shuts the door, and you begin to stand outside and to knock at the door, saying, 'Lord, open to us,' and answering he will say to you, 'I do not know you—from where are you?'
Then you will begin to say, 'We ate before you and drank, and in our streets you taught.'

verses.flow: And he will speak, saying to you, 'I do not know from where you are. Depart from me, all you workers of unrighteousness.'
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The phrase ἐργάται ἀδικίας ("workers of unrighteousness") echoes Psalm 6:8 (LXX Ps 6:9), where David commands evildoers to depart from him. Jesus here assumes the divine prerogative of the final Judge who determines covenant membership. The repeated "I do not know you, from where you are" constitutes a formal judicial disavowal.

verses.flow: There will be the weeping and the gnashing of teeth, when you see Abraham and Isaac and Jacob and all the prophets in the kingdom of God, but you yourselves being cast out.
And they will come from east and west, and from north and south, and will recline at table in the kingdom of God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The ingathering from the four compass points fulfills the prophetic vision of the nations streaming to Zion (Isa 2:2-3; 25:6-8; 49:12). The verb ἀνακλιθήσονται (anaklithēsontai, "will recline at table") evokes the eschatological messianic banquet. This is a radical inclusion of Gentiles alongside the patriarchs, while those who presumed covenant privilege are excluded.

verses.flow: And behold, there are last who will be first, and there are first who will be last."

verses.flow: In that same hour, certain Pharisees came near, saying to him, "Go out and depart from here, for Herod desires to kill you."

verses.flow: And he said to them, "Go and tell that fox, 'Behold, I cast out demons and perform healings today and tomorrow, and on the third day I am perfected.'
verses.note: GREEK: The term ἀλώπεκι (alōpeki, "fox," Strong's G258) denotes cunning and insignificance rather than power—a deliberate insult to Herod Antipas. THEOLOGICAL: The phrase τῇ τρίτῃ τελειοῦμαι ("on the third day I am perfected/completed," Strong's G5048) carries deep christological weight, pointing to the resurrection as the completion of Christ's redemptive work (cf. Heb 2:10; 5:9). Jesus declares sovereign control over his own timetable against any earthly ruler.

verses.flow: Nevertheless it is necessary for me to journey today and tomorrow and the day following, for it is not possible for a prophet to perish outside of Jerusalem.

verses.flow: Jerusalem, Jerusalem, the one killing the prophets and stoning those sent to her—how often I desired to gather your children in the manner a hen gathers her own brood under her wings, and you were not willing!
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Jesus' lament over Jerusalem employs the maternal image of a hen (ὄρνις, ornis) gathering her brood (νοσσιάν, nossian) under her wings (πτέρυγας, pterygas), echoing the divine wing imagery of Deuteronomy 32:11, Ruth 2:12, and Psalm 91:4, where God shelters Israel under his wings. By applying this image to himself, Jesus claims the role of YHWH as protector of his people. THEOLOGICAL: The repeated vocative "Jerusalem, Jerusalem" parallels the prophetic double address of lament (cf. 2 Sam 18:33). The statement "you were not willing" (οὐκ ἠθελήσατε) places responsibility squarely on Jerusalem's corporate refusal.

verses.flow: Behold, your house is left to you. And I say to you, you will by no means see me until the time comes when you say, 'Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord.'"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Your house is left to you" (ἀφίεται ὑμῖν ὁ οἶκος ὑμῶν) echoes Jeremiah 22:5 and 12:7, signifying the departure of divine presence from the temple—the Shekinah withdrawal. TYPOLOGY: The citation of Psalm 118:26 ("Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord") identifies Jesus as the Coming One of messianic expectation. The conditional "until" (ἕως) implies a future recognition, pointing toward Israel's eschatological repentance and acknowledgment of their Messiah (cf. Rom 11:25-26; Zech 12:10).

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And it came to pass when he entered into the house of a certain one of the rulers of the Pharisees on a Sabbath to eat bread, that they were watching him closely.
verses.note: GREEK: The verb παρατηρούμενοι (paratēroumenoi, from παρατηρέω, Strong's G3906) carries the sense of watching closely with hostile intent, not casual observation. It denotes suspicious, scrutinizing surveillance. HISTORICAL: Dining at a Pharisaic ruler's home on the Sabbath was a formal occasion; meals followed synagogue services and were governed by strict purity regulations.

verses.flow: And behold, a certain man was before him who was dropsical.
verses.note: GREEK: ὑδρωπικός (hydrōpikos, G5203) is a hapax legomenon in the New Testament, appearing only here. It refers to a condition of abnormal fluid retention (dropsy/edema). HISTORICAL: Some rabbinical traditions associated dropsy with sinfulness, which may explain the test being set before Jesus on the Sabbath.

verses.flow: And answering, Jesus spoke to the lawyers and Pharisees, saying, "Is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath, or not?"

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But they were silent. And having taken hold of him, he healed him and released him.
And to them he said, "Which of you, having a son or an ox that has fallen into a well, will not straightway pull him up on the day of the Sabbath?"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus employs a qal wahomer (lesser to greater) argument common in rabbinical discourse. If compassion compels rescue of a son or even an animal on the Sabbath, how much more should a suffering human being be healed? The textual variant between "son" (υἱός) and "donkey" (ὄνος) is significant; the harder reading "son" is likely original.

verses.flow: And they were not able to answer back against these things.

verses.flow: For everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, and he who humbles himself will be exalted."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This principle of divine reversal is a central theme in Luke's Gospel (Luke 1:52; 18:14). The passive verbs ταπεινωθήσεται and ὑψωθήσεται are divine passives—God is the unstated agent who humbles the proud and exalts the humble. TYPOLOGY: This echoes Proverbs 25:6-7 and anticipates the exaltation of Christ through his own humiliation (Phil 2:8-9).

verses.flow: And he said also to the one who had invited him, "When you prepare a dinner or a supper, do not call your friends, nor your brothers, nor your kinsmen, nor wealthy neighbors, lest they also invite you in return and a repayment be made to you.
But when you prepare a feast, invite the poor, the maimed, the lame, the blind;
and you will be blessed, because they do not have the means to repay you; for it will be repaid to you in the resurrection of the righteous."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase ἐν τῇ ἀναστάσει τῶν δικαίων ("in the resurrection of the righteous") presupposes a Pharisaic eschatology of bodily resurrection, which Jesus here affirms. This implies a distinction between the resurrection of the righteous and a general resurrection, consistent with Daniel 12:2 and Revelation 20:5-6.

verses.flow: And hearing these things, a certain one of those reclining at table said to him, "Blessed is the one who shall eat bread in the kingdom of God."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The guest's exclamation reflects a common Jewish eschatological expectation of the Messianic banquet (Isa 25:6-8). The phrase "eat bread in the kingdom of God" evokes the image of table fellowship in the age to come. Jesus' response in the following parable challenges the assumption that all Israel would automatically participate.

verses.flow: And having arrived, the servant reported these things to his lord. Then the master of the house, being angered, said to his servant, 'Go out quickly into the streets and lanes of the city, and bring in here the poor and maimed and blind and lame.'
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The master's command to bring in the poor, maimed, blind, and lame from the city streets echoes the categories from v.13 and reverses the exclusions of 2 Samuel 5:8. Those barred from the temple are now welcomed to the Lord's table. THEOLOGICAL: The progression from city streets (v.21) to highways and hedges (v.23) typologically represents the extension of God's invitation from Israel to the Gentiles (Acts 13:46).

And the lord said to the servant, 'Go out into the roads and hedges and compel them to come in, so that my house may be filled.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The shift from third person parable to first person "I say to you" (λέγω γὰρ ὑμῖν) is striking—it suggests Jesus speaks not merely as narrator but as the Lord of the feast himself. This is a solemn warning of eschatological exclusion for those who refuse the divine invitation. TYPOLOGY: The Great Supper parable parallels Matthew 22:1-14 but with distinctly Lukan emphases on the marginalized.

verses.flow: Now great crowds were traveling together with him, and having turned, he said to them,
"If anyone comes to me and does not hate his own father and mother and wife and children and brothers and sisters, and yet even his own life, he is not able to be my disciple.
verses.note: GREEK: μισεῖ (miseī, G3404) "hate" is a Semitic idiom of preference and comparative priority, not emotional hatred (cf. Gen 29:31; Mal 1:2-3). Jesus demands that loyalty to him surpass all natural bonds, including one's own ψυχή (psychē)—life, soul, self. THEOLOGICAL: The radical nature of discipleship here reflects the absolute claim of the Messiah-King over every allegiance.

verses.flow: Whoever does not bear his own cross and come after me is not able to be my disciple.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: βαστάζει τὸν σταυρὸν ἑαυτοῦ ("bears his own cross") is spoken before the crucifixion, yet prefigures Christ's own path. For the original audience, this evoked the Roman practice of condemned criminals carrying the crossbeam (patibulum) to execution—a summons to total self-denial unto death (Luke 9:23).

Mark 4:9; Rev 2:7) signals that the entire chapter—Sabbath healing, banquet teachings, cost of discipleship—forms a unified discourse demanding a decisive response.

verses.flow: And he spoke to them this parable, saying,

verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb perfect active participle accusative singular neuter

verses.flow: "What man among you, having a hundred sheep and having lost one of them, does not leave behind the ninety-nine in the wilderness and go after the one that is lost until he finds it?
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The shepherd seeking the lost sheep echoes Ezekiel 34:11-16, where God himself declares he will search for his scattered sheep, in contrast to the faithless shepherds of Israel. Jesus implicitly identifies himself with YHWH as the true Shepherd (cf. Ps 23:1; John 10:11). GREEK: ἀπολωλός (apolōlos) is the perfect active participle of ἀπόλλυμι — the sheep is in a state of having been lost, emphasizing its ongoing condition of lostness.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having found it, he lays it upon his shoulders, rejoicing.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb perfect active participle accusative singular neuter

verses.flow: And having come to the house, he calls together the friends and the neighbors, saying to them, 'Rejoice with me, for I have found my sheep, the one that was lost.'

words.m: verb present active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: I say to you that thus there shall be joy in heaven over one sinner who repents, more than over ninety-nine righteous ones who have no need of repentance.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus establishes a startling heavenly economy of joy: heaven's celebration is disproportionately directed toward the repentant sinner rather than the already-righteous. This directly addresses the Pharisees' grumbling in v.2. GREEK: μετανοοῦντι (metanoounti) — present active participle of μετανοέω (Strong's G3340), literally "changing one's mind," indicating a turning of the whole orientation of life, not merely feeling sorry.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: Or what woman, having ten drachmas, if she loses one drachma, does she not light a lamp and sweep the house and seek diligently until she finds it?

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular feminine
verb present active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: And having found it, she calls together the friends and neighbors, saying, 'Rejoice with me, for I have found the drachma which I had lost.'

words.m: verb present active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: Thus, I say to you, there comes to be joy before the angels of God over one sinner who repents."
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The phrase ἐνώπιον τῶν ἀγγέλων τοῦ θεοῦ ("before the angels of God") reveals the heavenly court setting — the divine council witnesses and rejoices at the repentance of sinners. Joy originates in the presence of God himself, with the angels as the attending assembly (cf. Job 1:6; Ps 89:7; Dan 7:10). THEOLOGICAL: The formulation suggests that the ultimate source of joy is God himself; the angels are witnesses before whom the joy occurs.

verses.flow: And he said, "A certain man had two sons.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular neuter

verses.flow: And the younger of them said to the father, 'Father, give to me the portion of the estate that falls to me.' And he divided to them his livelihood.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And not many days after, the younger son, having gathered all together, went on a journey to a far country, and there he squandered his estate, living dissolutely.
verses.note: GREEK: ἀσώτως (asōtōs, Strong's G811) is a NT hapax legomenon, meaning "dissolutely" or "wastefully" — literally "unsavingly," from the alpha-privative + σῴζω (to save). It describes a manner of life that is beyond saving or recovery. HISTORICAL: Traveling to a "far country" (χώραν μακράν) implies leaving the land of Israel, thus abandoning covenant community and placing oneself among Gentiles.

words.m: verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: And when he had spent all, there arose a severe famine throughout that country, and he himself began to be in want.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And he went and joined himself to one of the citizens of that country, and he sent him into his fields to feed swine.
And he was longing to fill his belly from the carob pods which the swine were eating, and no one was giving to him.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: κερατίων (keratia) refers to carob pods, the fruit of the Ceratonia siliqua tree, used commonly as animal feed in the Mediterranean world. THEOLOGICAL: Feeding swine represents the nadir of Jewish degradation, as pigs were ritually unclean animals (Lev 11:7). That no one gave to him underscores his complete desolation and alienation from all human community.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having come to himself, he said, 'How many hired servants of my father abound in bread, yet I here am perishing with famine!

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Having risen, I will go to my father, and I will say to him, Father, I have sinned against heaven and before you.
No longer am I worthy to be called your son. Make me as one of your hired servants.'

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle genitive singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having risen, he came to his own father. And while he was still far off, his father saw him and was moved with compassion, and having run, he fell upon his neck and kissed him fervently.
verses.note: GREEK: ἐσπλαγχνίσθη (esplanchnisthē, Strong's G4697) — from σπλάγχνα, the inward parts or bowels, regarded as the seat of deep emotion. This is the same verb used of Jesus' compassion (Matt 9:36; Mark 1:41). κατεφίλησεν (katephilēsen) is an intensified form of φιλέω with the prefix κατα-, indicating fervent, repeated kissing. TYPOLOGY: The father's running is culturally extraordinary — a dignified patriarch in the ancient Near East would not run, suggesting a deliberate self-humiliation that prefigures God's condescension in Christ.

verses.flow: And the son said to him, 'Father, I have sinned against heaven and before you. No longer am I worthy to be called your son.'
But the father said to his servants, 'Quickly, bring out the best robe and clothe him, and give a ring for his hand and sandals for his feet.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The robe (στολὴν τὴν πρώτην — "the first/best robe"), the ring (δακτύλιον), and sandals (ὑποδήματα) together signify full restoration: the robe indicates honor and status, the ring signifies authority and family identity (cf. Gen 41:42), and sandals distinguish a son from a slave, who would go barefoot. THEOLOGICAL: This is not partial acceptance but complete reinstatement — the imagery of royal investiture echoes God's restoration of Israel (cf. Zech 3:4-5, where Joshua the high priest is reclothed).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And bring the fattened calf, slaughter it, and let us eat and celebrate.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For this son of mine was dead and has come to life again; he was lost and has been found.' And they began to celebrate.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The death-to-life and lost-to-found language carries profound theological weight, anticipating the Pauline theology of resurrection from spiritual death (Eph 2:1-5; Col 2:13). GREEK: ἀνέζησεν (anezēsen) — "came to life again," from ἀναζάω, emphasizing not merely survival but a genuine return from death to life. The father's declaration functions as a performative utterance establishing the son's new status.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Now his elder son was in the field, and as he came and drew near to the house, he heard music and dancing.

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having called one of the servants to him, he was inquiring what these things might be.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: And he said to him, 'Your brother has come, and your father has slaughtered the fattened calf, because he received him back in good health.'

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But he was angered and was not willing to go in. And his father, having come out, was entreating him.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But answering, he said to the father, 'Behold, so many years I serve you, and never have I transgressed a commandment of yours, and to me you never gave a young goat that I might celebrate with my friends.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But when this son of yours came, the one who devoured your livelihood with harlots, you slaughtered for him the fattened calf.'
And he said to him, 'Child, you are always with me, and all that is mine is yours.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But it was fitting to celebrate and to rejoice, for this brother of yours was dead and has come to life, and was lost and has been found.'"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: ἔδει (edei) — "it was necessary/fitting" — introduces divine necessity. The father does not merely choose to celebrate; it is a moral and theological imperative. The parable ends without resolution regarding the elder son — an open ending directed at the Pharisees and scribes of v.2, inviting them to respond. STRUCTURE: The three parables of Luke 15 form a unified triptych with escalating intimacy: a shepherd with sheep, a woman with a coin, a father with a son — each revealing God's relentless pursuit and extravagant joy over the lost who are found.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle accusative plural neuter

verses.flow: And he was saying also to the disciples, "A certain man was rich, who had a steward, and this one was accused before him as squandering his possessions."
verses.note: GREEK: "Steward" (οἰκονόμος, oikonomos, Strong's G3623) denotes a household manager or estate administrator—one entrusted with another's property. The term carries weight in Pauline theology as well (1 Cor 4:1-2; Tit 1:7). "Squandering" (διασκορπίζων, diaskorpizōn) is the same verb used of the prodigal son in Luke 15:13, linking the two parables thematically.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having summoned him, he said to him, "What is this I hear concerning you? Give back the account of your stewardship, for you are no longer able to be steward."
And the steward said within himself, "What shall I do, for my lord is taking away the stewardship from me? To dig I am not strong enough; to beg I am ashamed."
"I have known what I shall do, so that when I am removed from the stewardship, they may receive me into their houses."

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having summoned each one of his lord's debtors, he was saying to the first, "How much do you owe my lord?"

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And he said, "A hundred baths of oil." And he said to him, "Receive your documents, and having sat down quickly, write fifty."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: A "bath" (βάτος, batos) was a Hebrew liquid measure of approximately 22 liters (roughly 6 gallons). One hundred baths of oil represented a substantial debt. The reduction to fifty—a fifty percent discount—would have created significant goodwill and social obligation from the debtor toward the steward.

verses.flow: Then to another he said, "And you, how much do you owe?" And he said, "A hundred cors of wheat." He says to him, "Receive your documents, and write eighty."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: A "cor" (κόρος, koros) was a Hebrew dry measure equivalent to approximately 10-11 bushels. One hundred cors of wheat was an enormous quantity. The reduction to eighty—a twenty percent discount—was proportionally smaller than the oil reduction, perhaps reflecting different profit margins.

verses.flow: And the lord commended the steward of unrighteousness, because he had acted shrewdly; for the sons of this age are more shrewd than the sons of light toward their own generation.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The lord" (ὁ κύριος) here refers to the master in the parable, not to Jesus or God. The commendation is not for dishonesty but for shrewdness (φρονίμως, phronimōs). The contrast between "sons of this age" and "sons of light" echoes the language of Qumran (1QS 1:9; 3:13) and Pauline usage (1 Thess 5:5; Eph 5:8). DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "sons of light" designation implies a cosmic-spiritual allegiance, contrasting those aligned with God's realm against those operating by worldly wisdom.

verses.flow: And I say to you, make for yourselves friends by means of the mammon of unrighteousness, so that when it fails, they may receive you into the eternal dwellings.
verses.note: GREEK: "Mammon" (μαμωνᾶς, mamōnas) is an Aramaic loanword meaning wealth or property, here personified as a rival power. "Eternal dwellings" (αἰωνίους σκηνάς) deliberately echoes the tabernacle/tent (σκηνή) language of the LXX, pointing to eschatological habitations prepared by God. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus commands the strategic, generous use of worldly wealth for eternal relational gain—an inversion of the steward's self-serving shrewdness.

verses.flow: The one faithful in a least thing is faithful also in much, and the one unrighteous in a least thing is unrighteous also in much.
If therefore in the unrighteous mammon you have not been faithful, who will entrust to you the true riches?
And if in that which belongs to another you have not been faithful, who will give to you that which is your own?

No household servant is able to serve two lords; for either the one he will hate and the other he will love, or to one he will hold fast and the other he will despise. You are not able to serve God and mammon.
verses.note: GREEK: "Household servant" (οἰκέτης, oiketēs, Strong's G3610) is more specific than the general δοῦλος (doulos); it denotes a domestic slave, intensifying the intimacy of the service relationship. THEOLOGICAL: The parallel in Matt 6:24 omits οἰκέτης. The absolute impossibility of dual service frames mammon as a functional deity competing with the true God for total allegiance (cf. Deut 6:4-5).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And the Pharisees, being lovers of money, were hearing all these things, and they were deriding him.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And he said to them, "You are those who justify yourselves before men, but God knows your hearts; for that which is exalted among men is an abomination before God."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Abomination" (βδέλυγμα, bdelygma, Strong's G946) is the LXX term for idolatrous defilement (cf. Dan 9:27; 11:31; 12:11). Jesus applies this cultic pollution language to human self-exaltation, equating the Pharisees' honor-seeking with idolatry. What is "exalted" (ὑψηλόν) among men stands as detestable before God—a radical inversion of human value systems.

verses.flow: "The Law and the Prophets were until John; from that time the kingdom of God is proclaimed as good news, and everyone forces his way into it."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse marks a pivotal salvation-historical transition: the epoch of the Law and the Prophets finds its terminus ad quem in John the Baptist. From that point, the kingdom of God is "proclaimed as good news" (εὐαγγελίζεται, euangelizetai). TYPOLOGY: John the Baptist functions as the Elijah figure (Mal 4:5; Luke 1:17) who heralds the new era. The verb βιάζεται can be middle ("forces his way in") or passive ("is being forced"), with the former reading emphasizing the urgency of kingdom entry.

verses.flow: "But it is easier for heaven and earth to pass away than for one stroke of the Law to fall."

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb perfect passive participle accusative singular feminine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: "Everyone who divorces his wife and marries another commits adultery, and the one who marries her who has been divorced from a husband commits adultery."

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: "Now there was a certain rich man, and he was clothing himself in purple and fine linen, feasting sumptuously each day."

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: "And a certain poor man named Lazarus had been laid at his gate, covered with sores,"

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle genitive plural neuter
verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: "and longing to be fed from the things falling from the table of the rich man; but even the dogs, coming, were licking his sores."
"And it came to pass that the poor man died and was carried away by the angels to the bosom of Abraham. And the rich man also died and was buried."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The bosom of Abraham" (τὸν κόλπον Ἀβραάμ) denotes a place of honor and intimate fellowship at the eschatological banquet (cf. John 13:23 for the same idiom). This reflects Second Temple Jewish belief in a differentiated intermediate state. HISTORICAL: The imagery of angels carrying the righteous dead is attested in Jewish tradition (Testament of Abraham; 4 Maccabees). The rich man receives only burial—no angelic escort.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: "And in Hades, having lifted up his eyes, being in torments, he sees Abraham from afar and Lazarus in his bosom."
verses.note: GREEK: "Hades" (ᾅδης, hadēs, Strong's G86) translates Hebrew Sheol and in this context denotes the abode of the unrighteous dead in conscious torment, distinct from the place of comfort where Abraham and Lazarus reside. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The passage reflects a cosmology in which the dead exist in a differentiated intermediate state under divine governance, awaiting final judgment.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: "And he, having cried out, said, 'Father Abraham, have mercy on me and send Lazarus, that he may dip the tip of his finger in water and cool my tongue, for I am in anguish in this flame.'"
"But Abraham said, 'Child, remember that you received your good things in your life, and Lazarus likewise the evil things; but now here he is comforted, and you are in anguish.'"

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: "'And in all these things, between us and you a great chasm has been fixed, so that those wishing to cross over from here to you are not able, nor may they cross from there to us.'"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "great chasm" (χάσμα μέγα) that has been "fixed" (ἐστήρικται, perfect passive of στηρίζω) indicates a permanent, divinely established boundary. The perfect tense emphasizes the settled, irrevocable nature of this separation. This excludes any post-mortem crossing or second chance, underscoring the finality of one's state after death.

verses.flow: "And he said, 'I ask you then, father, that you would send him to the house of my father,'"
"'for I have five brothers, so that he may solemnly warn them, lest they also come into this place of torment.'"
"But Abraham says, 'They have Moses and the Prophets; let them hear them.'"
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "Moses and the Prophets" constitutes the entirety of the Hebrew Scriptures as witness to God's covenant faithfulness and ethical demands. Abraham's response places supreme authority in the written Word of God over miraculous signs—a theme central to Luke's theology (Luke 24:25-27, 44-45).

verses.flow: "And he said, 'No, father Abraham, but if someone from the dead should go to them, they will repent.'"
"But he said to him, 'If they do not hear Moses and the Prophets, neither will they be persuaded if someone should rise from the dead.'"
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This climactic verse carries profound irony: one named Lazarus will indeed be raised from the dead (John 11:1-44), and the religious leaders will not believe but will plot to kill both Jesus and Lazarus (John 11:53; 12:10). Ultimately, Jesus himself rises from the dead, and still many refuse to believe. The sufficiency of Scripture as witness to Christ is thus powerfully affirmed (cf. John 5:39-47).

verses.flow: And he said to his disciples, "It is impossible for the stumbling-blocks not to come, but woe to him through whom they come.
verses.note: GREEK: "Anendekton" (ἀνένδεκτον) is a NT hapax legomenon meaning "inadmissible, impossible." "Skandala" (σκάνδαλα, Strong's G4625) refers to a trap-stick or stumbling-block—an occasion that causes spiritual ruin. The term carries connotations of entrapment in both moral and spiritual senses.

verses.flow: It would be better for him if a millstone were hung around his neck and he were cast into the sea, than that he should cause one of these little ones to stumble.
verses.note: GREEK: "Lithos mylikos" (λίθος μυλικός) refers to a large millstone turned by a donkey, not a small hand-mill stone. The hyperbolic image emphasizes the catastrophic severity of leading a "little one" (mikros) astray. THEOLOGICAL: The "little ones" likely refers to new or vulnerable believers whose faith can be destroyed by the stumbling-blocks of verse 1.

verses.flow: Take heed to yourselves. If your brother sins, rebuke him, and if he repents, forgive him.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And if seven times in the day he sins against you, and seven times turns back to you, saying, "I repent," you shall forgive him.
And the apostles said to the Lord, "Add to us faith."
verses.note: GREEK: "Prosthes" (πρόσθες) is an aorist imperative of prostithēmi—"Add!" The apostles recognize that the demands of limitless forgiveness (v.4) exceed natural human capacity and require a divine grant of faith. This is the only place in Luke where "apostoloi" address Jesus as "Kyrios" in a direct petition.

verses.flow: And the Lord said, "If you had faith as a grain of mustard, you would say to this mulberry tree, 'Be uprooted and be planted in the sea,' and it would have obeyed you.
verses.note: GREEK: "Kokkos sinapeōs" (κόκκον σινάπεως)—a grain of mustard, proverbially the smallest of seeds. "Sykaminos" (συκαμίνος, G4807) is the black mulberry tree, distinct from the sycamore-fig (sykomoros) of Luke 19:4. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus does not promise to increase their faith quantitatively; he redefines faith as operative trust in God's power, where even a minuscule measure can accomplish the impossible.

verb present active participle accusative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle dative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But which of you, having a servant plowing or tending sheep, will say to him when he has come in from the field, "Come at once and recline at table"?

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But will he not say to him, "Prepare what I shall eat, and gird yourself and serve me while I eat and drink, and after these things you shall eat and drink"?

words.m: verb aorist passive participle accusative plural neuter

verses.flow: Does he have gratitude toward that servant because he did the things that were commanded?

words.m: verb aorist passive participle accusative plural neuter

verses.flow: So also you, when you have done all the things commanded you, say, "We are unprofitable servants; what we were obligated to do, we have done."
verses.note: GREEK: "Achreioi douloi" (ἀχρεῖοι δοῦλοι)—"unprofitable servants" or "unworthy servants." The word achreios (G888) means "useless" in the sense of having no claim to special merit. THEOLOGICAL: This parable establishes that obedience to God's commands creates no basis for boasting or meritorious standing; the servant has merely fulfilled obligation. This anticipates Pauline grace theology (Rom 4:4-5; Eph 2:8-9).

verses.flow: And it came to pass in his journeying to Jerusalem, that he was passing through the midst of Samaria and Galilee.

words.m: verb present middle participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: And as he entered into a certain village, ten leprous men met him, who stood at a distance.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And they lifted up their voice, saying, "Jesus, Master, have mercy on us!"

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And having seen them, he said to them, "Go and show yourselves to the priests." And it came to pass that as they were going, they were cleansed.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Jesus commands the lepers to show themselves to the priests according to the Torah purification rite of Leviticus 14:1-32. Their cleansing occurs en route—while obeying in faith before the evidence appears. This prefigures justification by faith: acting on the word of Christ before visible confirmation. HISTORICAL: That the group included a Samaritan (v.16) is remarkable, as Samaritans would not normally travel with Jews to Jerusalem's temple priests.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And one of them, having seen that he was healed, turned back, with a loud voice glorifying God.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And he fell upon his face at his feet, giving thanks to him. And he was a Samaritan.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And Jesus answered and said, "Were not the ten cleansed? But the nine—where are they?

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Were none found returning to give glory to God except this foreigner?"
verses.note: GREEK: "Allogenēs" (ἀλλογενής, G241) is a NT hapax legomenon meaning "of another race/nation, foreigner." Strikingly, this exact word appears on the Jerusalem temple warning inscription forbidding non-Jews from entering the inner courts. THEOLOGICAL: The irony is devastating—the one whom the temple placard would exclude as allogenēs is the only one who returns to glorify God. This foreshadows the inclusion of the Gentiles and the obsolescence of ethnic barriers in the kingdom.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And he said to him, "Rise and go your way; your faith has saved you."

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having been asked by the Pharisees when the kingdom of God is coming, he answered them and said, "The kingdom of God does not come with observation.
Nor will they say, "Behold, here!" or "There!" For behold, the kingdom of God is in your midst."
verses.note: GREEK: "Entos hymōn" (ἐντὸς ὑμῶν) can mean either "within you" (interior/spiritual) or "in your midst" (among you). Given that Jesus addresses hostile Pharisees, "in your midst" is the more likely sense—the kingdom is present because the King himself stands among them. THEOLOGICAL: The kingdom of God is not a geopolitical event to be charted by observation (paratērēsis, G3907) but is inaugurated in the person and authority of Jesus the Christ.

verses.flow: And he said to the disciples, "Days will come when you will desire to see one of the days of the Son of Man, and you will not see it.
And they will say to you, "Behold, there! Behold, here!" Do not go away, nor pursue after them.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: For just as the lightning, flashing, shines from one part under heaven to the other part under heaven, so shall the Son of Man be in his day.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The Son of Man's revelation (apokalypsis) will be unmistakable and universal—like lightning illuminating the entire sky. No secret or localized coming is in view. TYPOLOGY: The "Son of Man" title draws from Daniel 7:13-14, where one like a son of man receives dominion, glory, and an everlasting kingdom from the Ancient of Days. DIVINE_COUNCIL: This evokes the heavenly court scene of Daniel 7, where the Son of Man is enthroned above the hostile beast-kingdoms.

verses.flow: But first it is necessary for him to suffer many things and to be rejected by this generation.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Before the glory of revelation comes the necessity (dei, δεῖ—divine necessity) of suffering and rejection. This is the pattern of Christ's messianic vocation: suffering precedes glory, the cross before the crown. This verse anchors the eschatological discourse in the immediate reality of the passion (cf. Luke 9:22, 18:31-33).

verses.flow: And just as it came to pass in the days of Noah, so also shall it be in the days of the Son of Man.
They were eating, they were drinking, they were marrying, they were being given in marriage, until the day Noah entered into the ark, and the flood came and destroyed them all.
Likewise, just as it came to pass in the days of Lot—they were eating, they were drinking, they were buying, they were selling, they were planting, they were building—
but on the day Lot went out from Sodom, it rained fire and brimstone from heaven and destroyed them all.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The destruction of Sodom by fire and brimstone (theion, θεῖον) from heaven serves as a type of the eschatological judgment at the Son of Man's revelation. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Genesis 19:24 describes YHWH raining fire "from YHWH out of heaven," a passage long noted in Jewish exegesis as indicating a two-powers-in-heaven theology. Jesus applies this divine judgment pattern to his own coming as the Son of Man.

verses.flow: According to these same things shall it be on the day the Son of Man is revealed.
On that day, let the one who is on the housetop, with his goods in the house, not come down to take them away; and likewise let the one in the field not turn back to the things behind.
Remember the wife of Lot.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Lot's wife (Gen 19:26) looked back toward Sodom and became a pillar of salt. She serves as a negative type of attachment to the world that is under judgment. Her backward glance represents divided loyalty—the antithesis of the total surrender demanded in v.33.

verses.flow: Whoever seeks to preserve his life shall lose it, and whoever loses it shall give it life.
verses.note: GREEK: "Zōogonēsei" (ζῳογονήσει, G2225) literally means "shall bring to life, shall give life to." This is a rare and vivid term, stronger than simply "save." THEOLOGICAL: The paradox of the kingdom: self-preservation leads to destruction, while self-loss for Christ's sake leads to the generation of true, eschatological life. This echoes Luke 9:24 and anticipates the theology of dying and rising with Christ (Rom 6:4-5).

verses.flow: And answering, they said to him, "Where, Lord?" And he said to them, "Where the body is, there also the eagles will be gathered together."
verses.note: GREEK: "Aetoi" (ἀετοί, G105) can mean either "eagles" or "vultures." In context, the proverb points to vultures gathering where a corpse lies—judgment is drawn inevitably to the place of spiritual death. THEOLOGICAL: The disciples ask "Where?" regarding the coming judgment. Jesus answers with a proverb rather than a location: judgment is not a matter of geography but of spiritual condition. Where moral corruption exists, divine judgment will inevitably descend.

STRUCTURE: This parable (vv. 1-8) and the one following (vv. 9-14) form a pair on prayer, the first addressing perseverance, the second addressing posture of heart.

HISTORICAL: Some later interpreters invented a small gate called "the eye of the needle" in Jerusalem, but no archaeological or literary evidence from the period supports this. verses.flow: And having taken aside the twelve, He said to them, "Behold, we are going up to Jerusalem, and all the things that have been written through the prophets concerning the Son of Man shall be accomplished.

The phrase "all the things that have been written through the prophets" (πάντα τὰ γεγραμμένα διὰ τῶν προφητῶν) affirms that the suffering of the Son of Man is not failure but prophetic fulfillment — Isaiah 53, Psalm 22, Zechariah 12:10 all converge on the Messiah's suffering and vindication. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The Son of Man title (Dan 7:13-14) designates a heavenly figure who receives authority from the Ancient of Days within the divine council assembly, yet this same figure must first suffer — a mystery hidden from the disciples (v. 34).

verses.note: GREEK: The verb σέσωκέν (sesōken, perfect active of σῴζω, Strong's G4982) means both "has saved" and "has healed/made whole." The perfect tense indicates a completed action with ongoing results. Faith (πίστις) is the instrument, but the power is Christ's alone. THEOLOGICAL: Physical healing serves as a sign of the greater salvation Jesus brings — the restoration of all things. verses.note: GREEK: ἀρχιτελώνης (architelōnēs) is a NT hapax legomenon, occurring only here. It denotes a "chief tax-collector" — one who supervised other tax-collectors (telōnai) in a district. Jericho was a lucrative taxation hub due to balsam trade and its position on a major trade route. HISTORICAL: Tax-collectors (publicani) contracted with Rome and were despised as collaborators and extortioners. A chief tax-collector would have been especially wealthy and socially ostracized among Jews.

verses.note: GREEK: σταθείς (statheis, aorist passive participle of ἵστημι) — "having stood," indicating a formal, solemn posture of public declaration. τετραπλοῦν (tetraploun, "fourfold") exceeds the Torah requirement of restoring the principal plus one-fifth (Lev 6:5; Num 5:7), matching instead the penalty for theft of a sheep (Exod 22:1 LXX). verses.flow: And as they were hearing these things, he proceeded to speak a parable, because he was near Jerusalem, and because they supposed that the kingdom of God was about to appear immediately.

Luke's audience would have recognized this political backdrop. TYPOLOGY: The nobleman going to a far country to receive a kingdom and return prefigures Christ's ascension and second coming. and will dash you to the ground, and your children within you, and they will not leave in you stone upon stone, because you did not know the time of your visitation."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: ἐδαφιοῦσίν σε (edaphiousin se, "they will dash you to the ground") and the stone-upon-stone language precisely describe the Roman destruction of Jerusalem in AD 70 under Titus, when the city and temple were razed. GREEK: ἐπισκοπή (episkopē, "visitation") carries dual meaning — it can be a gracious visitation for salvation or a judicial visitation for judgment (cf. 1 Pet 2:12). Israel missed the former and received the latter. TYPOLOGY: This echoes the covenant pattern of Deuteronomy 28-32 — blessings for obedience, curses for rejecting God's appointed deliverer.

verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Jesus combines Isaiah 56:7 ("my house shall be called a house of prayer") with Jeremiah 7:11 ("den of robbers"), both prophetic indictments of temple corruption. Just as Jeremiah warned of the first temple's destruction, Jesus warns of the second. THEOLOGICAL: By calling the temple "my house," Jesus implicitly claims divine prerogative — it is his Father's house and thus his own. The cleansing enacts prophetic authority over the temple itself (cf. Mal 3:1-3).

verses.note: GREEK: ἐξεκρέματο (exekremato) is a vivid NT hapax legomenon meaning "was hanging upon" — the people hung on his every word, an image of rapt, absorbed attention. The imperfect tense conveys ongoing, continuous action. STRUCTURE: This verse sets the stage for the passion narrative: the leaders desire to kill Jesus but are restrained by the people's devotion, creating the dramatic tension that drives the subsequent chapters.

The combination of chief priests, scribes, and elders represents the full Sanhedrin—the three constituent groups of the ruling council. HISTORICAL: This confrontation occurs during Jesus's final week of temple teaching, when his authority directly challenged the religious establishment's control of the temple precinct.

verses.flow: and they spoke, saying to him, "Tell us, by what authority do you do these things, or who is the one having given you this authority?"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The question of ἐξουσία (exousia, "authority") is central to this chapter. The religious leaders demand Jesus name the source of his authority—whether rabbinic ordination, prophetic commission, or divine appointment. Jesus's counter-question reveals that they have already rejected the prophetic witness that would have answered their question (John 1:29-34).

verses.note: TYPOLOGY: John's baptism as "from heaven" (ἐξ οὐρανοῦ) connects the prophetic office to divine commissioning. John served as the Elijah-figure preparing the way (Mal 3:1; Luke 1:17), and his testimony pointed directly to Jesus's authority as the Coming One. To acknowledge John was to acknowledge Jesus.

verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The parable of the vineyard directly echoes Isaiah 5:1-7, where Israel is God's vineyard. The owner (God) entrusting it to vinedressers (Israel's leaders) establishes the covenant framework. The "considerable time" (χρόνους ἱκανούς) reflects the long span of salvation history from Sinai to the Second Temple period.

verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Jesus quotes Psalm 118:22 (LXX 117:22), the rejected stone becoming the cornerstone. This psalm was sung during Passover and Feast of Tabernacles processions. The rejected stone is a messianic image: Christ, rejected by Israel's builders (leaders), becomes the foundational stone of God's new temple (Eph 2:20; 1 Pet 2:4-7). GREEK: κεφαλὴν γωνίας (kephalēn gōnias, "head of the corner") denotes the capstone or keystone that holds the entire structure together.
