verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse likely alludes to both Isaiah 8:14-15 (the stone of stumbling) and Daniel 2:34-35, 44-45 (the stone that crushes the kingdoms). The dual action—being shattered by falling on the stone, or being ground to powder when it falls—expresses the inescapable judgment of rejecting the Messiah. GREEK: λικμήσει (likmēsei, from λικμάω, "to winnow, scatter like chaff") is rare in the NT, intensifying the imagery of total destruction.

verses.flow: And the scribes and the chief priests sought to lay hands on him in that very hour, but they feared the people, for they knew that he had spoken this parable against them.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: ἀπόδοτε (apodote, from ἀποδίδωμι, "render back, return what is owed") is more precise than simply "give"—it implies returning what belongs to its rightful owner. Caesar's image on the coin establishes his claim; but humanity bears God's image (Gen 1:26-27), establishing God's total claim over human beings. The answer transcends the political trap entirely.

The phrase υἱοί εἰσιν θεοῦ ("they are sons of God") places resurrected believers within the divine council framework—they become members of the heavenly family, sharing the status of the "sons of God" (bene elohim) of the heavenly assembly (Job 1:6; Ps 89:6). verses.flow: But that the dead are raised, even Moses showed at the bush, when he calls the Lord 'the God of Abraham and the God of Isaac and the God of Jacob.'

God's self-identification as the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob in the present tense at the burning bush implies their continued existence. The covenant relationship endures beyond death. GREEK: ἐμήνυσεν (emēnysen, from μηνύω, "to disclose, reveal") suggests Moses made known a hidden truth about God's nature.

verses.flow: And some of the scribes, answering, said, "Teacher, you have spoken well."
For they no longer dared to question him about anything.
And he said to them, "How do they say that the Christ is the son of David?
For David himself says in the book of Psalms, 'The Lord said to my Lord, "Sit at my right hand,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Jesus quotes Psalm 110:1 (LXX 109:1), the most frequently cited OT passage in the NT. εἶπεν κύριος τῷ κυρίῳ μου—"The Lord said to my Lord"—in the Hebrew, YHWH speaks to the Davidic king addressed as אדני (adoni). Jesus's argument is that David, through the Spirit, calls the Messiah "Lord," which means the Christ cannot be merely David's biological descendant but must be his divine superior. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Psalm 110 depicts the Messiah enthroned at God's right hand, exercising divine rule—a throne-sharing that transcends any merely human kingship.

verses.flow: until I place your enemies as a footstool for your feet."'
David therefore calls him Lord, and how is he his son?"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This is the climactic christological question of the chapter. If David calls the Christ "Lord" (κύριον), the Messiah must be more than a human descendant of David. Jesus does not deny Davidic sonship but reveals that it is insufficient to explain the Messiah's full identity. He is both David's son according to the flesh and David's Lord according to his divine nature (Rom 1:3-4).

verses.note: GREEK: κρίμα (krima, "judgment, verdict") here carries the sense of condemnatory sentence. περισσότερον (perissoteron, "more abundant, greater") indicates that those who exploit their religious position for personal gain face a stricter accounting. THEOLOGICAL: The devouring of widows' houses likely refers to exploiting vulnerable women through excessive legal fees, mismanagement of estates, or extracting hospitality. This warning transitions directly into the widow's offering in Luke 21:1-4, creating a deliberate literary contrast.

Luke uses both terms to emphasize the depth of her poverty. The "λεπτά" (lepta) were the smallest copper coins in circulation, worth about 1/128 of a denarius.

verses.flow: And he said, "Truly I say to you that this poor widow cast in more than all;

verses.flow: for all these out of their abundance cast into the gifts, but she out of her lack cast in all the livelihood that she had."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The contrast between "περισσεύοντος" (perisseúontos, surplus/abundance) and "ὑστερήματος" (hysterēmatos, deficiency/lack) establishes a principle of sacrificial giving that measures not by amount but by cost to the giver. Christ evaluates the heart behind the offering, not the treasury impact. Her giving of "πάντα τὸν βίον" (all her livelihood) prefigures Christ's own total self-offering (Phil 2:7-8).

TYPOLOGY: The phrase "stone upon stone" (λίθος ἐπὶ λίθῳ) echoes prophetic judgment language, recalling the desolation oracles of Micah 3:12 and Jeremiah 26:18.

verses.flow: But from now on the Son of Man shall be seated at the right hand of the power of God."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus' declaration that "from now on the Son of Man shall be seated at the right hand of the power of God" combines Daniel 7:13-14 (the Son of Man receiving dominion) with Psalm 110:1 (sitting at God's right hand). This is a claim to divine enthronement and co-regency with the Most High — the very claim that provokes the question in v. 70. TYPOLOGY: The "right hand of the power of God" (ἐκ δεξιῶν τῆς δυνάμεως τοῦ θεοῦ) uses "power" (δύναμις) as a reverential circumlocution for God himself, common in Second Temple Judaism.

verses.flow: And they all said, "You then are the Son of God?" And he said to them, "You say that I am."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The question "You then are the Son of God?" (Σὺ οὖν εἶ ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ θεοῦ;) shows the Sanhedrin understood Jesus' claim in v. 69 as a claim to divine identity, not merely messiahship. Jesus' response Ὑμεῖς λέγετε ὅτι ἐγώ εἰμι ("You say that I am") is an affirmative declaration cast in a form that places responsibility on his questioners. The ἐγώ εἰμι ("I am") may also echo the divine self-identification of Exodus 3:14 (LXX). DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Son of God" in its fullest sense designates Jesus not merely as a member of the divine council but as the unique Son who shares the Father's nature and throne (cf. Ps 2:7; Heb 1:3-5).

verses.flow: And they began to accuse him, saying, "This one we found perverting our nation and forbidding to give tribute to Caesar, and saying himself to be Christ, a king."
verses.note: GREEK: κατηγορεῖν (katēgorein, Strong's G2723) means "to accuse formally in a legal setting." The three charges—perverting the nation, forbidding tribute to Caesar, and claiming to be Christ a king—are crafted to present Jesus as a political threat to Rome. THEOLOGICAL: The charge that he calls himself "Christon Basilea" (Christ, a king) is ironically the most truthful statement his accusers make; Jesus is indeed the Anointed King (Ps 2:2, Dan 7:13-14).

This is neither evasion nor denial but an indirect confirmation that shifts the weight of the claim onto the questioner. THEOLOGICAL: Two earthly rulers pass judgment on the Lord of glory, fulfilling the pattern of Psalm 2:2: "The kings of the earth set themselves... against the Lord and against his Anointed."

verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Jesus quotes Hosea 10:8, a prophetic oracle against the idolatrous Northern Kingdom. The language of mountains falling and hills covering is apocalyptic judgment imagery, applied here to the coming destruction of Jerusalem in AD 70. This passage is also echoed in Revelation 6:16, where the same cry is directed at the Lamb in the final judgment.

The "green wood" represents the living, innocent Jesus; the "dry wood" represents the guilty nation ripe for judgment. THEOLOGICAL: If Rome does this to the innocent one, what will befall the guilty? This is a prophetic warning of the destruction of Jerusalem (AD 70).

And he said, "Jesus, remember me when you come into your kingdom."
And he said to him, "Truly I say to you, today you shall be with me in Paradise."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus's declaration "Today you shall be with me in Paradise" (παραδείσῳ, paradeisō, Strong's G3857, a Persian loanword used in the LXX for the Garden of Eden in Gen 2:8) affirms the immediate conscious presence of the redeemed with Christ after death. GREEK: παράδεισος in Second Temple Jewish literature referred to the blessed abode of the righteous dead (cf. 2 Cor 12:4; Rev 2:7). This is the only use of the word in the Gospels.

verses.flow: And it was already about the sixth hour, and darkness came over the whole land until the ninth hour,

words.m: verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: the sun having been darkened. And the veil of the temple was torn in the middle.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The tearing of the temple veil (καταπέτασμα τοῦ ναοῦ, katapetasma tou naou) signifies the opening of access to the divine presence. The inner veil separated the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place (Exod 26:33). Its rending from top indicates divine action. TYPOLOGY: Hebrews 10:19-20 interprets this veil as Christ's flesh—through his death a new and living way is opened into the true Holy of Holies. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The barrier between the divine throne room and humanity is removed by the sacrifice of the incarnate Son.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And Jesus, having cried out with a loud voice, said, "Father, into your hands I commit my spirit." And having said this, he breathed his last.
verses.note: GREEK: παρατίθεμαι (paratithēmi, Strong's G3908)—"I commit, I entrust, I deposit." Jesus quotes Psalm 31:5 (LXX 30:6), a psalm of trust in the Lord. THEOLOGICAL: Unlike Mark's account of the cry of dereliction (Mark 15:34, quoting Ps 22:1), Luke records Jesus dying with a prayer of trust and self-surrender to the Father, demonstrating his unbroken communion with God even in death. The voluntary nature of his death is emphasized: he commits his spirit; it is not taken from him (cf. John 10:18).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist middle participle accusative singular neuter
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And the centurion, having seen what had happened, glorified God, saying, "Certainly this man was righteous."
verses.note: GREEK: δίκαιος (dikaios)—"righteous, just, innocent." The centurion's declaration in Luke is "righteous" rather than "Son of God" as in Mark/Matthew. THEOLOGICAL: A Gentile Roman soldier recognizes the righteousness of the one condemned by both Jewish leaders and Roman authority, previewing the Gentile mission. This echoes the Suffering Servant of Isaiah 53:11: "the righteous one, my servant, shall make many to be accounted righteous."

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist middle participle accusative plural neuter
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And all the crowds who had come together for this spectacle, having beheld the things that happened, returned, beating their breasts.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural feminine
verb present active participle nominative plural feminine

verses.flow: And all those known to him stood at a distance, and the women who had followed with him from Galilee, beholding these things.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And behold, a man named Joseph, a council member, a good and righteous man—

words.m: verb perfect middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: this one had not consented to their counsel and deed—from Arimathea, a city of the Jews, who was waiting for the kingdom of God.
verses.note: GREEK: προσεδέχετο (prosedecheto, Strong's G4327)—imperfect tense, "was waiting for, was expectantly receiving." THEOLOGICAL: Joseph of Arimathea embodies the faithful remnant of Israel who awaited the kingdom of God (cf. Luke 2:25, 38 where Simeon and Anna likewise awaited redemption). His dissent from the Sanhedrin's decision shows that not all of Israel's council rejected their Messiah.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: This man, having approached Pilate, asked for the body of Jesus.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having taken it down, he wrapped it in linen cloth, and placed him in a hewn tomb where no one had yet been laid.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The detail that the tomb was hewn in stone and that no one had yet been laid in it (cf. John 19:41) parallels the prophetic significance of purity and newness—the body of the Holy One would see no corruption (Ps 16:10; Acts 2:27). HISTORICAL: Rock-hewn tombs (λαξευτός, laxeutos, a NT hapax legomenon) were costly and typically belonged to wealthy families, fulfilling Isaiah 53:9: "with a rich man in his death."

verses.flow: And it was the day of Preparation, and the Sabbath was drawing near.
verses.note: GREEK: παρασκευή (paraskeuē, Strong's G3904)—the technical term for Friday, the day of Preparation before the Sabbath. ἐπέφωσκεν (epephōsken)—"was dawning," but Jewish days begin at sunset, so this likely means the Sabbath lights were beginning to appear at dusk. HISTORICAL: The urgency to complete the burial before the Sabbath began at sundown shaped the haste of the burial proceedings.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural feminine
verb perfect active participle nominative plural feminine

verses.flow: And the women who had come with him from Galilee, having followed closely, observed the tomb and how his body was placed.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural feminine

verses.flow: And having returned, they prepared spices and ointments. And on the Sabbath they rested according to the commandment.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The women's preparation of spices and ointments, followed by Sabbath rest "according to the commandment" (κατὰ τὴν ἐντολήν), demonstrates their Torah-faithfulness even in grief. TYPOLOGY: The Sabbath rest between crucifixion and resurrection carries deep typological weight—the Creator rested on the seventh day (Gen 2:2-3), and now the Redeemer rests in the tomb on the Sabbath before the new creation dawns on the first day of the week.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural feminine

verses.flow: And on the first day of the week, at deep dawn, they came to the tomb, bringing the spices which they had prepared.
verses.note: GREEK: "τῇ μιᾷ τῶν σαββάτων" (tē mia tōn sabbatōn) literally "on the first of the sabbaths," a Semitic idiom for the first day of the week (Sunday). HISTORICAL: Jewish custom required waiting until after the Sabbath rest to prepare a body with spices; the women's early arrival underscores their devotion and urgency.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: And they found the stone rolled away from the tomb.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural feminine

verses.flow: And having entered, they did not find the body of the Lord Jesus.

words.m: verb present active participle dative singular feminine

verses.flow: And it came to pass, while they were perplexed concerning this, behold, two men stood by them in dazzling garments.
verses.note: GREEK: "ἐσθῆτι ἀστραπτούσῃ" (esthēti astraptousē) — "lightning-flashing garments." The participle ἀστράπτω (Strong's G797) denotes the brilliance of lightning. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Angelic beings appearing in radiant garments is consistent with divine council messenger imagery (cf. Dan 10:5-6; Acts 1:10). Two witnesses establish testimony under Torah law (Deut 19:15).

words.m: verb aorist middle participle genitive plural feminine
verb present active participle genitive plural feminine
verb present active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: And as they became fearful and bowed their faces to the ground, they said to them, "Why do you seek the living among the dead?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Why do you seek the living among the dead?" — This rhetorical question from the angels contains a profound christological declaration: Jesus belongs to the category of "the living" (τὸν ζῶντα), not "the dead" (τῶν νεκρῶν). This echoes divine self-identification language (cf. Josh 3:10; Ps 42:2; Dan 6:26).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: He is not here, but has been raised. Remember how he spoke to you while he was still in Galilee,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: saying that the Son of Man must be delivered into the hands of sinful men, and be crucified, and on the third day rise again."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "δεῖ παραδοθῆναι" (dei paradothēnai, "it is necessary to be delivered") expresses divine necessity—the cross was not accidental but ordained in the counsel of God. TYPOLOGY: The three-fold pattern—delivered, crucified, risen on the third day—becomes the kerygmatic core of apostolic preaching (cf. 1 Cor 15:3-4).

verses.flow: And they remembered his words.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural feminine

verses.flow: And having returned from the tomb, they reported all these things to the eleven and to all the rest.
Now they were Mary Magdalene, and Joanna, and Mary the mother of James; and the rest of the women with them were telling these things to the apostles.
And these words appeared before them as idle talk, and they were disbelieving them.
verses.note: GREEK: "λῆρος" (lēros, Strong's G3026) is a NT hapax legomenon meaning "idle talk, nonsense, delirium." It was used in medical literature for the babbling of the fevered. The disciples' disbelief of the women's testimony heightens the apologetic force of the resurrection narrative—they were not credulous.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb perfect active participle accusative singular neuter

verses.flow: But Peter, having risen up, ran to the tomb; and having stooped down, he sees the linen cloths alone; and he departed to his own home, marveling at what had happened.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: And behold, two of them on that same day were journeying to a village being sixty stadia distant from Jerusalem, whose name was Emmaus.

words.m: verb perfect active participle genitive plural neuter

verses.flow: And they were conversing with one another concerning all these things that had taken place.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And it came to pass, while they were conversing and discussing, Jesus himself also, having drawn near, was journeying with them.
But their eyes were being held so as not to recognize him.
verses.note: GREEK: "ἐκρατοῦντο" (ekratounto) is a divine passive—their eyes "were being held" (by God). THEOLOGICAL: The supernatural restraint on recognition indicates that the risen Christ's identity is disclosed by divine initiative, not by human perception alone. This parallels the theme of spiritual blindness and divine revelation throughout Luke-Acts.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And he said to them, "What are these words that you are exchanging with one another while walking?" And they stood still, looking sad.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist middle participle accusative plural neuter

verses.flow: And the one named Cleopas, answering, said to him, "Are you the only one sojourning in Jerusalem who does not know the things that have come to pass in it in these days?"
And he said to them, "What things?" And they said to him, "The things concerning Jesus the Nazarene, who was a man, a prophet, mighty in deed and word before God and all the people,
and how the chief priests and our rulers delivered him up to a sentence of death, and crucified him.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But we were hoping that he is the one about to redeem Israel. But indeed, besides all this, it is now the third day since these things came to pass.
verses.note: GREEK: "λυτροῦσθαι τὸν Ἰσραήλ" (lytrousthai ton Israēl, "to redeem Israel") uses the middle voice of λυτρόω (Strong's G3084), reflecting OT redemption language (cf. Ps 130:8 LXX; Isa 41:14). THEOLOGICAL: Their hope was not wrong in substance—Jesus is indeed the Redeemer—but was incomplete in understanding the mode: redemption through suffering before glory.

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative plural feminine

verses.flow: Moreover, certain women from among us astonished us, having been at the tomb early in the morning,

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural feminine
verb present active participle nominative plural feminine

verses.flow: and not having found his body, they came saying that they had also seen a vision of angels, who say that he is alive.
And some of those with us went to the tomb and found it so, just as the women also had said; but him they did not see."
And he said to them, "O foolish ones, and slow of heart to believe upon all that the prophets have spoken!
verses.note: GREEK: "ἀνόητοι" (anoētoi, "without understanding/foolish") and "βραδεῖς τῇ καρδίᾳ" (bradeis tē kardia, "slow in heart") — the heart in Hebraic thought is the seat of understanding, not merely emotion. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus rebukes not intellectual incapacity but a failure of the heart to trust the prophetic witness. Faith must encompass the whole prophetic testimony, including the suffering of the Messiah.

verses.flow: Was it not necessary for the Christ to suffer these things and to enter into his glory?"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "οὐχὶ ταῦτα ἔδει παθεῖν τὸν χριστόν" — "Was it not necessary for the Christ to suffer?" The divine "δεῖ" (dei, divine necessity) governs both the suffering (παθεῖν) and the entrance into glory (εἰσελθεῖν εἰς τὴν δόξαν). TYPOLOGY: This establishes the suffering-then-glory pattern as the definitive hermeneutical key for reading the OT messianic witness (cf. 1 Pet 1:10-11; Isa 53; Ps 22).

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having begun from Moses and from all the prophets, he interpreted to them in all the Scriptures the things concerning himself.
verses.note: GREEK: "διερμήνευσεν" (diermēneusen, Strong's G1329) means "to interpret thoroughly, to translate/expound." THEOLOGICAL: This is the paradigmatic christological reading of Scripture: beginning from Moses (Torah) through all the prophets, Jesus demonstrates that the entire OT testifies concerning himself. This verse is foundational for Christian hermeneutics—Christ is the interpretive center of all Scripture.

verses.flow: And they drew near to the village where they were going, and he made as though to journey farther.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And they constrained him, saying, "Remain with us, for it is toward evening and the day has already declined." And he entered in to remain with them.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And it came to pass, as he reclined at table with them, having taken the bread, he blessed it, and having broken it, he was giving it to them.
And their eyes were opened, and they recognized him; and he became invisible from them.
verses.note: GREEK: "ἄφαντος" (aphantos, Strong's G855) is a NT hapax legomenon meaning "invisible, vanished from sight." THEOLOGICAL: Recognition comes precisely at the breaking of bread (τῇ κλάσει τοῦ ἄρτου), echoing the Last Supper (Luke 22:19). The risen Christ is known not merely by sight but through the sacramental act he instituted. His vanishing underscores that his post-resurrection presence operates in a new mode.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: And they said to one another, "Was not our heart burning within us as he was speaking to us on the road, as he was opening to us the Scriptures?"

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine
verb perfect passive participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: And having risen up that same hour, they returned to Jerusalem, and found the eleven gathered together, and those with them,

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: saying, "The Lord has truly been raised, and has appeared to Simon!"
verses.note: GREEK: "ὄντως ἠγέρθη ὁ κύριος" — "Truly the Lord has been raised." The adverb ὄντως (ontōs, "in reality, truly") emphasizes the factual nature of the resurrection. THEOLOGICAL: The title "ὁ κύριος" (ho kyrios, "the Lord") applied to the risen Jesus carries its fullest theological weight here, identifying the risen Christ with the divine Lord of Israel (cf. Ps 110:1; Phil 2:11).

verses.flow: And they were recounting the things on the road, and how he was made known to them in the breaking of the bread.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: And as they were speaking these things, he himself stood in the midst of them.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And being terrified and becoming fearful, they were supposing they beheld a spirit.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And he said to them, "Why are you troubled, and why do doubts arise in your hearts?

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: See my hands and my feet, that it is I myself. Handle me and see, for a spirit does not have flesh and bones, as you behold me having."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus' emphatic "ἐγώ εἰμι αὐτός" (egō eimi autos, "I am he myself") combined with the invitation to touch his flesh and bones establishes bodily resurrection against any docetic or purely spiritual interpretation. The resurrection body is continuous with the crucified body—bearing the marks of the nails—yet transformed beyond ordinary physicality.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having said this, he showed them his hands and his feet.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine
verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: And while they were still disbelieving from joy and marveling, he said to them, "Have you anything edible here?"
And they gave him a piece of broiled fish.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having taken it, he ate before them.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb perfect passive participle accusative plural neuter

verses.flow: And he said to them, "These are my words which I spoke to you while I was still with you, that all things written in the Law of Moses and the Prophets and the Psalms concerning me must be fulfilled."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus identifies three divisions of the Hebrew Scriptures—the Law of Moses, the Prophets, and the Psalms (representing the Writings/Ketuvim)—as testifying about himself. This is the only NT text explicitly naming all three divisions of the Tanakh. STRUCTURE: This verse forms the theological climax of the Emmaus narrative expanded: all Scripture finds its fulfillment ("δεῖ πληρωθῆναι," must be fulfilled) in the person and work of Christ.

verses.flow: Then he opened their mind to understand the Scriptures.
verses.note: GREEK: "διήνοιξεν αὐτῶν τὸν νοῦν" — "he opened their mind." The verb διανοίγω (dianoigō, Strong's G1272) is the same used for opening the womb (Luke 2:23), opening eyes (v.31), and opening Scriptures (v.32). THEOLOGICAL: Understanding Scripture requires divine illumination; the noetic effect of sin prevents natural comprehension of the christological content of the OT. Only the risen Lord can grant this spiritual sight.

verses.flow: And he said to them, "Thus it has been written, that the Christ should suffer and rise from the dead on the third day,

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: and that repentance and forgiveness of sins should be proclaimed in his name to all the nations—beginning from Jerusalem.
verses.note: GREEK: "μετάνοιαν καὶ ἄφεσιν ἁμαρτιῶν" (metanoian kai aphesin hamartiōn) — "repentance and forgiveness of sins." THEOLOGICAL: The content of the proclamation flows directly from the suffering and resurrection of the Christ (v.46). "Εἰς πάντα τὰ ἔθνη" (eis panta ta ethnē, "to all the nations") fulfills the Abrahamic promise (Gen 12:3) and Isaianic vision (Isa 49:6). The phrase "ἀρξάμενοι ἀπὸ Ἰερουσαλήμ" ("beginning from Jerusalem") establishes the geographic-theological program fulfilled in Acts.

verses.flow: You are witnesses of these things.
And behold, I am sending the promise of my Father upon you. But you, remain in the city until you are clothed with power from on high."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "τὴν ἐπαγγελίαν τοῦ πατρός μου" — "the promise of my Father." Jesus identifies the coming Holy Spirit as the Father's promise, linking to Joel 2:28-32 and the Pentecost fulfillment in Acts 2. "Δύναμιν ἐξ ὕψους" (dynamin ex hypsous, "power from on high") — ὕψος connotes the heavenly realm, the divine throne (cf. Isa 32:15; Luke 1:35). STRUCTURE: This verse bridges Luke's Gospel to Acts, creating a literary and theological hinge for the two-volume work.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And he led them out as far as Bethany, and having lifted up his hands, he blessed them.
And it came to pass, while he was blessing them, he parted from them and was carried up into heaven.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "ἀνεφέρετο εἰς τὸν οὐρανόν" — "he was carried up into heaven." The imperfect passive ἀνεφέρετο depicts an ongoing, visible ascension. TYPOLOGY: The ascension fulfills the enthronement pattern of Psalm 110:1 and Daniel 7:13-14, where the Son of Man approaches the Ancient of Days and receives dominion. Jesus' departure while blessing (εὐλογεῖν) echoes the high-priestly blessing (Num 6:22-27; Sir 50:20-21), casting him as the true and final High Priest.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And they, having worshiped him, returned to Jerusalem with great joy.
verses.note: GREEK: "προσκυνήσαντες αὐτόν" (proskynēsantes auton) — "having worshiped him." The verb προσκυνέω (proskyneō, Strong's G4352) denotes full religious worship, the homage due to God alone. THEOLOGICAL: The disciples' worship of the ascended Jesus constitutes a profound christological confession—they recognize in the risen and ascended Christ one worthy of the worship belonging to the God of Israel. Their "great joy" contrasts with the sorrow of the passion and confirms the resurrection's transformative power.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And they were continually in the temple, blessing God.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: Luke's Gospel ends where it began—in the temple (cf. Luke 1:8-10). The inclusion forms a literary frame: from Zechariah's priestly service to the disciples' continuous worship. THEOLOGICAL: "εὐλογοῦντες τὸν θεόν" — "blessing God." The proper response to the resurrection, ascension, and promised Spirit is unceasing doxology. The open ending anticipates Acts, where the Spirit descends and the mission to all nations begins.
